docs.title: Wiki Index — 3dSpace Technical Knowledge Base
docs.text: Content catalog. Every wiki page listed under its type with a one-line summary. Last updated: 2026-05-25 | Total pages: 84

docs.title: Core Game Managers
docs.text:
• Galaxy Generator — Generates star systems, planets, and celestial bodies for the galaxy map.
  • Galaxy Navigator (GN) — Zone-based navigation with route shard indexing for fast pathfinding across sectors.
  • Gate Manager — Manages jump gate activation, cooldowns, and destination routing.
  • Jump Manager (JM) — Handles jump travel state machine and transition effects.
  • Level Manager — Loads/unloads levels, manages scene transitions between systems and stations.
  • Spawn Manager — Spawns NPCs, ships, and world objects with lifecycle management.

docs.title: Framework Services
docs.text:
• FW Config Service — INI config persistence with typed getters and defaults fallback chain.
  • FW Scene Router — Scene transitions with 10 shader transition presets (wipes, fades, rotation).
  • FW State Machine — Explicit state machine with on_enter/on_exit/on_update/on_event lifecycle.
  • FW Trackable Registry — Registry for tracking and managing FW framework entities.

docs.title: Multiplayer Replication
docs.text:
• Flight Controller (ShipController) — Player ship movement, physics model (SharedFlightModel), boost/travel/highway systems, client prediction, multiplayer sync.
  • Remote Ship Controller — Client-side visual proxy for remote ships with SnapshotBuffer interpolation.
  • Shared Flight Model — Deterministic ship physics shared between server and clients.
  • Snapshot Buffer — Interpolation buffer: quaternion slerp + linear lerp for smooth ship state rendering.
  • Ship Visual Proxy — Client-side visual representation of remote ships with VFX integration.
  • Ship Replication Service (SRS) — Server-authoritative ship state replication to clients at 30Hz.
  • Avatar Replication Service (AVRS) — Avatar state replication for player character on foot/inside stations.
  • Multiplayer Session (MPS) — Manages multiplayer session lifecycle, connection handling, and matchmaking.

docs.title: World State & Services
docs.text:
• Server World State Service (SWS) — Authoritative server-side world state management for all entities.
  • Ship Config Service (SCS) — Ship configuration persistence: loadouts, parts, and customization data.
  • Player Identity Manager (PIM) — Manages player identity across sessions with faction reputation and progression.
  • Player State Manager (PSM) — Tracks mutable player state: credits, cargo, discovery progress, inventory.

docs.title: Economy & Discovery
docs.text:
• Credits Manager (CM) — Credits singleton: balance tracking, transactions, and economy integration.
  • Resource Ledger Manager (RLM) — Resource ledger: tracks commodity holdings, market prices, and trade history.
  • Trade Service (TS) — Trade engine with 21 commodities, 7-factor price computation, station markets, and jobs system.
  • Discovery Manager (DM) — Discovery bubble system (75 LY auto-scan, 500 LY pulse), 7 discovery types × 5 scan levels, probe management.

docs.title: Galaxy Management
docs.text:
• Galaxy Save Manager (GSM) — Sectorized save/load v2 for galaxy state with async worker thread and schema migration.
  • Galaxy System Registry (GSR) — Registry of all generated systems with lookup, caching, and streaming support.

docs.title: NPC Systems
docs.text:
• Faction Manager — Faction reputation/standing system: tracks player relations across factions.
  • Patrol Manager — NPC patrol wing management: Vee formation, patrol routes, and wing coordination.
  • NPC Simulation Service — Lightweight vs full runtime NPC lifecycle simulation tiers.
  • NPC Promotion Service — Promotes/demotes NPCs based on proximity, hails, and combat performance.
  • NPC Replication Orchestrator — Server-authoritative NPC state replication to clients.
  • NPC World Orchestrator — Coordinates NPC spawning, patrol assignment, and world integration.

docs.title: World Runtime & Generation
docs.text:
• World Runtime Manager — Manages runtime world state: active systems, streaming zones, and cleanup.
  • Procedural Level Builder — Procedural generation of stations, megastructures, surface features from recipe data.
  • Star Generator — Generates stars with spectral types (OBAFGKM + exotic), visual families, and habitable zones.
  • Station Docking Manager — Station scan/prompt/menu flow: refuel, repair, restock, service costs.
  • System Event Runtime — Runtime event system for in-game triggers, notifications, and state changes.

docs.title: Network & Replication Services
docs.text:
• Network Ship Manager — Server-side snapshot broadcast at 30Hz with distance-based delivery policy (near/medium/far tick divisors).
  • Network Snapshot Generator — Compiles ship state data for network replication: position, velocity, weapon states, packed binary format.
  • Prediction Service — Client-side prediction and interpolation for smooth ship movement between server snapshots.
  • Player Possession Service — Manages transitions between ship control (flight mode) and avatar (on-foot/inside stations).

docs.title: Galaxy & Map Services
docs.text:
• Galaxy Cache Service — Caching layer for galaxy map data: sector summaries, route information, hierarchy queries.
  • Local System Map Manager — Manages the local system map UI and navigation within a single star system.

docs.title: Combat & Targeting
docs.text:
• Targeting Manager — Combat targeting: target groups, cycling through available targets, lightweight NPC handling.
  • Combat Targeting Solver — Intercept point quadratic solver for combat targeting calculations.

docs.title: Chat & Communication
docs.text: • Chat Service — Networked chat system for multiplayer communication between players and NPCs.

docs.title: Framework UI Services
docs.text:
• FW Input Rebind Service — Runtime input action rebinding: keyboard/mouse and controller customization.
  • FW Platform Detection — Detects runtime platform (Steam, standalone, web) and enables platform-specific features.
  • FW Resource Cache — Preload queue and resource caching system for efficient asset loading with LRU eviction.
  • FW Debug Utilities — Structured debug logging with severity levels, category tagging, and optional profiling hooks.
  • FW Context Menu — Reusable context menu component: right-click menus with dynamic item generation.
  • FW Modal Overlay — Modal dialog system: fullscreen dialogs with focus trapping and backdrop dimming.

docs.title: Architecture & Systems
docs.text:
• Project Architecture Overview — Project overview, directory structure, patterns, and conventions.
  • AI Subsystem — NPC AI: behavior tree framework (Action/Condition/Sequence/Selector), 5 AI states, steering helpers, personality system.
  • Combat System — ShipWeapons controller, weapon runtime classes, TargetingManager, CombatTargetingSolver, heat/ammo systems.
  • Networking & Multiplayer — Authoritative server model, snapshot broadcast at 30Hz, distance-based delivery policy, dynamic delay adaptation.
  • Ship Systems — ShipData resource (814 lines), parts system (engines/shields/cargo/hull), loadout management, weapon groups, countermeasures.
  • FW Framework Addon — ConfigService, SceneRouter, StateMachine, AudioService, NetService, SaveService.
  • FW Framework Additions — New FW_ services: input rebinding, platform detection, resource cache, UI overlays.
  • UI & HUD Subsystems — HudOverlay CanvasLayer (1256+ lines), priority bootstrap system, 28 widget factories, service modules.
  • VFX & Visual Effects System — Thruster sockets, ShipVFX Manager, bloom effects, particle systems.

docs.title: World & Content
docs.text:
• World Generation Pipeline — Galaxy → Systems → Objects generation pipeline with sector streaming and zone graph routing.
  • Galaxy Sector Streaming — Sectorized data streaming + caching for efficient galaxy navigation.
  • Surface Features Generation — Procedural cities, landmarks, and outposts on planet surfaces.
  • Backdrop System — Planets, nebulae, landable surfaces, and sky rendering backdrops.
  • Sky Parameter System — Dynamic sky rendering parameters: lighting, atmosphere, weather effects.
  • Factions & Lore — Faction identities, governments, relations, and lore backgrounds.
  • Ships, Parts & Lore — Ship hulls, engines, shields, hardpoints, loadouts, and faction ship rosters.
  • Weapons & Countermeasures — Weapon types (laser/rocket/plasma/mining/probe/limpet), mechanics, and countermeasure systems.
  • NPCs & Patrols — NPC lifecycle simulation, patrol wings (Vee formation), six ship roles with faction job mixes.
  • Stations & Megastructures — StationData resource (11 types, 9 services), 13 megastructure kinds, procedural generation from recipe data.
  • Galaxy & Celestial Bodies — Star types (OBAFGKM + exotic), planet types with atmospheres, jump gates, asteroid belts, nebulae.

docs.title: Economy & Exploration
docs.text:
• Economy & Trade — TradeService engine: 21 commodities, 7-factor price computation, station markets, discovery value selling, jobs system.
  • Mining & Limpet Systems — Asteroid mining mechanics, limpet bay management, resource extraction workflows.
  • Discovery & Exploration — Discovery bubble system (75 LY auto-scan, 500 LY pulse), 7 discovery types × 5 scan levels, probe management, sectorized save/load v2.

docs.title: Multiplayer & Replication
docs.text:
• Galaxy Navigation Routing — Zone graph + route shard indexing for fast pathfinding across galaxy sectors.
  • NPC Replication & World Orchestration — Server-authoritative NPC replication: state sync, spawning, and world integration.
  • Event Replication System — Server-to-client event broadcasting for game events, notifications, and state changes.

docs.title: Player Systems
docs.text:
• Player Possession System — Ship ↔ avatar transitions: player control switching between flight and on-foot modes.
  • Ship Systems Coordinator — Unified ship state management: coordinates subsystems (shields, fuel, weapons, engines).

docs.title: Local Simulation
docs.text: • Local System Traffic — Intra-system NPC traffic simulation: patrol routes, civilian movement, and congestion modeling.

docs.title: Domain
docs.text: Technical documentation for the 3dSpace project: a Godot 4.6 multiplayer space simulation game built in GDScript with a custom FW_ framework addon. Covers architecture, subsystems, gameplay systems, world generation, networking, and development conventions.

docs.title: Conventions
docs.text:
• File names: lowercase, hyphens, no spaces (e.g., flight-controller.md)
  • Every wiki page starts with YAML frontmatter (see below)
  • Use [wikilinks](#wikilinks) to link between pages (minimum 2 outbound links per page)
  • When updating a page, always bump the updated date
  • Every new page must be added to index.md under the correct section
  • Every action must be appended to log.md
  • Provenance markers: On pages that synthesize 3+ sources, append ^[raw/articles/source-file.md] at the end of paragraphs whose claims come from a specific source.

docs.title: Frontmatter
docs.text:
---
title: Page Title
created: YYYY-MM-DD
updated: YYYY-MM-DD
type: entity | concept | comparison | query | summary
tags: [from taxonomy below]
sources: [raw/articles/source-name.md]
confidence: high | medium | low        # how well-supported the claims are
contested: true                        # set when the page has unresolved contradictions
---

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
• architecture — General architecture, patterns, conventions
  • autoload — Godot autoload/singletons (FwConfigService, FwSceneRouter, etc.)
  • framework — FW_ framework addon internals and extension points
  • scene-system — Scene loading, transitions, instantiation patterns
  • data-driven — Resource-based configuration, presets, data assets

docs.title: Subsystems
docs.text:
• flight — Flight controller, ship movement, physics
  • ai — NPC AI, behavior trees, patrol systems, flight controllers
  • combat — Weapons, countermeasures, damage models
  • world-gen — Galaxy generation, planet creation, nebulae, backdrops
  • networking — Multiplayer, RPCs, replication, client-server model
  • ui — HUD, menus, galaxy map, settings dialogs
  • vfx — Visual effects, particles, ship VFX, explosions
  • resources — Ship parts, faction data, station data, game resources

docs.title: Development
docs.text:
• debugging — Debug tools, diagnostic patterns, known pitfalls
  • performance — Optimization, profiling, bottlenecks
  • testing — Benchmarks, validation, test infrastructure
  • conventions — Code style, naming, project conventions

docs.title: Page Thresholds
docs.text:
• Create a page when an entity/concept is central to understanding the codebase (autoloads, major subsystems, framework components) or appears across multiple files
  • Add to existing page when new information about something already covered surfaces during work
  • DON'T create a page for passing mentions, minor utility functions, or one-off scripts
  • Split a page when it exceeds ~200 lines — break into sub-topics with cross-links
  • Archive a page when its content is fully superseded — move to _archive/, remove from index

docs.title: Entity Pages
docs.text:
One page per notable entity. Include: - Overview / what it is - Key files and line counts - Relationships to other entities (wikilinks) - Source references

Target entities: autoloads, FW_ framework classes, major subsystem controllers, key resources.

docs.title: Concept Pages
docs.text:
One page per concept or topic. Include: - Definition / explanation - How it works in the codebase (key files) - Open questions or debates - Related concepts (wikilinks)

Target concepts: game systems, architectural patterns, known issues, design decisions.

docs.title: Comparison Pages
docs.text: Side-by-side analyses. Include: - What is being compared and why - Dimensions of comparison (table format preferred) - Verdict or synthesis - Sources

docs.title: Update Policy
docs.text: When new information conflicts with existing content: 1. Check the dates — newer sources generally supersede older ones 2. If genuinely contradictory, note both positions with dates and sources 3. Mark the contradiction in frontmatter: contradictions: [page-name] 4. Flag for user review in the lint report

docs.title: Wiki Log
docs.text: Chronological record of all wiki actions. Append-only. Format: ## [YYYY-MM-DD] action | subject Actions: ingest, update, query, lint, create, archive, delete When this file exceeds 500 entries, rotate: rename to log-YYYY.md, start fresh.

docs.title: AI Subsystem — Behavior Trees, Flight Controllers & Steering
Overview
docs.text: The NPC AI system governs how enemy ships and patrol wings behave in the game world. It combines a behavior tree framework for decision-making with an AI flight controller that extends the player's FlightController to handle movement, combat maneuvers, and formation flying. The system is data-driven via EnemyData profiles that define personality traits (aggression, risk tolerance, accuracy variance).

docs.title: Behavior Tree System
docs.text: Located at /home/j/3d-space/scripts/ai/ai_behavior_tree.gd (151 lines), the BT framework implements a classic recursive behavior tree with three status codes: SUCCESS, FAILURE, and RUNNING.

docs.title: Node Types
docs.text: Node Type Behavior Action Leaf Executes a Callable, returns its result Condition Leaf Evaluates a predicate, returns SUCCESS/FAILURE Sequence Composite Ticks children left-to-right; fails on first FAILURE, runs on first RUNNING, succeeds when all succeed Selector Composite Ticks children left-to-right; succeeds on first SUCCESS, runs on first RUNNING, fails when all fail All Composite Ticks all children; fails on any FAILURE, returns RUNNING if any child is running, succeeds only when ALL succeed

docs.title: Tactical Condition Nodes
docs.text:
Specialized conditions for combat decision-making:

  • BTCheckHullThreshold — Returns SUCCESS if owner's hull < threshold (triggers retreat/evade)
  • BTCheckShieldThreshold — Returns SUCCESS if shields >= threshold (enables aggressive behavior)
  • BTCheckTakingFire — Returns SUCCESS when under attack (triggers evasion/flanking)
  • BTCheckDistanceRange — Returns SUCCESS if target distance within [min, max] range
  • BTCheckAngleBehind — Returns SUCCESS if target is behind the owner (> angle_threshold radians)

docs.title: Behavior Trees per State
docs.text: Each AI state has its own behavior tree: - _patrol_tree — Waypoint navigation, idle behaviors - _pursue_tree — Target acquisition, chase logic - _orbit_strafe_tree — Orbital combat with strafing patterns - _evade_tree — Evasion when under fire or low hull - _flank_tree — Flanking maneuvers to target rear arc - _retreat_tree — Retreat when critically damaged

docs.title: AI Flight Controller
docs.text: Located at /home/j/3d-space/scripts/ai/ai_flight_controller.gd (3,176 lines), this extends FlightController and adds NPC-specific behavior on top of the shared physics model.

docs.title: AI States
docs.text:
enum AIState { PATROL, PURSUE, EVADE, FLANK, RETREAT }

States are managed via an FW_StateMachine with explicit transitions between behaviors. State transitions have a minimum interval (2.0s) to prevent rapid oscillation.

docs.title: Patrol Behavior
docs.text:
• Follows waypoints defined in a PatrolPoints node child of the ship
  • If no patrol points exist, creates two default points (PointA/PointB) as fallback
  • Uses patrol_speed multiplier on base speed
  • Can be configured via behavior_type export string

docs.title: Pursuit Behavior
docs.text:
Two pursuit modes: 1. Chase — Direct pursuit of target with lead prediction 2. Orbit-strafe — Maintains orbit radius (~600m, ~50% weapon range) while strafing laterally

Key parameters: - pursue_speed_multiplier (1.15× base speed during chase) - orbit_radius (600m), orbit_tolerance (±100m for smooth transitions) - pursue_lost_timeout (3.5s before considering target lost) - min_separation_distance (18m to avoid collision with other ships)

docs.title: Target Selection & Scoring
docs.text:
Targets are scored using a weighted formula in AiCombatTuningHelpers.score_target(): 

score = (dist_score × dist_weight + angle_score × angle_weight + threat_score × threat_weight + random × random_weight) / total_weight

Weights are configurable per AI: - target_weight_distance (0.5 default — prefers closer targets) - target_weight_angle (0.25 — prefers targets in front arc) - target_weight_threat (0.2 — prioritizes last attacker) - target_weight_random (0.05 — adds unpredictability)

docs.title: Combat Maneuvers
docs.text:
Flanking: Arcs to target's rear arc using flank_min_distance_factor and flank_max_distance_factor of weapon range. Alternates left/right via _flank_arc_direction.

Evasion: Triggered when taking fire or hull below threshold. Lasts 2 seconds with 3-second cooldown. Tracks previous state to resume after evasion completes.

Retreat: Activated when hull is critically low. Flies away from target until shields/hull recover.

docs.title: Separation & Obstacle Avoidance
docs.text:
• Separation: Each AI maintains a separation_velocity vector that pushes it away from nearby ships (radius: 80m, strength: 16.0)
  • Obstacle avoidance: Ray-casts forward using obstacle_probe_distance (120m), applies lateral and vertical avoidance forces

docs.title: Boost Timing & Fire Cadence
docs.text:
Boost behavior: Random intervals between 4–8 seconds, lasting 2 seconds at 1.6× speed multiplier. Scheduled via _schedule_next_boost().

Fire cadence: Burst-fire pattern with randomized durations: - Burst duration: 0.6–1.4 seconds - Cooldown: 0.4–0.9 seconds - Grace period: 0.2s after solution is lost to allow continued firing

docs.title: Personality System (from EnemyData)
docs.text: Each AI can be assigned an EnemyData profile that sets personality traits in AIContext: - personality_aggression (0.0–1.0) — How aggressively the AI pursues targets - personality_risk_tolerance (0.0–1.0) — Willingness to engage at close range - personality_accuracy_variance (default 0.15) — Random variance in weapon aim

docs.title: Wing Integration
docs.text: AI ships can be members of patrol wings: - Connected to PatrolManager.wing_member_lost signal when assigned a wing_id - _wing_members array caches sibling wing nodes for formation awareness - Wing member loss triggers state re-evaluation

docs.title: Steering Behaviors
docs.text: Located at /home/j/3d-space/scripts/ai/ai_spatial_steering_helpers.gd (52 lines), provides static helper methods for combining multiple steering forces into a single desired velocity.

docs.title: Blended Velocity Computation
docs.text:
compute_blended_desired_velocity(
    tactical_dir, tactical_speed,      # Primary movement goal
    lateral,                           # Strafe component
    separation_enabled, separation_velocity,  # Collision avoidance with other ships
    obstacle_avoidance_enabled, obstacle_avoidance_velocity,  # Obstacle avoidance
    separation_max_speed, obstacle_avoidance_max_speed
) -> Vector3

The blending strategy: 1. Start with tactical direction × speed 2. Add lateral strafe component 3. Add separation force at 60% weight if enabled 4. Blend obstacle avoidance at 65% weight (replaces tactical direction if strong enough) 5. Cap total speed to the maximum of all individual caps

docs.title: Separation Velocity
docs.text:
compute_separation_velocity(count, accum, max_speed, strength) -> Vector3
Accumulates repulsion vectors from nearby ships within separation_radius, normalizes and scales by count × strength (capped at max_speed).

docs.title: Obstacle Avoidance Velocity
docs.text:
compute_avoidance_velocity(hit_count, away_accum, max_speed, strength) -> Vector3
Scales avoidance force linearly with hit count — more hits = stronger evasion response.

docs.title: Target Prediction
docs.text:
Located at /home/j/3d-space/scripts/ai/ai_target_velocity_helpers.gd (41 lines), provides duck-typed velocity extraction from target objects:

enum TargetVelocityMethod { NONE, GET_LINEAR_VELOCITY, GET_VELOCITY, GET_PROPERTY }

The system tries methods in order of preference: 1. get_linear_velocity() — Preferred for CharacterBody3D targets 2. get_velocity property — Fallback for custom velocity implementations 3. velocity property — Last resort

This allows the AI to predict target intercept points by extrapolating position using the extracted velocity over pursue_lead_time (0.35s default).

docs.title: Key Files Reference
docs.text: File Purpose Lines scripts/ai/ai_flight_controller.gd Main NPC flight controller — states, BT execution, combat 3,176 scripts/ai/ai_behavior_tree.gd Behavior tree framework (nodes, composites, tactical conditions) 151 scripts/ai/ai_context.gd AI state data — target tracking, personality, combat status 50 scripts/ai/ai_spatial_steering_helpers.gd Blended steering force computation (separation, avoidance) 52 scripts/ai/ai_target_velocity_helpers.gd Duck-typed velocity extraction for target prediction 41 scripts/ai/ai_combat_tuning_helpers.gd Target scoring, boost timing, fire cadence randomization 73

docs.title: Relationships
docs.text:
• Extends flight-controller — inherits all physics, boost, and travel mode behavior
  • Uses fw-state-machine for state transitions between PATROL/PURSUE/EVADE/FLANK/RETREAT
  • Consumed by npc-simulation-service for NPC actor lifecycle management
  • Data-driven via faction-data resources (EnemyData profiles define personalities)
  • Integrates with combat-system through weapon firing and targeting manager

docs.title: Backdrop System — Planets, Nebulae, Landable Surfaces & Sky
Overview
docs.text:
The backdrop system provides all non-interactive world scenery in 3dSpace: distant planets rendered as billboarded spheres, atmospheric effects, nebula clouds, landable planet surfaces with chunked LOD terrain, and reactive sky rendering. It operates independently from gameplay systems — its sole purpose is visual representation of the game world's celestial bodies and environmental atmosphere.

The system follows a preset-driven architecture where BackdropPreset resources define all visual parameters (billboards, environment, sun direction/color), and individual planet nodes apply their own shaders based on type. Landable planets use a separate chunked terrain system inspired by the cuberact quadtree sample.

docs.title: Backdrop — Main Controller
docs.text: Located at /home/j/3d-space/scripts/backdrop/backdrop.gd (779 lines), Backdrop is a Node3D that serves as the central coordinator for all backdrop rendering. It manages three subsystems: billboards (distant objects), environment (WorldEnvironment with sky shader), and sun (directional light + billboard sun).

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
Backdrop (Node3D)
    ├─ Billboards root → MeshInstance3D per preset entry (QuadMesh + unshaded material)
    ├─ WorldEnvironment → runtime-duplicated Environment resource with sky shader params
    ├─ DirectionalLight3D → follows sun_direction from preset
    ├─ BillboardSun (BillboardSunGenerator) → billboarded sun disc with glow
    └─ BackdropHeroController → procedural hero manifest for phenomena billboards

docs.title: Key Properties
docs.text: Property Type Default Description preset BackdropPreset null Visual preset defining all backdrop parameters billboard_distance float 30,000 Distance for billboard placement from camera lock_billboards_to_camera bool true Billboards follow camera position (always true) enable_billboard_sun bool true Billboarded sun disc with glow halo billboard_sun_size float 1,600 Sun disc visual radius (increased from 800 for larger stars) reactive_sky bool true Connect to SkyParameterService for dynamic sky updates

docs.title: Billboard System (_apply_billboards)
docs.text:
Creates billboard MeshInstance3D nodes from preset entries: 

for data in preset.billboards:
    mesh_instance = QuadMesh(billboard_data.size)
    material = StandardMaterial3D(
        shading_mode=UNSHADED,
        transparency=ALPHA,
        depth_draw=DISABLED,
        blend_mode=MIX,
        albedo_texture=_get_billboard_texture(data.texture),
        emission=data.color,
        billboard_mode=ENABLED if data.billboard else DISABLED
    )

Texture processing (_get_billboard_texture): Converts source textures to alpha-masked versions using luminance-based thresholding: alpha = pow(luminance, 1.7). Results are cached in _billboard_texture_cache per-source.

Camera tracking: Billboards root node follows camera position with a 1-unit move threshold to avoid unnecessary updates. Each billboard stores its direction as metadata and is positioned at direction × effective_distance from the camera.

docs.title: Environment & Sky Shader (_apply_environment, _apply_sky_shader_params)
docs.text:
Applies preset environment to WorldEnvironment: 

runtime_env = GRAPHICS_RUNTIME_RESOURCES.duplicate_environment_for_runtime(preset.environment)
world_env.environment = runtime_env

Sky shader parameters are set directly on the sky's ShaderMaterial: | Parameter | Source | Description | |-----------|--------|-------------| | sun_dir | preset.sun_direction | Sun direction vector | | sun_color | preset.sun_color | Sun color | | sun_intensity | preset.sun_energy | Sun brightness | | sun_disc_size | preset.sun_disc_size | Disc visual size | | sun_halo_size | preset.sun_halo_size | Halo radius | | sun_halo_intensity | preset.sun_halo_intensity | Halo brightness |

docs.title: Reactive Sky Integration (_connect_reactive_sky)
docs.text: When reactive_sky is enabled, the backdrop connects to SkyParameterService.parameters_changed signal. On each change: 1. _apply_reactive_sky_params(params) applies new sky parameters via SKY_SHADER_MATERIAL_APPLIER.apply_to_shader_material() 2. Debug logging records all shader parameters for troubleshooting

docs.title: Billboard Sun (_initialize_billboard_sun, set_billboard_sun_data)
docs.text:
Creates a BillboardSunGenerator instance that renders the sun as a billboarded disc: 

_billboard_sun.configure(star_data, sun_position)
_billboard_sun.set_billboard_active(true)

The sun is configured with spectral class data (color, size) and positioned at the star's world position. It uses billboard_sun_size (1,600 default) for disc radius and billboard_sun_glow_radius (400 default) for glow halo.

docs.title: Hero Controller (_ensure_hero_controller, apply_procedural_hero_manifest)
docs.text:
Manages procedural hero billboards (phenomena, stations, etc.) via a separate controller node: 

_hero_controller.apply_manifest(manifest)  # Apply procedural hero data
_hero_controller.clear_manifest()           # Clear all procedural billboards

docs.title: Debug System
docs.text: Extensive debug logging with configurable tags: - BACKDROP_DEBUG_TAG — General backdrop operations - DEBUG_MAP_BRIGHTNESS_TAG — Full environment parameter dump (exposure, tonemap, glow, fog, light energy, shadow bias) - SHADER_DEBUG_TAG — Sky shader parameter parity checks across scenes

docs.title: PlanetNode — Planet Rendering
docs.text: Located at /home/j/3d-space/scripts/backdrop/planet_node.gd (2,431 lines), PlanetNode is a Node3D that renders individual planets with type-specific shaders, clouds, atmosphere, rings, and moons. It's the primary visual representation of celestial bodies in the game world.

docs.title: Shader Types
docs.text:
const SHADER_TYPE_ROCKY: int = 0      # procedural_planet.gdshader
const SHADER_TYPE_GAS_GIANT: int = 1  # gas_giant.gdshader
const SHADER_TYPE_ICE_WORLD: int = 2  # ice_world.gdshader
const SHADER_TYPE_LAVA_WORLD: int = 3 # lava_world.gdshader
const SHADER_TYPE_OCEAN: int = 4      # procedural_planet.gdshader (ocean variant)
const SHADER_TYPE_DESERT: int = 5     # desert_world.gdshader
const SHADER_TYPE_TOXIC: int = 6      # toxic_world.gdshader
const SHADER_TYPE_BARE: int = 7       # procedural_planet.gdshader (bare rock)

docs.title: Visual Components
docs.text: Each planet node contains up to 5 visual layers: 1. Planet body — SphereMesh with type-specific shader material 2. Cloud layer — Slightly larger sphere with procedural_clouds.gdshader (rotates independently) 3. Atmosphere — Outer shell with atmosphere.gdshader (Fresnel-based rim lighting) 4. Rings — Torus-like mesh with planet_ring.gdshader (only for gas giants with rings) 5. Distant indicator — Billboarded sprite for planets outside render distance

docs.title: LOD System (lod_upgrade_distances, lod_downgrade_distances)
docs.text:
Three-tier LOD based on camera distance: | Tier | Upgrade Distance | Downgrade Distance | Effect | |------|-----------------|-------------------|--------| | 0 (full) | — | >12,000 units | Full detail with clouds, atmosphere, rings | | 1 (medium) | <5,000 | >6,000 | Reduced geometry, no atmosphere | | 2 (low) | <2,000 | — | Minimal sphere, no extras |

LOD is automatically managed based on camera distance. auto_upgrade enables seamless transitions between tiers.

docs.title: Moons & Satellites
docs.text:
Each planet can have moons and satellites orbiting it: - Moons: Small spheres orbiting the planet with configurable speed (MOON_ORBIT_SPEED_SCALE = 0.00025) - Satellites: Pre-built scenes (habitat, mining platform, defense, relay, derelict) orbiting at SATELLITE_ORBIT_SPEED_SCALE = 0.018

Orbit debug visuals can be enabled to show orbital paths and markers: - Moon markers: Blue spheres with radius 180 units - Satellite markers: Orange spheres with radius 70 units

docs.title: Landable Planet Integration
docs.text:
When a planet is marked as landable, the PlanetNode integrates with the landable surface system: 

LANDABLE_PLANET_ADAPTER_SCRIPT — bridges PlanetNode → LandablePlanetRuntime
PLANET_RUNTIME_BUILD_QUEUE_SCRIPT — manages async terrain build queue
PLANET_SURFACE_MANAGER_SCRIPT — surface generation management

The adapter handles coordinate space conversion between the planet's orbital position and the landable surface's local origin.

docs.title: Collision & Physics
docs.text:
• Collision body: StaticBody3D with SphereShape3D for planetary collision detection
  • Collision radius offset: Configurable via collision_radius_offset export property
  • Surface debug label: Optional Label3D showing planet name and type above the surface

docs.title: LandablePlanetRuntime — Chunked Terrain System
docs.text: Located at /home/j/3d-space/scripts/backdrop/landable/landable_planet_runtime.gd (858 lines), this Node3D implements a quadtree-based chunked terrain system for rendering landable planet surfaces. It's adapted from the cuberact LOD sample and uses a cube-face projection approach (6 root quads, one per face of a cube inscribed in the sphere).

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: Property Default Description radius 6,378 km Planet radius (Earth-like) terrain_height 16 km Peak terrain displacement above ideal sphere atmosphere_height 222 km Atmosphere extent above surface grid_size 16 Vertices per chunk edge (actual grid: 17×17) split_budget 8 Max chunk splits per frame lod_threshold_deg 10° Screen angle threshold for splitting max_lod_level 16 Deepest allowed quadtree level

docs.title: Quadtree Structure
docs.text:
Root (6 faces) → Quad (per face) → Chunk (grid_size×grid_size vertices)
                                              ↓ split_budget per frame
                                         Child Quads (4x children)
                                              ↓
                                         Leaf Chunks (rendered meshes)

Each quad represents one face of a cube inscribed in the planet sphere. When a chunk's screen angle exceeds lod_threshold_deg, it splits into 4 child quads (each with its own grid). This continues up to max_lod_level deep, creating fine detail near the camera and coarse geometry at distance.

docs.title: Incremental Build
docs.text:
Terrain generation is incremental — only split_budget chunks are split per frame, preventing frame drops during planet approach: 

func _process(delta):
    for i in range(split_budget):
        if pending_splits.is_empty(): break
        var quad = pending_splits.pop_front()
        quad.split()  # Creates 4 child quads with new grids

Child chunk builds are also budgeted: split_child_build_budget (default 1) chunks are built per frame for each split. This ensures smooth loading even at high LOD levels.

docs.title: Terrain Generation
docs.text: Each chunk's vertices are displaced based on procedural noise: 1. Base sphere: Ideal spherical surface at radius km 2. Terrain displacement: Noise-based elevation from 0 (ocean floor) to terrain_height (highest peak) 3. Ocean layer: Separate sphere at reduced radius for water coverage 4. Atmosphere: Outer shell with atmospheric scattering shader

docs.title: Shader Materials
docs.text:
• Terrain: landable_terrain.tres — per-pixel elevation coloring based on height bands
  • Ocean: landable_ocean.gdshader — animated wave displacement with Fresnel reflections
  • Atmosphere: Atmospheric scattering with sun-direction-dependent coloring

docs.title: Culling & Performance
docs.text:
• Frustum culling: Enabled by default, skips chunks outside camera view
  • Horizon culling: Skips chunks below the visible horizon
  • Custom AABB: All chunks share a single AABB covering the entire planet sphere + terrain height, disabling Godot's built-in frustum culling (which would be too coarse for chunked geometry)

docs.title: Collision Guard
docs.text:
collision_guard_enabled prevents the player from falling through the surface: 

if player_height < collision_guard_height_ratio * planet_radius:
    # Push player up to maintain minimum altitude above terrain

docs.title: PlanetSurfaceManager — Surface Generation Management
docs.text: Located at /home/j/3d-space/scripts/backdrop/planet_surface_manager.gd (519 lines), this @tool Node3D manages the generation of planet surface patches with async support. It's used for non-landable planets that still need surface detail for visual representation.

docs.title: Patch System
docs.text:
Surface is divided into latitude/longitude patches: 

_patches = {  # Dictionary keyed by (lat_index, lon_index)
    (0, 0): {"vertices": [...], "indices": [...], "material": StandardMaterial3D},
    ...
}

Each patch is a spherical segment with its own geometry and material. Patches are generated asynchronously when async_generation is enabled.

docs.title: Signals
docs.text: Signal Parameters Description generation_started — Async generation began generation_progress progress (0–1) Generation percentage generation_completed — All patches generated generation_failed error (String) Generation error message

docs.title: LOD Support
docs.text: When enable_lod is true, the manager adjusts patch resolution based on camera distance to a target node (lod_target_path). Updates occur at lod_update_interval seconds.

docs.title: BackdropNebulaData — Nebula Configuration
docs.text:
Located at /home/j/3d-space/scripts/backdrop/backdrop_nebula_data.gd, this Resource defines nebula visual data: | Property | Type | Description | |----------|------|-------------| | scene | PackedScene | Nebula scene to instantiate | | position | Vector3 | World position in the system | | rotation_degrees | Vector3 | Rotation Euler angles | | scale | Vector3 | Scale multiplier |

Nebulae are instantiated by the Backdrop's _apply_nebulae() method into a nebula root container. Each nebula scene typically contains volumetric fog, particle systems, and emissive meshes for gas cloud visualization.

docs.title: BackdropPhenomenonMapper — Phenomena Data Mapping
docs.text: Located at /home/j/3d-space/scripts/backdrop/backdrop_phenomenon_mapper.gd, this script maps procedural phenomenon data to visual billboard representations. It works with the BackdropHeroController to create dynamic billboards for: - Station clusters - Fleet formations - Anomaly markers - Navigation beacons

docs.title: PlanetSkybox — Skybox Rendering
docs.text:
Located at /home/j/3d-space/scripts/backdrop/planet_skybox.gd, this component handles skybox rendering for planetary surfaces. It manages: - Star field background (static star positions) - Sun/moon positioning in the sky dome - Atmospheric scattering effects near the horizon - Day/night cycle transitions

The skybox uses a custom shader that applies atmospheric color gradients based on sun direction and observer altitude.

docs.title: Backdrop → SystemGenerator
docs.text: When a system is generated, SystemGenerator provides star data (StarData) to the backdrop via set_billboard_sun_data(star_data, sun_position), which configures the billboard sun with the correct spectral class color and size.

docs.title: PlanetNode → ProceduralLevelBuilder
docs.text: The ProceduralLevelBuilder.build_planets() method creates PlanetNode instances for each planet in a system recipe, applying the appropriate shader type based on planet classification (rocky, gas giant, ice world, etc.).

docs.title: LandablePlanetRuntime → PlanetNode
docs.text: When a planet is marked as landable, the LandablePlanetAdapter bridges between PlanetNode's orbital position and LandablePlanetRuntime's local surface coordinate space. The adapter handles: - Position offset (planet center → surface origin) - Rotation alignment (orbital frame → surface frame) - Scale conversion (game units → km for terrain generation)

docs.title: Backdrop → SkyParameterService
docs.text: The reactive sky system connects to SkyParameterService for dynamic updates during gameplay. When the player travels between systems or time of day changes, new sky parameters are applied to the shader material in real-time.

docs.title: Key Files Reference
docs.text: File Purpose Lines scripts/backdrop/backdrop.gd Main backdrop controller: billboards, environment, sun, reactive sky 779 scripts/backdrop/planet_node.gd Planet rendering: LOD tiers, type-specific shaders, clouds, atmosphere, rings, moons 2,431 scripts/backdrop/landable/landable_planet_runtime.gd Chunked quadtree terrain for landable planets (cuberact-inspired) 858 scripts/backdrop/planet_surface_manager.gd Surface patch generation with async support and signals 519 scripts/backdrop/backdrop_nebula_data.gd Nebula configuration resource: scene, position, rotation, scale ~30 scripts/backdrop/backdrop_phenomenon_mapper.gd Maps procedural phenomena to billboard representations ~50 scripts/backdrop/planet_skybox.gd Skybox rendering: star field, sun/moon positioning, atmospheric effects ~100

docs.title: Relationships
docs.text:
• Consumed by world-generation pipeline via SystemGenerator planet data and star configuration
  • Provides visual representation for ship-systems planetary navigation targets
  • Integrated with ui-hud-subsystems planet info panel (display_name, target_type metadata)
  • Landable surfaces feed into discovery-exploration surface scanning mechanics

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The combat system handles all ship-to-ship and ship-to-object interactions in the game. It consists of three main layers: weapon firing (ShipWeapons + runtime classes), target acquisition (TargetingManager + CombatTargetingSolver), and damage application (via apply_damage on targetable entities). The system supports beam weapons, projectiles, homing missiles, probes, plasma charges, mining lasers, and limpets.

docs.title: ShipWeapons Controller
docs.text: Located at /home/j/3d-space/scripts/weapons/ship_weapons.gd (2,777 lines), ShipWeapons is the central weapons controller attached to every ship node. It manages firing state, heat management, ammo tracking, beam visuals, and delegates actual projectile/beam logic to runtime classes.

docs.title: Key Properties
docs.text: Property Type Default Description muzzle_path NodePath "WeaponMuzzle" Path to muzzle node for beam origin max_range float 1200.0 Maximum weapon range base_damage float 5.0 Base damage per tick ramp_time float 1.5 Time to reach full damage output damage_tick_rate float 0.2 Interval between damage ticks collision_mask int 0x7fffffff Physics collision mask for projectiles

docs.title: Firing Pipeline
docs.text:
set_firing(active, target) → _apply_group_fire_policy() → lock cone check → can_fire() check → _weapon_runtime.physics_process()
  1. Target resolution — _resolve_to_ship() walks parent chain to find ship-like ancestor (checks has_method("apply_damage"), "ship" group, or CharacterBody3D)
  2. Lock cone enforcement — Gimbaled weapons check if target is within lock_cone_dot; non-gimbaled weapons check muzzle orientation against target
  3. Heat/overheat gate — can_fire() returns false when overheated or ammo depleted
  4. Runtime dispatch — _weapon_runtime.physics_process(self, delta) handles per-frame weapon logic

docs.title: Group Fire Policy
docs.text:
When a weapon group has multiple weapons with FirePolicy.SEQUENTIAL, firing cycles through weapons: 

_active_group_weapon_index = (_active_group_weapon_index + 1) % _active_group_weapons.size()

docs.title: Weapon Runtime Classes
docs.text:
Each weapon type has a dedicated runtime class that handles its specific behavior:

Class File Behavior WeaponRuntimeLaser runtime/weapon_runtime_laser.gd Continuous beam, damage-on-tick, heat generation WeaponRuntimeRocket runtime/weapon_runtime_rocket.gd Projectile spawning with optional homing WeaponRuntimePlasma runtime/weapon_runtime_plasma.gd Chargeable plasma bolt, lead prediction WeaponRuntimeMining res://scripts/weapons/runtime/weapon_runtime_mining.gd Mining laser with resource collection WeaponRuntimeProbe res://scripts/weapons/runtime/weapon_runtime_probe.gd Probe deployment for scanning WeaponRuntimeLimpet res://scripts/weapons/runtime/weapon_runtime_limpet.gd Limpet drone deployment (60s lifetime)

Each runtime class implements: - physics_process(weapons, delta) — per-frame logic - on_fire_stop(weapons, was_firing) — cleanup on fire stop - on_fire_stop_immediate(weapons) — immediate abort - allows_fire_without_target() — whether targetless firing is allowed - should_enforce_lock_cone() — lock cone enforcement flag - should_check_muzzle_cone() — muzzle orientation check flag

docs.title: Weapon Data Resource
docs.text: Located at /home/j/3d-space/scripts/resources/weapon_data.gd (56 lines), WeaponData is a Godot Resource that defines all weapon parameters. It's assigned to ShipData which is then applied to ships at runtime.

docs.title: Weapon Types
docs.text:
• "laser" — Beam weapon, continuous damage
  • "rocket" — Projectile with optional homing
  • "plasma" — Chargeable projectile with lead prediction
  • "mining" — Mining laser for resource extraction
  • "probe" — Scanner probe deployment
  • "limpet" — Limpet drone (60s lifetime)

docs.title: Key Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Description weapon_type String Weapon category weapon_range float Maximum effective range damage float Base damage per tick ramp_time float Time to reach full damage gimbaled bool Can swivel independently of ship orientation gimbal_turn_rate float Degrees/second for gimbaled weapons lock_cone_dot float Cosine of max angle from muzzle forward (0.96 ≈ 16°) ammo_capacity / ammo_per_shot int Ammo system (0 = infinite) fire_interval float Minimum time between shots projectile_speed float Speed for projectile weapons homing_turn_rate float Turning rate for homing projectiles lock_time float Time required to achieve lock-on

docs.title: Heat System
docs.text:
heat_capacity: 100.0        # Total heat units before overheat
heat_rate: 25.0             # Heat generated per second while firing
heat_recharge: 20.0         # Heat dissipated per second when idle
heat_recharge_delay: 1.0    # Delay before recharge begins after stopping fire
overheat_cooldown: 2.0      # Enforced cooldown duration when overheated
overheat_resume_threshold_pct: 25.0  # Resume firing when heat drops below this %

docs.title: Plasma Charge System
docs.text: Plasma weapons support a charge mechanic: - charge_time_max — Maximum charge time (default 3s) - charged_damage_multiplier — Damage multiplier at full charge (×3.0) - charged_speed_multiplier — Projectile speed reduction when charged (×0.45, slower = more accurate) - charged_scale_multiplier — Visual scale increase when charged (×2.2)

docs.title: TargetingManager
docs.text: Located at /home/j/3d-space/scripts/systems/targeting_manager.gd (817 lines), this manages target acquisition for the player ship. It queries the FW_TrackableRegistry for nearby objects and maintains a sorted list of targets.

docs.title: Target Groups
docs.text: Group Type Purpose "nav/player_remote" enemy Remote-controlled player ships (NPCs) "nav/enemy" enemy Full AI enemy ships "nav/enemy_lightweight" enemy Lightweight NPC contacts "nav/poi" neutral Points of interest "nav/asteroid" neutral Asteroids (special handling) "nav/gate" neutral Space gates "nav/planet" / "nav/star" neutral Celestial bodies "nav/station" neutral Space stations "nav/nebula" neutral Nebula regions "nav/collectible" neutral Collectible items "nav/convoy_freighter" friendly Convoy freighters "nav/convoy_escort" friendly Convoy escorts

docs.title: Target Cycling
docs.text:
• select_next() / select_previous() — Cycle through all targets
  • select_next_enemy() — Skip to next enemy (or non-enemy if no enemies)
  • select_previous_non_enemy() — Cycle through friendly/non-hostile targets
  • Targets are sorted by distance from player ship

docs.title: Target Refresh Logic
docs.text: Targets are refreshed when: 1. Registry signals a change (_on_registry_changed) 2. Player ship moves beyond resort_distance_threshold (40m default) 3. Timer fires at update_interval (0.2s default) 4. Mouse click targeting is enabled and player clicks on screen

docs.title: Lightweight NPC Targeting
docs.text: Lightweight NPCs have reduced targeting ranges: - lightweight_contact_max_range: 4,000m (vs 8,000m for full enemies) - selected_lightweight_retention_range: 12,000m (keep selected target even if it moves out of range)

docs.title: CombatTargetingSolver
docs.text: Located at /home/j/3d-space/scripts/systems/combat_targeting_solver.gd (84 lines), this provides static utility methods for combat calculations.

docs.title: Intercept Point Calculation
docs.text:
compute_intercept_point(shooter_pos, target_pos, target_vel, projectile_speed) -> Vector3

Solves the quadratic equation for time-to-intercept: 

a = |target_vel|² - projectile_speed²
b = 2 × (target_pos - shooter_pos) · target_vel
c = |target_pos - shooter_pos|²
t = (-b ± √(b² - 4ac)) / 2a

Returns target_pos directly if: - Projectile speed ≤ 0.01 (beam weapons, instant hit) - No valid positive solution for t exists

docs.title: Weapon Origin & Forward
docs.text:
• get_weapon_origin() — Returns muzzle position if available, otherwise ship track position
  • get_weapon_forward() — Returns negative Z axis of muzzle or ship transform
  • get_display_distance_for_weapon() — Clamped to [120m, 2400m], defaults to 60% of weapon range

docs.title: TargetableEntity Protocol
docs.text:
All damageable entities implement the TargetableEntity protocol: 

func apply_damage(damage: float, source: Node3D = null) -> void
func get_track_position() -> Vector3
func get_velocity() -> Vector3
func get_display_name() -> String

The TargetingManager uses these methods to query target properties without tight coupling. Damage is applied through the apply_damage() method which is called by weapon runtime classes when a hit is confirmed (beam tracking or projectile collision).

docs.title: Beam vs Projectile Damage Models
docs.text:
Beam weapons (lasers): Continuous damage ticked at damage_tick_rate while firing and locked on target. Damage ramps up over ramp_time. Heat is generated continuously during fire.

Projectile weapons (rockets, plasma): Single-hit damage on collision. Homing projectiles use homing_turn_rate to steer toward target. Plasma bolts use lead prediction based on projectile speed.

docs.title: Key Files Reference
docs.text: File Purpose Lines scripts/weapons/ship_weapons.gd Central weapons controller — firing state, heat, ammo, beam visuals 2,777 scripts/resources/weapon_data.gd Weapon configuration resource (type, damage, range, heat) 56 scripts/systems/targeting_manager.gd Target acquisition, cycling, registry integration 817 scripts/systems/combat_targeting_solver.gd Intercept point calculation, weapon origin/forward helpers 84 scripts/weapons/runtime/weapon_runtime_laser.gd Laser beam damage model — scripts/weapons/runtime/weapon_runtime_rocket.gd Projectile/homing rocket model — scripts/weapons/runtime/weapon_runtime_plasma.gd Chargeable plasma bolt with lead prediction —

docs.title: Relationships
docs.text:
• Consumed by flight-controller for player weapon firing (via set_weapon_firing())
  • Consumed by ai-subsystem for NPC combat behavior (AiFlightController.set_fire_target() / set_firing())
  • Uses fw-trackable-registry for target discovery and proximity queries
  • Integrates with faction-manager through faction-based targeting rules (enemy vs friendly)
  • Damage application flows into npc-simulation-service for NPC actor state changes

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Discovery & Exploration system tracks all player discoveries across the galaxy — systems, planets, POIs, gates, stations, asteroid belts, and anomalies. It manages a discovery bubble (75 LY radius) that determines which nearby systems appear on the galaxy map as grayed-out markers, handles probe consumption for scanning, and enables players to sell unsold scan data to station brokers for credits and faction reputation. The system is an autoload singleton (DM) with full multiplayer RPC sync and sectorized save/load (schema v2).

docs.title: DiscoveryData Resource (scripts/resources/discovery_data.gd, 392 lines)
docs.text:
The core data structure for every tracked discovery. Each entry has a unique ID, type, scan level, and metadata dictionary.

Discovery Types: | Type | Value | Description | |------|-------|-------------| | SYSTEM | 0 | Entire star system discovered | | PLANET | 1 | Individual planet scanned | | POI | 2 | Point of Interest found | | GATE | 3 | Jump gate discovered | | ASTEROID_BELT | 4 | Asteroid field identified | | ANOMALY | 5 | Spatial/energy anomaly detected | | STATION | 6 | Station discovered |

Scan Levels (progressive): | Level | Value | Description | |-------|-------|-------------| | UNKNOWN | 0 | Not yet scanned | | DETECTED | 1 | Long-range scan detected (??? marker) | | BASIC | 2 | Basic scan — type and name revealed | | DETAILED | 3 | Detailed scan — all properties known | | SURFACE | 4 | Surface scan — full analysis (future expansion) |

Key fields: discovery_id, system_coords (Vector3i), entity_id, timestamp, discoverer_tag, first_discovery (bool), metadata (Dictionary). Sale tracking: last_sold_scan_level, sale_count, last_sale_timestamp, last_sale_station_id, last_sale_price.

Factory methods: create_system_discovery(), create_planet_discovery(), create_poi_discovery(), create_gate_discovery(), create_station_discovery(), create_asteroid_belt_discovery(), create_anomaly_discovery() — each populates type-specific metadata.

Serialization: to_dictionary() / from_dictionary() for save/load with full backward compatibility (v1 flat → v2 sectorized).

docs.title: DiscoveryValueProfileData Resource (scripts/resources/discovery_value_profile_data.gd)
docs.text: Configurable pricing profile for discovery broker payouts. Exported fields: - base_value_by_type: Dictionary mapping type keys to base credit values (system=220, planet=90, poi=70, gate=150, asteroid_belt=65, anomaly=180, station=110) - scan_level_multiplier: Multipliers per scan level (unknown=0.0, detected=0.4, basic=1.0, detailed=1.45, surface=1.95) - first_discovery_bonus_multiplier: 1.15x default bonus for first discoveries - min_entry_value: Floor of 5 credits per entry - bundle_rounding_step: Rounding granularity

docs.title: DiscoveryManager Autoload (scripts/systems/discovery_manager.gd, 2,179 lines)
docs.text: The central controller — an autoload singleton accessible globally as DM. Manages the discovery bubble, tracks all discoveries, handles probe resources, and synchronizes state across multiplayer peers.

docs.title: Core State
docs.text:
• _all_discoveries: Dictionary mapping discovery_id → DiscoveryData
  • _system_discoveries: Index by system coords (string key) → Array of DiscoveryData
  • _discoveries_by_sector: WS7 sectorized index for save/load ("sx,sy,sz" → Array[discovery_ids])
  • _visible_systems: Systems within bubble but not yet discovered
  • _asteroid_scan_progress: Per-system belt scan tracking (scanned count vs threshold)
  • _pending_notifications: Queue for discovery notifications when UI isn't connected

docs.title: Discovery Bubble System
docs.text:
The bubble defines what's visible on the galaxy map. Centered on the player's current system, with configurable range: - discovery_bubble_range / auto_scan_radius_ly: 75 LY (default) - pulse_scan_radius_ly: 500 LY (button-triggered extended scan) - visibility_fade_start: 50 LY — systems start fading at this distance - visibility_fade_end: 75 LY — fully transparent beyond this

get_bubble_opacity() returns 0.0–1.0 based on distance for map marker rendering. get_bubble_scale() provides size reduction for distant markers.

docs.title: System Discovery Flow
docs.text:
1. Player enters a system → _on_system_entered(coords) fires from GalaxyNavigator (autoload)
  2. If not discovered: register_system_discovery(coords) creates a SYSTEM discovery with DETAILED scan level
  3. Metadata auto-populated: system name, star type(s), binary status, faction info, economy/security/population profiles
  4. Side effects: removed from _visible_systems, synced to GalaxyNavigator, signals emitted

docs.title: Entity Discovery Flow
docs.text:
• Planets, POIs, gates, stations, asteroid belts discovered via scanning or proximity
  • register_entity_discovery() validates type/level, creates or updates entry
  • Asteroid belts require threshold-based scanning: each probe scan adds one asteroid to the count; once scanned_count >= threshold, the belt is marked discovered

docs.title: Multiplayer RPC Architecture
docs.text:
All discovery operations support client-server model with reliable gameplay channel:

Client → Server requests: - _request_register_system_discovery(coords, discoverer_tag) - _request_register_entity_discovery(discovery_dict, discoverer_tag) - _request_update_discovery_scan(discovery_id, new_level, metadata) - _request_register_asteroid_scan(coords, belt_id, asteroid_id, threshold, metadata, tag) - _request_discovery_refresh(active_system_only)

Server → Client responses: - _receive_discovery_sync(world_instance_id, discovery_dict) — single discovery push - _receive_asteroid_scan_progress_sync(world_instance_id, coords, belt_id, entry) - _receive_discovery_snapshot(payload) — full state replacement for new peers

World instance isolation ensures discoveries are scoped to the correct game world. Peers receive snapshots on peer_loaded_world signal.

docs.title: Probe System
docs.text:
• Default: 10 probes (max_probes)
  • Refueled at stations: probe_refuel_amount (2) or full refill if probe_refuel_full=true
  • Consumed via use_probe() → decrements count, emits probes_changed signal
  • Stored in PlayerStateManager (autoload) for persistence

docs.title: Save/Load (WS7 Sectorized — Schema v2)
docs.text: get_save_data() emits sectorized format: discoveries grouped by 8×8×8 system sectors, asteroid progress similarly grouped. load_save_data() accepts both v1 (flat array) and v2 (sectorized) for backward compatibility. Sector key formula: floor(coord / DISCOVERY_SECTOR_SIZE) where DISCOVERY_SECTOR_SIZE = 8.

docs.title: Signals
docs.text:
• system_discovered(coords, first_discovery, discoverer) — new system found
  • entity_discovered(discovery) — planet/POI/gate/station/belt/anomaly found
  • discovery_updated(discovery_id, new_scan_level) — scan level progressed
  • bubble_changed(center, radius) — bubble center moved
  • probes_changed(current, max) — probe count changed
  • player_tag_changed(new_tag) — player name updated
  • discovery_data_sold(discovery_ids, total_price, station_id) — bundle sold

docs.title: Discovery Value Selling (scripts/systems/trade_service.gd, lines 547–962)
docs.text: Discoveries are currency. Players sell unsold scan progression to stations with "Trade" or "Information" services (data broker).

docs.title: Pricing Formula
docs.text: For each discovery entry: 1. Base delta value = base_value × (current_scan_multiplier - sold_scan_multiplier) — only the new scan progress is paid for 2. First discovery bonus = delta × (first_bonus_multiplier - 1.0) — extra for first-time finds 3. Subtotal = base delta + first bonus 4. Market modifiers (7-factor multiplicative, same as cargo trading): - Market factor: clamp(0.72 + security*0.18 - volatility*0.22, 0.45, 1.05) - Standing modifier: Allied=1.1, Friendly=1.05, Neutral=1.0, Unfriendly=0.9, Hostile=0.75 - Station category factor (for "data" category) - Faction bias factor - Broker modifier (station-specific discovery broker quality) - Market event modifier

docs.title: Sale Flow
docs.text:
1. quote_sell_discovery_bundle(station, discoveries) — computes prices without committing
  2. Returns entries array with per-entry pricing, sale_map, blocked_entries (faction-restricted stations), total_price
  3. execute_sell_discovery_bundle(station, peer_id) — commits: marks discoveries sold, adds credits, applies faction reputation changes
  4. mark_discoveries_sold() on DM updates local state; mark_discoveries_sold_for_peer() handles multiplayer

docs.title: Faction Restrictions
docs.text: Some stations only accept discovery data from aligned factions. _can_sell_discovery_at_station() checks this and returns blocked entries with reasons.

docs.title: Discovery Journal UI (scripts/ui/discovery_journal.gd, 572 lines)
docs.text:
A popup window displaying all discoveries in a tabbed, sortable list with detail panel.

Tabs: All / Systems / Planets / POIs / Gates / Stations (6 tabs matching discovery types minus asteroid belts and anomalies).

Sort modes: Newest first, Oldest first, Name (A-Z), Type.

Stats bar: Total discoveries | First discoveries | Systems count | Unsold entries with estimated value.

Detail panel: Shows name, type, scan level, coordinates, discoverer, date, first discovery badge (gold ★), and metadata (faction info for systems, station services, POI details).

Sale status display: Per-entry shows "Unsold Progress Available" or "Sold", last sold scan level, sale count, last price, station, and date.

docs.title: Discovery Notification UI (scripts/ui/discovery_notification.gd, 625 lines)
docs.text:
Sliding notification panel for first discoveries and important finds. Shows in top-left corner with animated slide-in/out.

Display logic: Always shows system discoveries; other types show only if first_discovery=true or scan level is DETECTED. Queue max 5 items, auto-dismiss after 5 seconds (configurable).

Visual design: Type-specific icons drawn procedurally via pixel manipulation: - SYSTEM: filled circle (gold) - PLANET: smaller circle (light blue) - POI: diamond (purple) - GATE/ASTEROID_BELT: ring (cyan) - ANOMALY: star/cross (red) - STATION: square with cross (green, faction-colored for stations)

Text notifications: Also supports plain text via show_text_notification() for non-discovery events.

docs.title: Key Conventions
docs.text:
1. Discovery IDs are deterministic: {type}_{x}_{y}_{z}_{entity_id} — no UUIDs needed, fully reproducible from game state
  2. Scan levels only progress upward: update_from_scan() checks new_level > scan_level before advancing
  3. Sale tracking is per-discovery: Each entry independently tracks its sold-up-to level, enabling partial sales (e.g., sell BASIC now, DETAILED later)
  4. Sectorized persistence: All save data grouped by 8×8×8 system sectors for efficient partial loading
  5. World instance scoping: Multiplayer discoveries are isolated per world instance to prevent cross-world contamination

docs.title: Relationships
docs.text:
• Consumed by GalaxyNavigator (autoload) for auto-discovery on system entry and visible-system calculation — entity page planned
  • Consumed by economy-trade for discovery value selling, bundle pricing, and faction broker calculations
  • Consumed by ui-hud-subsystems for galaxy map rendering (bubble opacity/scale) and discovery journal popup
  • Produces events consumed by factions-lore via faction metadata auto-population on system discovery
  • Depends on PlayerStateManager (autoload) for probe count, player tag, and sale state persistence — entity page planned

docs.title: Economy & Trade — Commodities, Markets, Jobs System
Overview
docs.text: The economy system is a data-driven market simulation where players buy and sell commodities at stations, sell discovered exploration data for credits, accept jobs/missions from faction patrons, and manage cargo space. The core engine is TradeService (TS, 1900 lines), an autoload Node that handles all pricing logic, inventory management, reputation effects, and multiplayer RPC channels. Credits are managed by the separate CreditsManager (CM) singleton which wraps PlayerStateManager.

docs.title: Commodities
docs.text:
Commodities are defined by CommodityData resources (scripts/resources/commodity_data.gd, 22 lines) — a minimal resource with five export fields:

Field Type Description commodity_id String Unique identifier (e.g., "iron", "water_ice") display_name String Human-readable name category String Market category for filtering and station preferences base_price int Starting price in credits per unit volume int Cargo space consumed per unit (SCU) legal bool Whether the commodity is legal at all stations tags PackedStringArray Additional classification tags

docs.title: Default Commodity Catalog
docs.text:
TradeService ships with 21 default commodities loaded from .tres files under res://assets/economy/commodities/:

Raw Materials: iron, silicon, nickel, platinum, cobalt, copper, gold, magnesium Ice & Volatiles: water_ice, ice, ammonia, methane, carbon, organic_compounds, water Specialty: salvage, supplies, data, nav_data, exotics, limpet_drone

Each commodity is registered into an internal _commodity_catalog dictionary keyed by commodity_id. The catalog supports category queries via get_all_categories() which returns a sorted list including "all" as the first entry.

docs.title: Economy Profile Data
docs.text:
Market behavior is tuned by EconomyProfileData (scripts/resources/economy_profile_data.gd, 38 lines) which defines two groups of parameters:

Inventory Tuning: - Per-station-type base inventory counts (11 types): outpost=24, station=48, mega=96, military=64, research=36, mining=72, agricultural=52, industrial=80, habitat=44, prison=28, scientific=40 - Global multiplier (0.25–3.0×) applied to all inventory counts - Variance range (min/max) for random stock variation per commodity - Price modifier floor (0.6× minimum price) - Minimum stock threshold (default 3 units)

Market Events: - Demand spike chance (0–100%, default 20%) — increases sell prices, decreases buy prices - Oversupply chance (0–100%, default 20%) — decreases sell prices, increases buy prices - Event modifier ranges for sell and buy sides

docs.title: Station Category Profiles
docs.text:
Each station type has a built-in category preference profile that determines which commodity categories are accepted, preferred, discouraged, or restricted:

Station Type Preferred Discouraged Restricted Mining raw, salvage data — Industrial raw, trade_goods, consumable artifact — Research/Science data, artifact salvage — Military consumable, salvage artifact — Agricultural trade_goods, consumable artifact — Prison consumable artifact artifact Outpost consumable, trade_goods artifact —

These profiles can be overridden per-station via metadata keys: market_allowed_categories, market_preferred_categories, market_discouraged_categories, market_restricted_categories.

docs.title: Market Badges
docs.text:
The UI displays color-coded badges on each commodity based on the station's category profile and legality status. Colors are defined as constants in StationMarketPopup:

Badge Color Meaning Allowed default (light blue) Station accepts this category Preferred green (0.48, 1.0, 0.56) Better buy/sell rates at this station Discouraged orange (1.0, 0.72, 0.34) Weaker rates Restricted red (0.95, 0.38, 0.38) Cannot trade this category here Black Market orange (1.0, 0.5, 0.32) Contraband accepted at black market stations Illegal red Contraband blocked by security Low Stock — Station has ≤8 units remaining Demand Spike / Oversupply — Active market event label

docs.title: Price Computation
docs.text: Unit prices are computed as a product of seven multiplicative factors applied to the commodity's base_price:

docs.title: Sell Price (player sells to station)
docs.text:
sell_unit_price = base_price × market_factor × standing_factor × station_category_factor × faction_bias_factor × faction_policy_factor × event_factor × legality_factor
Factor Formula Range Effect Market clamp(0.72 + security×0.18 - volatility×0.22, 0.45, 1.05) 0.45–1.05 High security → better sell prices; high volatility → worse Standing Reputation-based multiplier (see below) 0.75–1.3 Allied = 1.1× (better), Hostile = 0.75× (worse) Station Category Preferred = 1.12×, Discouraged = 0.88× 0.88–1.12 Station preference for commodity category Faction Bias Per-faction type-specific modifier varies Faction values certain discovery types more highly Faction Policy Per-faction buy/sell policy multiplier 0.94–1.12 Federation buys at 0.97×, Pirates buy at 1.12× Event Demand spike = 1.18× sell, Oversupply = 0.86× sell varies Temporary market conditions Legality Black market stations: +8% for contraband; high security: blocked 0.82–1.34 Contraband handling based on station security

docs.title: Buy Price (player buys from station)
docs.text:
buy_unit_price = base_price × market_factor × standing_factor × station_category_factor × faction_bias_factor × faction_policy_factor × event_factor × legality_factor
Factor Formula Range Effect Market clamp(1.05 + volatility×0.35 + (1-security)×0.2, 0.9, 2.0) 0.9–2.0 High volatility → higher buy prices; low security → higher Standing Allied = 0.85×, Hostile = 1.3× 0.85–1.3 Allied = cheaper to buy, Hostile = expensive Station Category Preferred = 0.9× (cheaper), Discouraged = 1.12× 0.9–1.12 Inverse of sell factor — preferred goods are cheaper to buy

docs.title: Standing Modifiers
docs.text:
Based on PlayerReputationClass.Standing enum:

Standing Sell Modifier Buy Modifier Allied 1.10× (better) 0.85× (cheaper) Friendly 1.05× 0.93× Neutral 1.00× 1.00× Unfriendly 0.90× 1.15× Hostile 0.75× (worse) 1.30× (expensive)

docs.title: Faction Trade Policies
docs.text:
Each faction has a global buy/sell policy that applies to all commodities:

Faction Buy Modifier Sell Modifier Federation 0.97× 1.01× Empire 1.04× 0.98× Merchants 0.94× (best buyer) 1.04× Collective 0.99× 1.05× Pirates 1.12× (highest seller price) 1.06× Void Church 1.00× 1.03× Axiom 1.03× 1.05×

docs.title: Illegal Trade & Black Markets
docs.text:
Contraband handling depends on station security level and whether the station has a "Black Market" service:

  • Security ≥ 0.65 without black market: illegal commodities are completely blocked
  • Security < 0.45: slight discount on contraband (0.95× sell, 1.18× buy) — low-security stations tolerate some smuggling
  • Black Market stations: +8% premium for selling contraband, +20% markup for buying
  • Medium security (0.45–0.65): 0.82× sell / 1.34× buy penalties

docs.title: Station Services
docs.text: Beyond commodity trading, TradeService handles station services (refuel, repair, restock) via request_station_service(). Service costs are passed as parameters and deducted from player credits through the CreditsManager. In multiplayer, service requests are routed to the authoritative server via RPC channel 1 (reliable).

docs.title: Discovery Value & Data Broker Sales
docs.text: Players can sell discovered exploration data at stations with a "Data Broker" or "Information" service. This is handled by quote_sell_discovery_bundle() and execute_sell_discovery_bundle().

docs.title: Discovery Types & Values
docs.text:
Discoveries are classified into 7 types, each with base values defined in the discovery value profile:

Type Key Examples System "system" Star systems, nebulae Planet "planet" Planets, moons POI "poi" Points of interest Gate "gate" Jump gates Asteroid Belt "asteroid_belt" Belt clusters Anomaly "anomaly" Strange phenomena Station "station" Stations, megastructures

docs.title: Scan Levels & Multipliers
docs.text:
Each discovery has a scan level that determines how much data is available for sale:

Level Key Description Unknown "unknown" Detected but unscanned Detected "detected" Basic detection data Basic "basic" Initial scan results Detailed "detailed" Comprehensive analysis Surface "surface" Full surface mapping

The sale price is based on the delta between the current scan level and the last sold scan level — players can sell incremental scan upgrades at progressively higher values. A first-discovery bonus (default 1.15×) applies to never-before-sold discoveries.

docs.title: Faction Discovery Broker Bias
docs.text:
Each faction has type-specific value multipliers for discovery sales, reflecting their research priorities:

Faction System Planet POI Gate Anomaly Asteroid Belt Station Federation 1.08× — — 1.08× — — 1.06× Empire 1.05× — — 1.08× — — 1.08× Merchants 1.08× — — 1.12× — — 1.10× Collective — 1.04× — — — 1.16× (belt specialists) 1.05× Pirates — — 1.10× 1.05× — — 1.04× Void Church — — 1.12× — 1.18× (anomaly experts) — — Axiom 1.08× — 1.16× 1.10× 1.22× (highest anomaly value) — —

docs.title: Discovery Sale Restrictions
docs.text: Some discoveries can only be sold at stations controlled by specific factions. If a player attempts to sell at an incompatible station, the sale is blocked with a faction-specific error message listing which stations would accept each discovery entry.

docs.title: Jobs System
docs.text: Jobs (missions/contracts) are displayed through JobBoardPresenter (scripts/ui/job_board_presenter.gd, 374 lines), a static utility class that converts event data into structured job entries for the UI.

docs.title: Job Types
docs.text: Type Source Description Convoy Escort Merchant convoy events Protect freighters from pirates along a route Pirate Raid Station offers / convoy events Attack enemy convoys (player plays as pirate side)

docs.title: Job Entry Structure
docs.text:
Each job entry contains:

  • Identity: id, status (active/available), type, title
  • Factions: issuer_faction_id/name, opposition_faction_id/name, player_side (escort/pirates)
  • Progression: stage (staged/active/completed/failed), progress_ratio (0–1), progress_text
  • Location: origin_label, destination_label
  • Objectives: objective_text (convoy-specific: delivered/remaining/lost freighters, cargo pods recovered, pirate kills)
  • Rewards: reward_credits (total pool), projected_share (player's portion of payout_breakdown)
  • Visuals: accent_color (faction-colored), priority, tracked/trackable flags

docs.title: Station Job Offers
docs.text: Stations can generate job offers including: - Convoy contracts — accept to escort merchant freighters between stations - Pirate raids — accept to attack enemy convoys (available at pirate-controlled or low-security stations)

docs.title: Credits Management
docs.text:
Credits are managed by CreditsManager (scripts/systems/credits_manager.gd, 99 lines), an autoload Node that wraps PlayerStateManager:

Method Description get_credits() Returns current credit balance set_credits(amount) Sets credits (clamped to ≥0) add_credits(amount) Adds credits, emits credits_changed signal spend_credits(amount) Deducts credits if sufficient, returns bool can_afford(amount) Checks if player has enough credits

The credits_changed(current, delta) signal is emitted on every change and consumed by the HUD economy module for real-time display updates. In multiplayer, credit operations are routed through PlayerStateManager which handles per-player state synchronization.

docs.title: Station Market Popup (StationMarketPopup, 650 lines)
docs.text:
A popup window (860×560px) with two panels:

Sell Panel: - ItemList showing player's cargo items filtered by category - Category filter dropdown (All, raw, trade_goods, consumable, data, artifact, salvage, etc.) - Quantity spinbox (1–stack size) - Quote label showing unit price and total credits - Sell button

Buy Panel: - ItemList showing station's available commodities with badges - Category filter dropdown - Quantity spinbox (up to 999 or stock limit) - Quote label with pricing - Buy button

Header: Station name, player credits, cargo volume/capacity Footer: Transaction history (last 14 entries), help text explaining badge meanings and station security level

docs.title: HUD Economy Module (hud_economy_module.gd)
docs.text: Integrates credit display and resource ledger into the main HUD overlay. Refreshes every 1 second with current credits and cargo values.

docs.title: Jobs Journal & Active Jobs Widget
docs.text: The jobs system displays active missions in a dedicated widget with progress bars, faction-colored accents, and reward projections. The JobsJournal tracks completed/failed missions for historical reference.

docs.title: Key Files Reference
docs.text: File Lines Purpose scripts/systems/trade_service.gd 1900 Core economy engine: catalog, pricing, inventory, discovery sales, RPC channels scripts/resources/commodity_data.gd 22 Commodity definition resource (id, name, category, price, volume, legal) scripts/resources/economy_profile_data.gd 38 Market tuning: inventory tiers, variance ranges, event modifiers scripts/systems/credits_manager.gd 99 Credits singleton wrapping PlayerStateManager scripts/ui/station_market_popup.gd 650 Buy/sell market UI with badges and category filters scripts/ui/job_board_presenter.gd 374 Job/mission board display logic (convoy escort, pirate raid) scripts/ui/hud_economy_module.gd — HUD integration: credits + resource ledger refresh scripts/ui/jobs_journal.gd — Mission history tracking

docs.title: Relationships
docs.text:
• Consumed by stations-megastructures for market popup and service costs at stations
  • Feeds into economy-trade via the commodity catalog shared with station inventory generation
  • Discovery sales connect to discovery-exploration journal system
  • Credits managed by CM feed into ship-systems fuel/purchase operations
  • Job board integrates with factions-lore for faction-colored UI and patron/opposition data
  • Market events (demand spike/oversupply) are seeded per-station using hash-based deterministic generation

docs.title: Event Replication System
docs.text: title: Event Replication System created: 2026-05-17 updated: 2026-05-17 type: concept tags: [networking] sources: [scripts/network/event_replication_service.gd, scripts/level/system_event_runtime.gd, scripts/world/world_replication_service.gd] confidence: high

docs.title: Event Replication System
docs.text: Total codebase footprint: ~3147 lines across 3 files

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Server-to-client event replication for system-level events like station arrivals, jump completions, and discovery notifications. Uses a routing table to deliver events only to relevant clients.

This subsystem is a core part of the 3dSpace game architecture, integrating with multiple other systems to provide seamless gameplay experiences. It follows the FW_ framework conventions and uses deterministic data pipelines for reproducible results.

docs.title: Key Design Decisions
docs.text:
• Deterministic generation: All system state derives from seeded data structures
  • Server-authoritative: Multiplayer operations use server as source of truth
  • Sectorized architecture: Galaxy data is divided into sectors for streaming efficiency
  • Runtime caching: Generated data persists between sessions to avoid regeneration

docs.title: Key Files (3147 lines total)
docs.text:
• scripts/network/event_replication_service.gd (183 lines)
  • scripts/level/system_event_runtime.gd (2964 lines)
  • scripts/world/world_replication_service.gd (not found)

docs.title: File Details
docs.text:
• unknown: _ready(), request_accept_merchant_convoy_from_client(world_instance_id: String, station_id: String), request_active_event_refresh_from_client(world_instance_id: String), request_accept_pirate_raid_from_client(world_instance_id: String, station_id: String), send_active_event_snapshot_to_peer(peer_id: int, world_instance_id: String, event_data: Dictionary)
  • SystemEventRuntime:

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
The subsystem follows a layered architecture pattern:

  1. Data layer: Resource definitions (SystemRecipe, GalaxySectorSummary, etc.) define the static configuration
  2. Generation layer: Procedural builders transform data into runtime objects
  3. Runtime layer: World managers handle live simulation and client synchronization
  4. Replication layer: Network services broadcast state changes to connected clients
