docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The political landscape of the game galaxy is shaped by seven major factions, each with distinct governments, territories, economies, and relationships. These factions control star systems across the procedurally generated galaxy, establishing borders, conflict zones, and core worlds that define the player's experience. The faction system drives gameplay through reputation mechanics, territorial conflicts, trade opportunities, and narrative context for missions and encounters.

Factions are defined by FactionData resources (loaded from .tres files in resources/factions/) and managed at runtime by the FM (FactionManager) autoload singleton. Territory allocation uses BFS cellular expansion — deterministic: the same galaxy seed produces identical faction layouts every time.

docs.title: Government Types
docs.text:
Each faction has a government type that influences station generation, mission availability, NPC behavior, and economic preferences. The seven types range from democratic republics to lawless anarchy.

Type Code Description Democracy DEMOCRACY (0) Elected officials govern with principles of liberty and mutual defense. Trading bonuses, lawful society. Known for advanced technology and strong navies. Oligarchy OLIGARCHY (1) Corporate control by a council of powerful interests. Mining bonuses, pragmatic neutrality. Self-reliant frontier settlements bound by economic cooperation. Dictatorship DICTATORSHIP (2) Military-focused authoritarian rule. Combat bonuses, strict law enforcement. Stability maintained through noble governors and military hierarchy. Theocracy THEOCRACY (3) Religious order governing spiritual matters. Exploration bonuses, isolationist tendencies. Worship of cosmic mysteries and ancient stellar phenomena. Anarchy ANARCHY (4) No central control — lawless frontier. Smuggling bonuses, chaotic internal dynamics. Loose alliances of outcasts operating in unclaimed space. Feudal FEUDAL (5) Noble houses with mixed governance styles. Balanced bonuses across military and civilian sectors. Traditional hierarchies adapted to spacefaring society. Corporate CORPORATE (6) Company rule driven by profit and trade. Economic focus, mining and trading bonuses. Corporate conglomerates controlling major trade routes.

docs.title: Terran Federation (FED)
docs.text:
• Government: Democracy
  • Colors: Primary #3380E6 (blue), Secondary #E6F2FF (white-blue), Map #408BF2 (light blue, 70% alpha)
  • Territory Weight: 2.5 — vast democratic empire spanning many systems
  • Preferred Economies: Industrial, Manufacturing, Agricultural, High Tech
  • Station Types: Military Outpost, Trading Post, Research Station
  • Commodity Bias: Values data (1.2×), consumables (1.1×), trade goods (1.05×)
  • Policy: Does not allow smuggling
  • Lore: A democratic alliance of human worlds united under principles of liberty, equality, and mutual defense. The Federation maintains the largest standing navy in the galaxy and is known for its advanced technology and strong trading networks.

docs.title: Stellar Empire (EMP)
docs.text:
• Government: Dictatorship
  • Colors: Primary #E62626 (red), Secondary #FFD94D (gold), Map #F23333 (light red, 70% alpha)
  • Territory Weight: 3.0 — the largest territorial empire in the galaxy
  • Preferred Economies: Military Industrial, Mining, High Tech, Industrial
  • Station Types: Military Outpost, Research Station, Trading Post
  • Commodity Bias: Values raw materials (1.1×), salvage (1.05×)
  • Policy: Does not allow smuggling
  • Lore: A vast imperial state ruled by an Emperor and a council of noble houses. The Empire values strength, order, and military prowess. While authoritarian, the Empire maintains stability across its worlds through a complex hierarchy of noble governors and military governors.

docs.title: Free Merchants Guild (FMG)
docs.text:
• Government: Corporate
  • Colors: Primary #33BF4D (green), Secondary #FFE680 (gold-yellow), Map #40CC59 (light green, 70% alpha)
  • Territory Weight: 1.5 — moderate territorial presence focused on trade hubs
  • Preferred Economies: Trading, Mining, Manufacturing, Industrial, High Tech
  • Station Types: Trading Post, Spaceport, Research Station
  • Commodity Bias: Strongly values trade goods (1.25×), consumables (1.15×)
  • Lore: A corporate conglomerate of trading houses, shipping companies, and merchant princes. The Guild controls major trade routes and maintains neutral relations with most factions. Profit drives their decisions, and they can be found doing business almost anywhere.

docs.title: Outer Rim Collective (ORC)
docs.text:
• Government: Oligarchy
  • Colors: Primary #E68026 (orange), Secondary #996633 (brown), Map #F28D33 (light orange, 70% alpha)
  • Territory Weight: 1.8 — substantial frontier territory in the outer reaches
  • Preferred Economies: Mining, Manufacturing, Military Industrial, High Tech
  • Station Types: Military Outpost, Trading Post, Research Station
  • Commodity Bias: Strongly values raw materials (1.2×), salvage (1.1×)
  • Lore: A loose federation of mining colonies, industrial worlds, and frontier settlements in the galaxy's outer reaches. The Collective values self-reliance and mutual aid. Life is hard on the rim, but the Collective provides protection and economic cooperation for its members.

docs.title: Pirate Confederation (PIR)
docs.text:
• Government: Anarchy
  • Colors: Primary #661A80 (purple), Secondary #262626 (dark gray), Map #802699 (light purple, 70% alpha)
  • Territory Weight: 0.6 — small, scattered pirate bases in unclaimed space
  • Preferred Economies: Salvage-based economy
  • Station Types: Military Outpost, Spaceport (hidden bases)
  • Commodity Bias: Strongly values artifacts (1.3×), salvage (1.25×), trade goods (1.1×)
  • Policy: Does not allow bounty hunting; pirate faction flag set
  • Lore: A loose alliance of pirate captains, smugglers, and outlaws who have carved out hidden bases in unclaimed space. While fractious and prone to internal conflict, the Confederation presents a unified front against the major powers. They raid shipping lanes and hide in the shadows.

docs.title: Church of the Void (CTV)
docs.text:
• Government: Theocracy
  • Colors: Primary #D9D9F2 (pale lavender), Secondary #9966CC (purple), Map #CCCCF2 (light lavender, 70% alpha)
  • Territory Weight: 1.2 — moderate territory focused on isolated research stations
  • Preferred Economies: Military Industrial, High Tech
  • Station Types: Research Station, Trading Post
  • Commodity Bias: Strongly values artifacts (1.25×), data (1.2×)
  • Policy: Does not allow smuggling; exploration-focused faction flag set
  • Lore: A religious order that worships the mysteries of deep space and ancient stellar phenomena. The Church maintains isolated monasteries and research stations, seeking spiritual enlightenment through exploration and study of the cosmos. They are generally neutral in galactic politics.

docs.title: Axiom Directorate (AXM)
docs.text:
• Government: Oligarchy
  • Colors: Primary #38E0E6 (cyan), Secondary #BFF2FF (light cyan), Map #2ED1E6 (medium cyan, 70% alpha)
  • Territory Weight: 0.9 — small but strategically important territory
  • Preferred Economies: Military Industrial, High Tech, Trading, Industrial
  • Station Types: Research Station, Observatory, Trading Post
  • Commodity Bias: Strongly values data (1.35×), artifacts (1.2×)
  • Policy: Does not allow smuggling; exploration-focused faction flag set
  • Lore: A classified technocratic research power built around observatories, anomaly laboratories, survey fleets, and tightly controlled data archives. The Directorate treats scientific discovery and deep-space signal analysis as strategic assets, trading advanced instrumentation for access to dangerous knowledge.

docs.title: Relations System
docs.text: Factions maintain relationships with each other on a scale from -1.0 (maximum hostility) to +1.0 (maximum alliance). These relations are static — defined in faction data resources and used during gameplay to determine NPC behavior, mission availability, and territorial conflicts.

docs.title: Relation Thresholds
docs.text:
• Allied: > 0.5 — factions cooperate, share intelligence, avoid conflict
  • Neutral: -0.5 to +0.5 — standard diplomatic relations, trade possible
  • Hostile: < -0.5 — active antagonism, combat encounters likely

docs.title: Key Relationships
docs.text:
• FED ↔ EMP (-0.8): Cold war rivalry — the two largest powers have been in tension for generations
  • FMG ↔ ORC (+0.7): Strong economic partnership — merchants and miners trade heavily
  • PIR vs Everyone (-0.6 to -0.9): Universal enemy — all major factions oppose piracy
  • CTV ↔ AXM (+0.45): Research alliance — both value scientific discovery and share knowledge
  • FED ↔ FMG (+0.6): Strong trade partnership — federation provides security, merchants provide goods

docs.title: Player Reputation
docs.text: Player reputation with each faction is tracked independently on a scale from -100 to +100. Actions in the game world affect standing with specific factions based on what faction controls the system where actions occur and which faction benefits or suffers from those actions.

docs.title: Standing Levels
docs.text: Score Range Standing Color Description +75 to +100 Allied Bright green #33CC33 Trusted ally — access to exclusive missions, discounts, friendly NPCs +25 to +75 Friendly Green #66B366 Well-regarded — bonus trade prices, some mission access -25 to +25 Neutral Gray #B3B3B3 Unknown status — standard interactions, no bonuses or penalties -75 to -25 Unfriendly Orange #CC8033 Disliked — higher prices, hostile NPC behavior in some systems -100 to -75 Hostile Red #E63333 Enemy — attacked on sight by faction NPCs, denied access to controlled systems

docs.title: Reputation Changes
docs.text: Reputation changes are triggered by: - Contract completion: Success/failure of missions affects the contracting faction's standing (0.12–0.42 per contract) - Combat outcomes: Destroying or sparing faction ships - Trade decisions: Buying/selling with faction-controlled stations - Exploration: Discovering systems in faction territory

docs.title: Reputation History
docs.text: The last 50 reputation changes are tracked with timestamps, faction IDs, delta values, and reasons. This history feeds into news events and narrative feedback for the player.

docs.title: Territory Generation
docs.text: Factions expand their territories using BFS cellular expansion from initial seed points. The territory_weight property controls how much territory each faction claims: - High weight (2.5–3.0): Large empires spanning many systems (Federation, Empire) - Medium weight (1.2–1.8): Moderate frontier presence (Collective, Church of the Void) - Low weight (0.6–0.9): Small scattered territories (Pirates, Axiom Directorate)

docs.title: Territory Zones
docs.text: Each territory is divided into three zones that affect gameplay: | Zone | Definition | Gameplay Effect | |------|-----------|-----------------| | Core | Systems at territory center | High security, friendly NPCs, exclusive missions | | Border | Systems adjacent to other factions | Moderate security, potential conflict encounters | | Conflict Zone | 10 systems deep from border | Low security, active faction conflicts, bounty opportunities |

docs.title: Policy Flags
docs.text: Factions have policy flags that affect gameplay: - is_pirate_faction: Marks the faction as pirate — affects NPC behavior and mission types - allows_smuggling: Whether smuggling is permitted in faction territory (only Pirates allow it) - allows_bounty_hunting: Whether bounty hunting contracts are available for this faction - is_exploration_focused: Faction values exploration — provides bonuses for survey missions

docs.title: Economy Preferences
docs.text: Each faction has preferred economy types that influence which systems fall under their control: - FED: Values industrial and high-tech economies (types 5, 6, 7) - EMP: Military-industrial focus with mining (types 4, 8, 7) - FMG: Broad economic interests across trading, manufacturing, and tech (types 1, 2, 3, 6, 7) - ORC: Mining and industrial production (types 2, 3, 4) - PIR: Salvage-based economy (type 0) — operates in lawless space - CTV: Military-industrial and high-tech research (types 5, 7) - AXM: Diverse tech-focused economies (types 4, 5, 6, 7)

docs.title: Faction Visual Identity
docs.text:
Each faction has three color definitions used consistently across the game:

Faction Primary Color Secondary Color Map Color Terran Federation #3380E6 (blue) #E6F2FF (white-blue) #408BF2 (light blue, 70% alpha) Stellar Empire #E62626 (red) #FFD94D (gold) #F23333 (light red, 70% alpha) Free Merchants Guild #33BF4D (green) #FFE680 (gold-yellow) #40CC59 (light green, 70% alpha) Outer Rim Collective #E68026 (orange) #996633 (brown) #F28D33 (light orange, 70% alpha) Pirate Confederation #661A80 (purple) #262626 (dark gray) #802699 (light purple, 70% alpha) Church of the Void #D9D9F2 (pale lavender) #9966CC (purple) #CCCCF2 (light lavender, 70% alpha) Axiom Directorate #38E0E6 (cyan) #BFF2FF (light cyan) #2ED1E6 (medium cyan, 70% alpha)

Primary color is used for ship liveries, UI accents, and faction branding. Secondary color provides contrast for highlights and decorative elements. Map color is the semi-transparent version used on the galaxy map to fill territory regions.

docs.title: Key Files Reference
docs.text: File Purpose Lines scripts/resources/faction_data.gd Faction definition resource: identity, government type, colors, policies, relations 174 scripts/autoload/faction_manager.gd Central manager: territory allocation (BFS), reputation tracking, faction queries 1,444 scripts/resources/faction_territory.gd Territory resource: system coords, core/border/conflict zones, hull computation 439 scripts/resources/player_reputation.gd Player standing tracker: -100 to +100 scale, 5 standing levels, history log 150 resources/factions/*.tres Faction data files (7 default factions) ~30 each

docs.title: Relationships
docs.text:
• Consumed by world-generation pipeline via ProceduralFactionAssigner for system-level faction assignment
  • Drives economy-trade through commodity category biases and preferred economy types
  • Influences npcs-patrol-wings NPC behavior based on player reputation with controlling faction
  • Provides context for discovery-exploration mission generation in faction-controlled systems

docs.title: Feed System — News Event Broadcast Pipeline
Summary
docs.text: The feed system is the game's centralized event broadcast and persistence layer. It collects gameplay events from multiple sources (discovery publications, faction reputation changes, route activity, world state shifts), enriches them with metadata, assigns priority scores for story ranking, persists everything to SQLite, and generates daily rollup summaries. Events flow through a deterministic pipeline: raw event dictionary → FES record method → database insertion into feed_event_log and feed_story_candidates tables → optional daily rollup rebuild. UI widgets consume the stored events to display news feeds, headlines, and summaries to players.

docs.title: Architecture Overview
docs.text:
┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐    ┌───────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐
│  GAMEPLAY    │    │   FES        │    │   SQLite DB   │    │     UI       │
│  SOURCES     │───▶│ (event bus)  │───▶│               │───▶│  WIDGETS     │
│              │    │              │    │ feed_event_log│    │              │
│ Discovery    │    │ record_      │    │ feed_candidates│   │ headlines    │
│ Publication  │    │ discovery_   │    │ feed_rollups  │    │ summaries    │
│ Faction rep  │    │ faction_     │    │               │    │ route events │
│ Route events │    │ route_       │    │               │    │ world state  │
│ World state  │    │ world_state_ │    │               │    │              │
└──────────────┘    └──────────────┘    └───────────────┘    └──────────────┘

docs.title: FES (FeedEventService) — Central Event Bus
docs.text:
class_name FES, loaded as an autoload singleton at /root/FeedEventService. It provides four public record methods, each accepting a Dictionary event payload and returning a result dict with ok, event_id, candidate_id, and status flags. All methods require ServerDatabase.fetch() to be non-null (server-side only).

Event scope: All events use EVENT_SCOPE_PUBLIC = "public".

docs.title: Event Types
docs.text:
Constant Value Source EVENT_TYPE_DISCOVERY_PUBLICATION "discovery_facet_published" First-time discovery of a subject/facet EVENT_TYPE_DISCOVERY_VALIDATION "discovery_facet_validated" Subsequent validation of existing discovery EVENT_TYPE_FACTION_REPUTATION "faction_reputation_swing" Faction standing changes

Route event types (validated against ROUTE_EVENT_TYPES array): - "delivery", "mining_delivery", "pirate_pressure", "pirate_eliminated", "patrol_response", "trader_destroyed"

World state event types: - "control_shift", "border_pressure_spike", "frontier_census"

docs.title: Discovery Publication Pipeline
docs.text:
record_discovery_publication(event) handles the most complex event type. The pipeline:

  1. Validation: Checks for required subject_id and facet_key; returns early with error if missing
  2. Timestamp resolution: Uses event_ts or falls back to system time; resolves date text
  3. Coordinate resolution: Parses system_coords (Vector3i or Dictionary) into X/Y/Z integers; generates display coordinates like "(1,2,3)"
  4. Facet lookup: Queries DiscoveryFacetRegistry.get_definition(subject_kind, facet_key) to determine if the discovery is newsworthy and get its category
  5. Story classification: Resolves story_kind (e.g., "science_breakthrough", "anomaly_alert", "frontier_milestone") based on facet key, first-publication flag, and category
  6. Priority scoring: _compute_discovery_story_weight() starts at base 40, adds: +25 if newsworthy, +20 for first publication, +min(10, validation_count) for extra confirmations, +5 each for system name and subject name presence, +5 for science category
  7. Event ID generation: "evt:{type}:{subject_id}:{facet_key}:{timestamp}" with player suffix for validations
  8. Candidate ID generation: "cand:{date}:{story_kind}:{subject_id}:{facet_key}"
  9. Headline/summary hints: Template-based text generation using _build_discovery_headline_hint() and _build_discovery_summary_hint()

docs.title: Faction Reputation Tracking
docs.text:
record_faction_event(event) handles standing changes:

  • Skips events where abs(delta) < 0.2 and no standing threshold was crossed
  • Story kind is "standing_shift" if the standing tier changed, otherwise "reputation_swing"
  • Priority score starts at 45 + min(20, abs(delta)*20), plus +20 for standing tier changes
  • Event ID: "evt:faction:{player_id}:{faction_id}:{timestamp}"

docs.title: Route Events
docs.text:
record_route_event(event) processes delivery, mining, and pirate activity:

  • Validates event type against ROUTE_EVENT_TYPES array
  • Categories: "economy" (delivery, mining_delivery) or "security" (pirate events, patrol, trader_destroyed)
  • Story kinds map to narrative types: "trade_report", "mining_report", "security_alert", "patrol_success", etc.
  • Priority scoring incorporates risk level and cargo amount as modifiers

docs.title: System Directory Upsert
docs.text: Both discovery publications and route events call upsert_system_directory_entry() which maintains a live directory of systems with their current faction control, economy type, security profile, and population data. Uses INSERT ... ON CONFLICT to update in place.

docs.title: Story Candidate Generation
docs.text:
Every event creates a corresponding entry in the feed_story_candidates table. Candidates are the primary source for UI feed widgets — they contain the pre-computed headline hint, summary hint, priority score, and metadata needed to render news items without re-processing raw events.

The build_story_candidates_for_date() method can regenerate candidates from existing event log entries (for rebuild scenarios).

docs.title: Daily Rollup System
docs.text:
_rebuild_discovery_rollup_for_date() and _rebuild_route_rollup_for_date() aggregate daily statistics into the feed_daily_rollups table:

Discovery rollup: - Total public discoveries count - First discovery count - Top 3 systems by event count - Top 5 players by candidate count

Route rollup: - Total deliveries, mining deliveries - Total disruptions (pirate pressure + trader destroyed) - Total pirates eliminated - Total mining quantity - Top 3 systems by route activity

The rebuild_daily_rollup(date_text) public method triggers both rollups for a given date. Rollups are automatically rebuilt after each discovery or route event insertion via _rebuild_discovery_rollup_for_date() and _rebuild_route_rollup_for_date().

docs.title: SQLite Persistence Schema
docs.text:
feed_event_log table columns: - event_id (PK), event_type, event_scope, event_ts, date_text, world_instance_id - player_id, player_tag, system_x/y/z, system_name, display_coordinates - subject_id/kind/entity_id, subject_display_name, parent_subject_id/display_name - faction_id/name, category, story_weight, newsworthy (0/1) - context_json, payload_json, tags_json, metadata_json

feed_story_candidates table columns: - candidate_id (PK), source_event_id, date_text, story_kind, category - priority_score, headline_hint, summary_hint - system_x/y/z, system_name, display_coordinates - subject_id/kind/display_name, source_player_id/tag, is_first_claim, validation_count - metadata_json

feed_daily_rollups table columns: - date_text (PK), discovery counts, route counts, mining quantities - top_systems_json, top_players_json - updated_at

docs.title: Event Flow Summary
docs.text:
1. Gameplay action occurs (e.g., player discovers a new system)
  2. Source calls FES method with an event dictionary containing all relevant fields
  3. FES validates and enriches: resolves timestamps, coordinates, facet definitions; computes story weight, headline/summary hints
  4. Database insertion: Event goes into feed_event_log; candidate goes into feed_story_candidates (both use upsert for idempotency)
  5. System directory updated (for discovery and route events)
  6. Daily rollup rebuilt automatically after the event
  7. UI widgets query feed_story_candidates ordered by priority score to display ranked news items

docs.title: Relationships
docs.text:
• Consumed by discovery-exploration for publication/validation events
  • Integrated with factions-lore via faction reputation tracking in FactionManager
  • Used by NPC simulation (NpcSimulationService) and patrol management (PatrolM) for route/world-state events
  • Depends on fw-config-service for server database configuration

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: The FPS HUD instrument system provides a canvas-layer overlay (FpsHudOverlay) that hosts a collection of UI widgets for the first-person player view. Widgets are created through a factory pattern managed by FpsHudWidgetBootstrap, which handles priority-ordered instantiation, visibility toggling via @export properties on the overlay, and frame-budgeted processing to avoid boot stalls. Individual instrument widgets (lidar, magnetometer, radiation meter, spectrometer, planet science dossier) extend FpsInstrumentWidget and follow a common activate/dismiss lifecycle with mouse capture/release semantics. The system integrates with the radial menu for instrument selection and connects to PlayerAvatarController signals for real-time data display.

docs.title: FpsHudOverlay — Canvas Layer Host
docs.text:
class_name FpsHudOverlay, extends CanvasLayer. It is the root container for all FPS HUD widgets. Created as a scene instance from FPS_HUD_SCENE in main.gd.

Container nodes (accessed via @onready): - %ReticleContainer — crosshair reticle - %PromptContainer — interaction prompts, ship interact widget - %DevReadoutContainer — developer debug readout - %CompassRingContainer — directional compass ring - %RadialMenuContainer — radial science menu - %ChatContainer — chat messages - %SpectrometerContainer — spectrometer instrument overlay - %RadiationMeterContainer — radiation meter overlay - %PlanetScienceDossierContainer — planet science dossier overlay

Export properties control widget visibility: - show_reticle, show_ship_interact, show_interact_prompt, show_dev_readout, show_compass, show_radial_menu, show_spectrometer

Avatar integration: Connects to PlayerPossession.local_avatar_changed signal. When the avatar changes, it: 1. Disconnects all signals from the old avatar 2. Reconnects to the new PlayerAvatarController: gravity_reading, temperature_reading, surface_reading_progress, surface_reading_completed, radiation_scan_requested, radial_menu_requested, spectrometer_scan_requested, planet_science_dossier_requested, lidar_scan_requested 3. Syncs all existing widgets with the new avatar via _sync_widget_with_avatar()

Signal handlers: - Gravity/temperature readings → toast notifications via %ToastAnchor/ToastNotification - Surface reading progress/completed → toast + screen flash effect (ColorRect tweened to alpha 0) - Scan requests → resolve and activate corresponding instrument widget - Radial menu request → open FpsRadialMenu

docs.title: FpsInstrumentWidget — Base Class
docs.text:
class_name FpsInstrumentWidget, extends Control. All instrument overlays inherit from this base. Key contract:

Lifecycle: 1. activate(avatar_node) — Sets avatar, adjusts size to viewport, calls _on_activate(), sets visible=true and active=true, enables input processing, notifies Game.notify_popup_opened() 2. dismiss() — Disables input, clears avatar, hides widget, calls _on_dismiss(), notifies Game.notify_popup_closed() and Game.consume_pause_action(), emits dismissed signal

Key methods: - _unlocks_mouse() — Returns true by default; when true, the HUD captures mouse mode to visible on activate and restores previous mode on dismiss - handle_scan() — Override point for instrument-specific scan logic - _build_backdrop(alpha) — Creates a semi-transparent black backdrop ColorRect (default alpha 0.35) - _build_label(parent, text, font_size, color) — Helper to create centered labels

Signal: dismissed — Emitted when the widget is dismissed; HUD overlay uses this to track the active instrument and restore mouse mode.

docs.title: FpsHudWidgetBootstrap — Factory Orchestrator
docs.text:
class_name FpsHudWidgetBootstrap, extends RefCounted. Manages widget creation with priority ordering and frame-budgeted processing.

Priority levels: - CRITICAL (0): Reticle, ShipInteract, InteractPrompt - HIGH (1): DevReadout - MEDIUM (2): CompassRing, RadialMenu, Spectrometer, RadiationMeter, PlanetScienceDossier

Registration: _register_widget_bootstraps() in FpsHudOverlay registers each widget with its factory callable and optional visibility property name. Widgets with empty visibility properties are always created; others respect the corresponding show_* export property.

Frame budgeting: process_next() processes one widget per frame, stopping if the frame time exceeds _time_budget_ms (5ms). This prevents HUD boot from stalling the game's main loop. The bootstrap is called each frame via FpsHudOverlay._process().

docs.title: FpsHudWidgetFactories — Widget Factory Functions
docs.text:
class_name FpsHudWidgetFactories, extends RefCounted. Static factory methods that create and parent widgets to their respective containers:

Factory Widget Type Container Notes factory_reticle ColorRect (crosshair) reticle_container Simple 4x4 white rect at center factory_ship_interact FpsShipInteractWidget prompt_container Preloaded script, shows ship re-enter prompts factory_dev_readout FpsDevReadoutWidget dev_readout_container Debug readout for development factory_interact_prompt FpsInteractPromptWidget prompt_container "Press F — {name}" interaction prompts factory_compass_ring CompassRing (scene) compass_ring_container Loaded from scene file factory_radial_menu FpsRadialMenu radial_menu_container Radial science action menu factory_spectrometer FpsSpectrometerWidget spectrometer_container Planet composition analysis overlay factory_radiation_meter FpsRadiationMeterWidget radiation_meter_container Radiation level display factory_planet_science_dossier FpsPlanetScienceDossierWidget dossier_container Comprehensive planet science data

docs.title: Instrument Activation Flow
docs.text:
When a scan is requested (e.g., via PlayerAvatarController.spectrometer_scan_requested):

  1. HUD resolves the widget instance (from bootstrap or container children)
  2. Disconnects old result handler, connects new one
  3. Calls widget.configure(profile, deposits) with scan data
  4. Calls _activate_instrument(widget) which:
  5. Dismisses any previously active instrument
  6. Captures mouse mode if _unlocks_mouse() returns true
  7. Calls widget.activate(_local_avatar)

When the user dismisses an instrument (e.g., presses Escape): 1. Widget emits dismissed signal 2. HUD restores previous mouse mode, clears _active_instrument 3. Widget notifies Game.notify_popup_closed() and Game.consume_pause_action()

docs.title: Surface Notification Widgets
docs.text:
Toast notifications (ToastNotification) display transient messages for: - Gravity readings (white text, 8s duration) - Temperature readings (orange tint, 8s duration) - Surface reading progress (amber, 10s duration) - Scan completion (custom color + screen flash effect)

The screen flash creates a full-screen ColorRect that tweens from the given color to transparent over 0.4s, then queues for freeing.

docs.title: Relationships
docs.text:
• Consumed by player-possession-system for avatar lifecycle management
  • Displays data from instruments controlled by PlayerAvatarController (gravity ball, thermal penetrator, gas sniffer, seismic thumper, magnetometer drifter)
  • Radial menu integrates with avatar's perform_science_action(index) method
  • Toast notifications connect to avatar signals: gravity_reading, temperature_reading, surface_reading_progress, surface_reading_completed

docs.title: FW Framework Additions
docs.text: title: FW Framework Additions (Post-Wiki) created: 2026-05-17 updated: 2026-05-17 type: concept tags: [framework, conventions] sources: [addons/fw/Input/FW_InputRebindService.gd, addons/fw/Platform/FW_Platform.gd, addons/fw/Platform/Steam/FW_SteamService.gd] confidence: high

docs.title: FW Framework Additions (Post-Wiki)
docs.text: Total codebase footprint: ~1255 lines across 9 files

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
New FW_ framework services added since the initial wiki: input rebinding, platform abstraction, resource caching, UI overlays, shader utilities, and tween helpers.

This subsystem is a core part of the 3dSpace game architecture, integrating with multiple other systems to provide seamless gameplay experiences. It follows the FW_ framework conventions and uses deterministic data pipelines for reproducible results.

docs.title: Key Design Decisions
docs.text:
• Deterministic generation: All system state derives from seeded data structures
  • Server-authoritative: Multiplayer operations use server as source of truth
  • Sectorized architecture: Galaxy data is divided into sectors for streaming efficiency
  • Runtime caching: Generated data persists between sessions to avoid regeneration

docs.title: Key Files (1255 lines total)
docs.text:
• addons/fw/Input/FW_InputRebindService.gd (656 lines)
  • addons/fw/Platform/FW_Platform.gd (10 lines)
  • addons/fw/Platform/Steam/FW_SteamService.gd (188 lines)
  • addons/fw/Resources/FW_PreloadQueue.gd (95 lines)

  • addons/fw/UI/FW_ContextMenu.gd (44 lines)
  • addons/fw/UI/FW_ModalOverlay.gd (106 lines)
  • addons/fw/Utils/FW_ShaderValues.gd (26 lines)
  • addons/fw/Utils/FW_TweenUtils.gd (17 lines)

docs.title: File Details
docs.text:
• FW_InputRebindService: configure(config: FW_ConfigService, actions: Variant, autosave_enabled: bool = true), begin_rebind(action: StringName, prefer_device: int = -1, expect: String = ""), cancel_rebind(), feed_event(event: InputEvent), apply_event(action: StringName, event: InputEvent)
  • FW_Platform:
  • FW_SteamService: configure(config: FW_ConfigService), is_platform_supported(), has_steam_singleton(), initialize(), shutdown()

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
The subsystem follows a layered architecture pattern:

  1. Data layer: Resource definitions (SystemRecipe, GalaxySectorSummary, etc.) define the static configuration
  2. Generation layer: Procedural builders transform data into runtime objects
  3. Runtime layer: World managers handle live simulation and client synchronization
  4. Replication layer: Network services broadcast state changes to connected clients

  • [ ] Are there performance implications for large sector counts?
  • [ ] Should event replication use a more granular delivery policy?

docs.title: FW Framework Addon — Service Classes & Architecture
Overview
docs.text: The FW (Framework) addon is a collection of reusable service classes located in /home/j/3d-space/addons/fw/. It provides core infrastructure for the 3dSpace project including configuration persistence, scene transitions, state machines, audio management, HTTP networking, and async save/load with schema migration. Services are configured via FW_ConfigService which acts as a central config hub — each service connects to it on initialization to read settings and listen for changes.

docs.title: Directory Structure
docs.text:
addons/fw/
├── Bootstrap/FW_Bootstrap.gd          # Initialization orchestrator
├── Config/
│   ├── FW_ConfigService.gd            # Central config persistence (INI-style)
│   ├── FW_FrameworkDefaults.gd        # Default configuration values
│   └── FW_WindowPrefs.gd              # Window preference management
├── State/
│   ├── FW_StateMachine.gd             # Explicit state machine pattern
│   └── FW_State.gd                    # Base state class (optional)
├── Scenes/FW_SceneRouter.gd           # Scene transitions with animated effects
├── Audio/FW_AudioService.gd           # SFX pool + music management
├── Net/FW_NetService.gd               # HTTP API client with health checks
├── Save/FW_SaveService.gd             # Async save/load with schema migration
├── Resources/
│   ├── FW_ResourceCache.gd            # Resource preloading cache
│   └── FW_PreloadQueue.gd             # Deferred preload queue
├── Input/FW_InputRebindService.gd     # Input action rebinding
├── Platform/
│   ├── FW_Platform.gd                 # Platform detection (Steam Deck, etc.)
│   └── Steam/FW_SteamService.gd       # Steam platform integration
└── Scenes/LevelHelpers/level_manager.gd  # Level loading helpers

docs.title: FW_ConfigService
docs.text: Located at /home/j/3d-space/addons/fw/Config/FW_ConfigService.gd (89 lines), this is the central configuration service. It wraps Godot's ConfigFile class with typed getters, default value support, and change signals.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• INI-style persistence — Saves to user://save/framework_config.cfg by default
  • Default values — Register defaults via register_defaults() before loading; fallback chain: saved value → registered default → explicit fallback
  • Typed getters — get_bool(), get_int(), get_float(), get_string(), get_vec2() with automatic type conversion
  • Change signals — Emits changed(section, key, value) on every set, enabling services to react to config changes

docs.title: API
docs.text:
func register_defaults(defaults_by_section: Dictionary) -> void  # Register fallback defaults
func load(path: String = DEFAULT_PATH) -> void                  # Load from file (creates new ConfigFile on error)
func save() -> void                                             # Save to loaded path
func get_value(section, key, fallback=null) -> Variant          # Get with fallback chain
func set_value(section, key, value, autosave=false) -> void     # Set and optionally auto-save
func has_key(section, key) -> bool                              # Check if key exists in saved config

docs.title: Config Sections Used by Project
docs.text: Section Keys Purpose audio sfx_enabled, sfx_volume_db, music_enabled, music_volume_db Audio settings display rotated Screen rotation state (Steam Deck) net enabled, base_url, api_key, auto_disable_on_fail, healthcheck_path, healthcheck_cache_seconds Network API config

docs.title: FW_SceneRouter
docs.text: Located at /home/j/3d-space/addons/fw/Scenes/FW_SceneRouter.gd (507 lines), manages scene transitions with animated effects and screen rotation support.

docs.title: Scene Transition System
docs.text: Two transition modes: 1. Shader-based — Uses a custom shader for complex visual transitions (wipes, iris, spikes, clock sectors) 2. Lightweight fade — Simple black fade in/out for mobile devices or when no shader is configured

docs.title: Built-in Transition Presets
docs.text: Preset Type Description "Directional Wipe" 0 (directional) Single-direction wipe "Corner Wipe" 0 Four-corner wipe "Diagonal Wipe" 0 45° diagonal wipe "Cross-shaped Transition" 0 Expanding cross from center "Iris Transition" 2 (radial) Circular iris opening/closing "Spike Transition" 2 Star/spike pattern from center "Overlapping Diamonds" 2 Diamond pattern with feathering "Center-Clock Transition" 3 (sector) Clock-sector wipe from center "Corner-Clock Transition3" 3 Multi-corner clock sectors "Seamless Striped Flower" 3 Complex striped flower pattern

docs.title: Screen Rotation Rig
docs.text: For Steam Deck and portrait-mode displays: 1. Creates a Node2D rotator rig that rotates the entire viewport 90° 2. Wraps the scene in a SubViewportContainer inside the rig 3. Reparents popups/windows to the scene host viewport for correct positioning 4. Toggled via set_rotated(value) with config persistence

docs.title: API
docs.text:
func change_scene(path: String, transition_params={}) -> void  # Load new scene with transition
func set_rotated(value: bool, persist=true) -> void           # Toggle screen rotation
func toggle_rotation(persist=true) -> void                    # Toggle and persist
func setup_rotation_rig() -> void                             # Build rotation rig manually
func teardown_rotation_rig() -> void                          # Remove rotation rig

docs.title: Signals
docs.text:
• rotation_changed(is_rotated: bool) — Rotation state changed
  • transition_started(path: String) — Transition animation began
  • transition_finished(path: String, ok: bool) — Scene swap completed

docs.title: FW_StateMachine
docs.text: Located at /home/j/3d-space/addons/fw/State/FW_StateMachine.gd (77 lines), implements an explicit state machine pattern for managing behavior modes. States can be either FW_State objects or plain dictionaries of callables.

docs.title: State Lifecycle
docs.text:
start(initial_state) → on_enter("", data)
transition_to(next, data) → on_exit(prev) → on_enter(prev, data) → emit state_changed(prev, next)
tick(delta) → on_update(delta)          # Called every frame if auto_process=true
send_event(event) → on_event(event)     # Dispatch events to current state

docs.title: State Definition Formats
docs.text:
Dictionary format (simple): 

state_machine.add_state("idle", {
    "on_enter": Callable(self, "_on_idle_enter"),
    "on_update": Callable(self, "_on_idle_update"),
    "on_event": Callable(self, "_on_idle_event"),
})

Object format (full): 

class MyState extends FW_State:
    func on_enter(prev_state, data): ...
    func on_exit(next_state): ...
    func on_update(delta): ...
    func on_event(event): ...

state_machine.add_state("combat", my_state_instance)

docs.title: API
docs.text:
func add_state(name: StringName, state: Variant) -> void     # Register a state
func has_state(name: StringName) -> bool                     # Check if state exists
func get_state() -> StringName                               # Get current state name
func start(initial: StringName, data=null) -> Dictionary     # Initialize and enter initial state
func transition_to(next: StringName, data=null) -> Dictionary  # Transition to new state
func tick(delta: float) -> void                              # Process current state update
func send_event(event: Variant) -> void                      # Dispatch event to current state

docs.title: FW_AudioService
docs.text: Located at /home/j/3d-space/addons/fw/Audio/FW_AudioService.gd (154 lines), manages sound effects and music with a reusable SFX player pool.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
• SFX Pool — Pre-creates sfx_pool_size (default 8) AudioStreamPlayer instances on the SFX bus. Reuses idle players for new sounds.
  • Music Player — Single dedicated AudioStreamPlayer on the Music bus.
  • Volume Control — Reads volume settings from FW_ConfigService (audio.sfx_volume_db, audio.music_volume_db). Mutes to -80dB when disabled.

docs.title: API
docs.text:
func register_sfx(id: StringName, stream: AudioStream) -> void    # Register SFX by ID
func register_music(id: StringName, stream: AudioStream) -> void  # Register music by ID
func play_sfx(id, volume_db_offset=0.0, pitch_scale=1.0) -> void  # Play SFX (pooled)
func play_music(id, volume_db_offset=0.0) -> void                 # Play/switch music
func stop_music() -> void                                         # Stop current music
func bind_button_group(group_name="all_buttons", sound_id="ui.click") -> void  # Auto-play SFX on button press

docs.title: FW_NetService
docs.text: Located at /home/j/3d-space/addons/fw/Net/FW_NetService.gd (257 lines), provides HTTP API access with health checking and automatic disable-on-failure.

docs.title: Health Check System
docs.text:
• Periodic health checks to base_url + healthcheck_path (default "/")
  • Results cached for healthcheck_cache_seconds (default 2s) to avoid redundant requests
  • auto_disable_on_fail flag: if true, permanently disables network after a failed request and emits network_auto_disabled signal

docs.title: Request Flow
docs.text:
func request_json(method, path_or_url, payload, callback, extra_headers=[], scene=null, auto_disable_override=null) -> void
1. Validates that network is enabled and base_url is set 2. Constructs full URL (joins base_url with relative path) 3. Serializes payload to JSON, builds headers (Content-Type + API key if configured) 4. Creates HTTPRequest node, attaches to scene tree 5. On completion: parses JSON response, calls callback with {ok: true/false, data/error}

docs.title: API Key Support
docs.text: If api_key is configured in the net section, it's automatically added as X-API-Key header to all requests.

docs.title: FW_SaveService
docs.text: Located at /home/j/3d-space/addons/fw/Save/FW_SaveService.gd (710+ lines), provides async save/load with schema migration and corruption recovery. This is the most complex service in the framework.

docs.title: Save Slot System
docs.text:
• 3 slots (0, 1, 2) stored as user://save/slot_0.json, slot_1.json, slot_2.json
  • Each save file contains: schema version, metadata (created_at_unix, saved_at_unix), galaxy state, and game data

docs.title: Schema Migration
docs.text:
The service supports automatic migration between schema versions: 

Version -1 (legacy) → Version 0 → Version 1 (current)
- On load: detects schema version, migrates if needed, writes back canonical format - On save: always writes current schema version (v1) - Corrupt files are backed up to user://save/corrupt/ before being replaced

docs.title: Async Save System
docs.text: Uses a dedicated worker thread with mutex/semaphore synchronization: 1. save_slot_async() queues the job, coalescing duplicate requests for the same slot 2. Worker thread processes jobs from queue, writes files synchronously 3. Results are collected in _async_completed array and dispatched on main thread via _process()

docs.title: Save File Format (v1 Canonical)
docs.text:
{
  "_fw_schema_version": 1,
  "created_at_unix": 1234567890,
  "saved_at_unix": 1234567890,
  "meta": { ... },
  "data": { ... },
  "galaxy_state": { ... }
}

docs.title: Corruption Handling
docs.text:
• Empty files → treated as corruption
  • JSON parse failures → backed up to corrupt/ directory
  • Future schema versions → rejected with error (no downgrade)
  • Migration failures → backed up and rejected

docs.title: API
docs.text:
func load_slot(slot_id: int) -> Dictionary                              # Load save slot (synchronous, emits signal)
func save_slot(slot_id, data, galaxy_state={}, meta_overrides={}) -> Dictionary  # Save slot (sync)
func save_slot_async(slot_id, data, galaxy_state={}, meta_overrides={}) -> Dictionary  # Queue async save
func flush_async_queue(timeout_ms=3000) -> bool                         # Wait for all pending saves
func delete_slot(slot_id: int) -> Dictionary                            # Delete save slot
func get_save_metadata(slot_id: int) -> Dictionary                      # Get metadata without loading full data
func slot_exists(slot_id: int) -> bool                                  # Check if slot has a file

docs.title: Signals
docs.text:
• save_completed(slot_id, ok, result) — Save finished (sync or async)
  • load_completed(slot_id, ok, result) — Load finished
  • slot_deleted(slot_id, ok, result) — Delete finished

docs.title: FW_Bootstrap
docs.text:
Located at /home/j/3d-space/addons/fw/Bootstrap/FW_Bootstrap.gd, orchestrates the initialization of all framework services in the correct order:

  1. Create and configure FW_ConfigService (load defaults + save file)
  2. Configure all other services with the config service reference
  3. Set up scene router, audio, networking, and save services
  4. Ensure proper cleanup on exit (flush async saves, close threads)

docs.title: Key Files Reference
docs.text: File Purpose Lines addons/fw/Config/FW_ConfigService.gd Central config persistence with defaults and typed getters 89 addons/fw/Scenes/FW_SceneRouter.gd Scene transitions (shader-based + fade), screen rotation rig 507 addons/fw/State/FW_StateMachine.gd Explicit state machine with on_enter/on_exit/on_update/on_event lifecycle 77 addons/fw/Audio/FW_AudioService.gd SFX pool (8 players) + music player, volume control from config 154 addons/fw/Net/FW_NetService.gd HTTP API client with health checks, JSON parsing, auto-disable on failure 257 addons/fw/Save/FW_SaveService.gd Async save/load (3 slots), schema migration (-1→0→1), corruption recovery 710+ addons/fw/Bootstrap/FW_Bootstrap.gd Service initialization orchestrator —

docs.title: Relationships
docs.text:
• FW_ConfigService is the foundation — all other services depend on it for configuration
  • FW_SceneRouter used by main game flow for scene transitions (title → galaxy → space)
  • FW_StateMachine consumed by ai-subsystem for NPC behavior state management
  • FW_AudioService integrated with UI via bind_button_group() for consistent sound feedback
  • FW_NetService provides API access for online features (leaderboards, cloud saves)
  • FW_SaveService handles all game persistence including galaxy state and faction data

docs.title: Galaxy & Celestial Bodies — Star Types, Planets, Gates, Phenomena
Overview
docs.text: This page documents the data-driven resource system that defines every celestial body in a procedurally generated galaxy. The pipeline flows from world-generation → individual galaxy-data configuration → per-system resources for stars, planets, asteroid belts, and jump gates. All resources are Resource subclasses with @export fields, enabling designers to author presets as .tres files while the generator creates instances deterministically from a galaxy seed.

docs.title: Star Types & Properties
docs.text: Stars are defined by StarData (scripts/resources/star_data.gd, 436 lines) and drive lighting, visual appearance, habitable zone calculations, and planet placement logic.

docs.title: Spectral Classification (Morgan-Keenan + Exotic)
docs.text:
The system uses the standard OBAFGKM sequence plus six exotic end-state types:

Class Color Temperature Range Mass (solar) Radius (solar) Luminosity O Blue >30,000 K 20.0 M☉ 10.0 R☉ 10,000 L☉ B Blue-white 10,000–30,000 K 10.0 M☉ 5.0 R☉ 1,000 L☉ A White 7,500–10,000 K 2.5 M☉ 1.8 R☉ 40 L☉ F Yellow-white 6,000–7,500 K 1.3 M☉ 1.2 R☉ 4 L☉ G Yellow (Sun) 5,000–6,000 K 1.0 M☉ 1.0 R☉ 1 L☉ K Orange 3,500–5,000 K 0.7 M☉ 0.7 R☉ 0.4 L☉ M Red (dwarf) <3,500 K 0.3 M☉ 0.3 R☉ 0.02 L☉ White Dwarf White halo 10,000–60,000 K 0.6 M☉ 0.01 R☉ 0.01 L☉ Neutron Star Needle ~1,000,000 K 1.4 M☉ ~10 km 0.1 L☉ Black Hole Devouring (dark) 0 K (no light) 10.0 M☉ 0 R☉ 0 L☉ Dark Star Occult ~500 K 3.0 M☉ 1.6 R☉ 0.02 L☉ Red Giant Bloated red ~3,000 K — expanded — Blue Giant Electric blue ~20,000 K — — — Supergiant Cataclysmic 8,000–15,000 K — — —

docs.title: Size / Evolutionary Classification
docs.text:
Stars also carry a SizeClass that affects visual scale and light intensity independently of spectral type:

  • Dwarf → base size ×1.0, light intensity 0.5×
  • Main Sequence → base size ×1.0, light intensity 1.0×
  • Subgiant → base size ×1.0, light intensity 1.5×
  • Giant → base size ×2.0, light intensity 2.0× (triggers proximity point light)
  • Supergiant → base size ×3.0, light intensity 3.0×
  • Hypergiant → base size ×5.0, light intensity 4.0×

docs.title: Visual Families
docs.text:
Each star maps to a VisualFamily that selects its shader/material preset:

Family Trigger Description Solar Calm G/K/M main sequence Steady yellow/orange glow Hot Plasma O/B/A Intense blue-white with turbulence Magnetic Active F-type Variable surface activity Aged Quiet K/M evolved Dim, stable red-orange Bloated Red Red Giant Expanding cool envelope Electric Blue Blue Giant Violent ionization glow Cataclysmic Supergiant Supergiant Pulsating, multi-layered emission White Dwarf Halo White Dwarf Compact bright core with faint halo Needle Neutron Neutron Star Ultra-compact, polar beam hints Devouring Black Hole Black Hole Accretion disk, gravitational lensing Occult Dark Star Dark Star Barely visible, subtle distortion

docs.title: Physical Computation
docs.text:
StarData provides several computed properties:

  • Temperature — auto-calculated from spectral class with ±10% randomization seeded by (spectral_class, size_class) hash for determinism.
  • Light color — black-body approximation mapping temperature to RGB (deep red at 1000 K → blue-white above 30,000 K). Visual color is boosted 1.3× saturation for better appearance.
  • Habitable zone — computed as inner = √luminosity × 0.95 AU, outer = √luminosity × 1.37 AU. Used by planet placement to determine which planets can support life.
  • Light intensity — clamp(base_intensity × √luminosity, 0.5, 5.0) where base depends on size class.
  • Visual size — 50 × √radius_solar with giant/supergiant/hypergiant multipliers (2×/3×/5×).

docs.title: Creation from Galaxy System Data
docs.text: Stars are instantiated via StarData.from_star_type(star_type: int) which maps the integer type codes from GalaxySystemData.StarType enum (0–10) to spectral classes with appropriate default mass/radius/luminosity values.

docs.title: Planets & Orbital Bodies
docs.text: Planets are defined by SystemPlanetData (scripts/resources/system_planet_data.gd, 235 lines). Each planet carries orbital parameters, physical properties, and resource deposits.

docs.title: Planet Types
docs.text: Type Description Atmosphere Surface Rocky Terrestrial (Earth-like) Standard/Thin/Thick Rocky surface with terrain Gas Giant Jupiter-like Thick (H/He) No solid surface — gaseous atmosphere only Ice World Frozen outer system Thin/Standard Frozen ice crust, possible subsurface ocean Lava World Inner system molten None/Thin Molten surface with volcanic activity Ocean World Water-covered Standard/Thick Global liquid water ocean Desert World Arid sandy Thin/Standard Sandy desert, minimal water Toxic World Poisonous atmosphere Toxic/Corrosive Toxic surface chemistry Barren Dead rock None Barren rock, no atmosphere

docs.title: Orbital Parameters
docs.text:
Each planet stores:

  • orbit_distance_au — distance from star in AU (used for placement and habitable zone checks)
  • radius_earth / mass_earth — relative to Earth (1.0 = Earth)
  • surface_gravity — derived from mass/radius, affects landing physics
  • axial_tilt — degrees, affects seasonal variation
  • orbital_period_days — year length in Earth days
  • rotation_period_hours — day length

docs.title: Atmosphere Types
docs.text: Five atmosphere categories affect visuals and gameplay: None, Thin (trace), Standard (Earth-like pressure), Thick (high pressure), Toxic (poisonous gases), Corrosive (acidic). Gas giants always report "no solid surface" regardless of atmosphere.

docs.title: Resources & Inhabitation
docs.text: Planets can carry resource_deposits (dictionary mapping resource name to abundance 0–1) and may be marked as inhabited with a population_level (0–10 log scale). The has_significant_resources() method returns true when total deposit value exceeds 0.5.

docs.title: Habitable Zone Integration
docs.text: is_in_habitable_zone(habitable_zone: Vector2) checks whether the planet's AU distance falls within its star's computed habitable zone bounds (from galaxy-data StarData.get_habitable_zone()). This is used by the generator to place life-bearing planets in appropriate orbits.

docs.title: Jump Gates
docs.text: Jump gates are defined by GateData (scripts/resources/gate_data.gd, 94 lines) and enable travel between systems or scenes.

docs.title: Destination Types
docs.text: Type Use Case Target Format SCENE_FILE Traditional level transitions Path to .tscn file (e.g., res://scenes/level_b.tscn) SYSTEM_COORDINATES Procedural system jumps Vector3i grid coordinates in the galaxy

docs.title: Gate Properties
docs.text:
• gate_id / target_gate_id — unique identifiers for bidirectional linking between gates
  • bi_directional — if true, both gates can trigger travel to each other
  • one_way — gate deactivates after a single use (for story-gated progression)
  • requires_key — optional key string that must be held to activate the gate
  • spawn_offset / spawn_rotation — where and how the ship appears on arrival

docs.title: Procedural Dimensions
docs.text: Gates have configurable ring geometry: ring_major_radius (default 500), ring_minor_radius (20), and portal_radius (450). These are used by procedural gate mesh builders to scale the visual model.

docs.title: Asteroid Belts
docs.text: Asteroid belts are defined by AsteroidBeltData (scripts/resources/asteroid_belt_data.gd, 304 lines) which converts at runtime into AsteroidFieldPreset instances for actual generation.

docs.title: Composition Types & Associated Minerals
docs.text: Composition Primary Minerals Visual Appearance Rocky Silicon, magnesium, iron Brown/tan, rough (0.55), 8 radial segments Metallic Iron, nickel, cobalt, platinum Silver/gray, smooth metallic (0.3 roughness) Icy Water ice, ammonia, methane White/blue-white, glossy (0.15 roughness) Mixed Iron, silicon, water ice Mixed brown, moderate roughness Carbonaceous Carbon, organic compounds, water Dark gray/black, very rough (0.95)

docs.title: Belt Geometry
docs.text:
• orbit_distance_au — center of belt ring from star
  • radial_width_au / thickness_au — radial spread and vertical thickness in AU
  • density (0–1) — controls asteroid count (600–2200 range), spacing, and size variation
  • mineral_richness (0–1) — multiplier for resource value of mined asteroids

docs.title: Shape Modes
docs.text:
The belt converts to one of two runtime shape modes:

  • DISC — flat ring around the star (standard asteroid belts). Uses Gaussian density falloff from center.
  • CLUSTER — compact spherical cluster (debris fields from recent collisions). Single anchor with spread radius.

docs.title: Density Profile & Generation Parameters
docs.text:
At runtime, to_field_preset() converts belt data into an AsteroidFieldPreset with:

  • Density: 0.00004–0.00018 per 1000 m³ (scales with density parameter)
  • Count: 80–320 minimum, 600–2200 maximum asteroids
  • Size range: 0.5× to 3.5× base scale with power-curve distribution skewed toward small objects
  • Large object chance: 3–6% for rare oversized asteroids (up to 14× scale)
  • Mineral weights: composition-specific resource value assignments

docs.title: Orbital Mechanics
docs.text: get_orbital_period_years() applies Kepler's third law: T = √(a³ / M) where a is orbital distance and M is star mass. This gives realistic year lengths for different belt positions.

docs.title: Nebulae & Background Phenomena
docs.text:
Nebulae are defined by BackdropNebulaData (scripts/backdrop/backdrop_nebula_data.gd, 7 lines) — a minimal resource that references a pre-built scene and transforms:

  • scene (PackedScene) — the nebula visual to instantiate
  • position / rotation_degrees / scale — placement in world space

Nebulae are placed as backdrop elements by the level generator, typically at system edges or along sightlines between stars and planets. They use billboard rendering and reactive sky shader parameters (see backdrop-system).

docs.title: Galaxy-Level Configuration
docs.text:
The galaxy itself is configured via GalaxyData (scripts/resources/galaxy_data.gd, 244 lines), which controls the large-scale structure that all celestial bodies inhabit:

  • Bounds: Default 10×10×10 grid = 1,000 possible system locations
  • System spacing: 100 light-years between grid points (configurable)
  • Connectivity radius: 150 LY for standard jump drives
  • Warp effects: Twist (65° max), arm-like radial banding (4 arms), noise displacement, and vertical disk flattening — all driven by galaxy_warp_strength (0–1)

The galaxy seed (galaxy_seed: int = 12345) ensures deterministic generation: the same seed always produces the same star types, planet orbits, belt positions, gate placements, and faction territories. See world-generation for the full pipeline from galaxy seed through system generation to POI placement.

docs.title: Key Files Reference

docs.title: Galaxy Navigation & Routing
docs.text: Total codebase footprint: ~6772 lines across 8 files

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Deterministic galaxy navigation using sectorized zone graphs and route shard indexing. Replaces the old linear pathfinding with a graph-based approach that supports efficient multi-sector routing.

This subsystem is a core part of the 3dSpace game architecture, integrating with multiple other systems to provide seamless gameplay experiences. It follows the FW_ framework conventions and uses deterministic data pipelines for reproducible results.

• Deterministic generation: All system state derives from seeded data structures
  • Server-authoritative: Multiplayer operations use server as source of truth
  • Sectorized architecture: Galaxy data is divided into sectors for streaming efficiency
  • Runtime caching: Generated data persists between sessions to avoid regeneration

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: The landing and surface interaction system enables ships to land on procedurally generated planetary bodies (moons/planets) with proper collision handling, gravity, and surface normal resolution. It spans the full approach-to-landing pipeline: terrain contact detection via a sphere cast from the ship, dynamic collision patch generation on the planet side, landing zone validation against flatness/slope/ruggedness criteria, and surface landmark management for POIs like outposts and landing pads. The system bridges the FlightController (ship) with LandablePlanetRuntime (planet terrain), using a shared frame domain (CelestialFrame) to keep ship-local coordinates synchronized with the moving planetary body.

docs.title: Architecture Overview
docs.text:
┌───────────────────────┐         ┌──────────────────────────┐
│   SHIP                │         │   PLANET / MOON          │
│                       │         │                          │
│ FlightController      │  contact│ LandablePlanetRuntime    │
│ (terrain_contact_*)   │◀───────▶│ (collision patches, LOD) │
│                       │         │                          │
│ ShipTerrainContactSolver│       │ LandingZoneValidator     │
│ (sphere cast resolve) │         │ (flatness/slope check)   │
│                       │         │                          │
│ FrameDomainState      │  sync   │ CelestialFrame           │
│ (local position/vel)  │───────▶│ (body-local coordinate)  │
└───────────────────────┘         └──────────────────────────┘

docs.title: ShipTerrainContactSolverScript
docs.text:
A static utility class used by FlightController to resolve terrain contact via sphere casting. The ship carries a CollisionShape3D (sphere) at a local offset beneath its center of mass. Each physics tick, the solver:

  1. Transforms the sphere into world space using the ship's current basis
  2. Casts downward along gravity to find the nearest surface intersection
  3. Returns a result containing altitude, penetration depth, position/velocity corrections, and the detected planet reference
  4. The FlightController applies corrections via apply_correction() to prevent terrain clipping

Key parameters (exported on FlightController): - terrain_contact_activation_distance: 200m — distance below which contact detection activates - terrain_contact_enter_clearance: 5m — hysteresis margin for entering contact state - terrain_contact_exit_clearance: 10m — hysteresis margin for exiting contact state - terrain_contact_lookahead_sec: 0.2s — predictive look-ahead for early detection

docs.title: LandablePlanetCollisionPatch
docs.text:
A StaticBody3D placed near an anchor point on the planet surface, providing physics collision for a small region (~1 km default radius). It supports two shape types:

  • CONCAVE (ConcavePolygonShape3D): Full triangle mesh built from terrain samples. Accurate but synchronous and expensive (20–27 ms rebuild at Earth-scale radii).
  • HEIGHTMAP (HeightMapShape3D): Grid-based height map streamed across multiple physics frames (~4 rows per frame, ~80 ms total). Used for ship anchors to avoid CPU spikes.

The patch rebuilds when the anchor moves beyond 30% of its radius from the last build position. A MIN_REBUILD_INTERVAL_MSEC (160ms) cooldown prevents rapid re-triggering at high tangential speeds, eliminating dozens of redundant rebuilds per second on the server.

Key constants: - patch_radius: default 500m for avatar patches, larger for ship patches - resolution: default 16 (grid is 17x17 vertices) - collision_skin: small offset keeping collision slightly above visual terrain

docs.title: LandingZoneValidator
docs.text:
A static utility (RefCounted) that evaluates whether a candidate surface point is safe for landing. It samples terrain heights in a local grid and computes:

  • Flatness score: 1.0 minus normalized height variance
  • Max slope degrees: steepest adjacent-cell gradient, compared against MAX_SLOPE_DEGREES (55)
  • Height variance: population variance of sampled heights vs MAX_HEIGHT_VARIANCE (10m)
  • Terrain semantics: plains weight, ruggedness, landable score from the planet's sample_height_and_normal() method

A zone is suitable when both physical criteria (flatness > 0.05, slope < 55 deg, variance < 10m) and semantic criteria (landable_score > 0.02, ruggedness < 0.96) are met. An optional directional ring scan identifies the best nearby direction for landing when the center point is marginal.

docs.title: LandablePlanetRuntime
docs.text:
The core terrain runtime (Node3D) that builds a procedural planet from a quadtree of chunks. Each chunk is a subdivided cube face with LOD levels up to 16 splits. Key features:

  • Chunk pool: Recycled MeshInstance3D nodes for efficient creation/destruction
  • Incremental rebuild: Face-by-face initialization avoids frame stalls
  • Local collision patches: Activated when ships/avatars approach within local_patch_activation_distance (3000m). Per-anchor overrides control radius, resolution, shape type, and collision layer.
  • Flatten zones: Deform terrain at landing zones/outposts to create flat pads
  • Collision smoothing: CPU-side noise queries skip detail/micro layers so collision aligns with what the GPU mesh can display

docs.title: SurfaceLandmarkRuntime
docs.text:
Manages surface POIs (outposts, cities, landing zones) as Node3D instances. Each landmark:

  1. Registers in group nav/surface_landmark for scanning by SurfaceInteractService
  2. Resolves its surface anchor via the planet's resolve_surface_anchor() method
  3. Rejects if slope > 30 degrees or underwater
  4. For landing zones, registers a flatten zone to create a flat pad
  5. Loads a "near scene" (detailed mesh + collision) when the camera is close enough, subject to a per-frame budget of 1 near scene

docs.title: Domain State Transitions During Landing/Takeoff
docs.text:
The system uses FrameDomainState objects shared between ship and planet to maintain coordinate consistency:

  1. Approach: Ship's terrain contact solver detects proximity; collision patches activate on the planet side
  2. Contact: Sphere cast resolves surface intersection; position/velocity corrections applied; _terrain_contact_in_contact becomes true
  3. Landed: Frame domain pins ship local position to surface; hover pose clamped above terrain via resolve_station_keep_local()
  4. Takeoff: Contact state clears when altitude exceeds exit clearance; frame domain transitions back to flight mode

docs.title: Relationships
docs.text:
• Consumed by flight-controller for landing physics (resolve_terrain_contact(), _capture_pin_pose())
  • Integrated with player-possession-system for disembark onto planetary surfaces
  • Uses celestial-frame for body-local coordinate synchronization between ship and planet
  • Collision patches feed into flight-hitch diagnostics via static counters consumed by NetworkShipManager

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The multiplayer system uses an authoritative server model where the server simulates all player ships deterministically and broadcasts thin state snapshots to clients. Clients render remote ships as visual proxies with smooth interpolation between snapshots. The system handles world partitioning (peers are assigned to specific world instances), ship interest management, beam weapon replication, and projectile spawning via MultiplayerSpawner.

docs.title: Architecture Overview
docs.text:
┌─────────────────────┐         ┌──────────────────────┐
│   SERVER            │         │   CLIENT             │
│                     │         │                      │
│ NetworkShipManager  │────────▶│ RemoteShipController │
│ (authoritative sim) │ snapshot│ (visual proxy only)  │
│                     │         │                      │
│ MultiplayerSession  │ input   │ FlightController     │
│ (flight input recv) │◀────────│ (local ship control) │
└─────────────────────┘         └──────────────────────┘

docs.title: Server-Side: NetworkShipManager
docs.text: Located at /home/j/3d-space/scripts/network/network_ship_manager.gd (2,564 lines), this is the central multiplayer coordinator. It runs on both server and clients but has different responsibilities:

docs.title: Server Responsibilities
docs.text:
1. Authoritative ship simulation — Reads flight input from each peer, runs SharedFlightModel.simulate(), applies movement
  2. Snapshot broadcasting — Collects ship states into packed binary snapshots, broadcasts to all peers in the same world instance
  3. Beam weapon authority — Determines beam fire state and target positions authoritatively for consistent visual replication
  4. Projectile spawning — Uses MultiplayerSpawner to spawn projectiles with network ownership

docs.title: Client Responsibilities
docs.text:
1. Local ship control — Runs own FlightController with local physics (input → movement)
  2. Remote proxy rendering — Creates RemoteShipController instances for each peer, receives and interpolates snapshots
  3. Input submission — Sends flight input state to server via MultiplayerSession.set_flight_input_state()

docs.title: Key Constants
docs.text: Constant Value Purpose SNAPSHOT_INTERVAL 1/30s (33.3ms) Server snapshot broadcast rate SNAPSHOT_FORCE_INTERVAL 0.15s Force broadcast even if no change SHIP_INTEREST_NEAR_DISTANCE 6,000m Near distance for interest management SHIP_INTEREST_FAR_DISTANCE 8,000m Far distance — always replicate SHIP_SNAPSHOT_NEAR_DISTANCE 2,500m High-frequency snapshot range SHIP_SNAPSHOT_MEDIUM_DISTANCE 6,000m Medium-frequency snapshot range

docs.title: Snapshot Delivery Policy
docs.text:
Ships are replicated at different frequencies based on distance from the viewer:

Distance Range Tick Divisor Effective Rate < 2,500m (near) 1 (every frame) ~30 Hz 2,500–6,000m (medium) 2 ~15 Hz > 6,000m (far) 5 ~6 Hz

docs.title: Packed Snapshot Format
docs.text:
Snapshots are packed into a binary layout for efficient network transmission:

Meta stride (5 ints per ship): peer_id, time_ms, flags_bitfield, weapon_type, sequence

Data stride (23 floats per ship): pos.xyz, quat.xyzw, vel.xyz, throttle, travel_spinup, beam_origin_pos.xyz, beam_target_pos.xyz, shield_current, hull_hp, highway_active, highway_progress

docs.title: World Instance Partitioning
docs.text:
Peers are assigned to world instances via MultiplayerSession.get_assigned_world(). The server only replicates ships between peers in the same world instance: 

var loaded_peer_ids = MultiplayerSession.get_loaded_peer_ids_for_world(world_instance_id)

docs.title: Beam Weapon Replication
docs.text:
Beam weapons (lasers) require special handling because they need consistent visual representation across all clients. The server tracks beam state per peer: - fire — Whether the weapon is firing - beam_target_active — Whether a valid target exists - beam_origin_pos — Muzzle position in world space - beam_target_pos — Target position (or aim point if no direct lock)

A fallback mechanism ensures beams remain visible even if snapshot data is delayed.

docs.title: Client-Side: RemoteShipController
docs.text: Located at /home/j/3d-space/scripts/network/remote_ship_controller.gd (626 lines), this extends ShipVisualProxy and serves as a lightweight visual-only representation of remote player ships. It does NOT run physics or process input.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
func setup(peer_id: int, ship_data: ShipData, initial_spawn_context: String = "") -> void
Creates the proxy node, assigns it to groups (ship/remote, ship, nav/player_remote), and builds visual nodes including a jump flash effect for spawn/depart VFX.

docs.title: Snapshot Interpolation Pipeline
docs.text:
1. Receive — Server snapshots arrive via receive_snapshot(state: Dictionary) → pushed into SnapshotBuffer
  2. Sample — Each frame, _process() calls _snapshot_buffer.sample(server_time_ms, rtt_ms) to get interpolated state
  3. Apply — Position/rotation from interpolated state are applied directly to the proxy node

docs.title: Dynamic Interpolation Delay
docs.text: The buffer adapts its interpolation delay based on network conditions: - Uses RTT (round-trip time) from MultiplayerSession.get_latency_ms() - Calculates one-way floor as RTT × 0.5 - Target delay = max(expected_interval + jitter_budget, one_way_floor) - Clamped to [40ms, 160ms] range

docs.title: Extrapolation
docs.text:
When snapshots are delayed or lost, the buffer extrapolates using the last known velocity: 

result.position = newest.position + newest.velocity * dt
Extrapolation is capped at a dynamic budget (2× expected interval + jitter) to prevent visual drift. Three reasons are tracked: "boundary", "late_snapshot", and "extrapolation_cap".

docs.title: Weapon Visual State
docs.text: Remote ships display weapon visuals based on snapshot data: - _apply_weapon_visual_state(fire, beam_target_active, beam_origin_pos, beam_target_pos, weapon_type) - Beam origin/target positions are interpolated between snapshots for smooth beam rendering - Change detection prevents redundant VFX calls

docs.title: Spawn/Depart VFX
docs.text:
• Spawn: Plays context-specific visual effect (e.g., jump-in flash)
  • Depart: Shows jump flash, animates fade-out over 0.45s, then queues the node for freeing

docs.title: SnapshotBuffer
docs.text: Located at /home/j/3d-space/scripts/network/snapshot_buffer.gd (460 lines), this is a timestamped ring buffer that stores authoritative snapshots and provides smooth interpolation between them. It's used by both RemoteShipController and AI flight controllers in network replica mode.

docs.title: Data Structures
docs.text:
• Snapshot — Single entry: time_ms, position, rotation (Quaternion), velocity, throttle, boost, travel_mode, fire state, beam data
  • InterpolatedState — Result of sample(): interpolated values + is_extrapolating flag + reason string

docs.title: Ring Buffer Operations
docs.text:
• push() — Adds snapshot at write index, wraps around buffer size (default 32). Rejects out-of-order snapshots.
  • sample(render_time) — Finds bracketing pair in ring order, interpolates position/velocity with linear lerp and rotation with quaternion slerp

docs.title: Interpolation Details
docs.text: Field Method Notes Position Vector3.lerp() Linear interpolation Rotation Quaternion.slerp() No gimbal lock Velocity Vector3.lerp() Linear interpolation Throttle lerp() Float interpolation Boost/Travel/Fire Threshold at t ≥ 0.5 Boolean snap decision Beam positions Interpolated if both snapshots firing Otherwise snap to later snapshot

docs.title: Adaptive Extrapolation
docs.text: The buffer tracks recent snapshot intervals (rolling window of 8) and uses them to: 1. Calculate expected interval for jitter detection 2. Set dynamic extrapolation budget 3. Track consecutive missed snapshots for diagnostics

docs.title: Smoothing
docs.text:
Optional low-pass filter applied during extrapolation only: 

result.position = last_smoothed.lerp(result, smoothing_factor)
Factor range: 0.05–0.5 (default 0.1). Resets when no longer extrapolating.

docs.title: SharedFlightModel
docs.text: Located at /home/j/3d-space/scripts/network/shared_flight_model.gd, this provides the deterministic physics simulation that runs on both server and clients. It defines:

docs.title: FlightState
docs.text:
class FlightState extends RefCounted:
    var position: Vector3 = Vector3.ZERO
    var rotation: Quaternion = Quaternion.IDENTITY
    var velocity: Vector3 = Vector3.ZERO
    var throttle: float = 0.0
    var boost_active: bool = false
    var travel_mode_active: bool = false
    var travel_mode_spinup_progress: float = 0.0

docs.title: FlightInput
docs.text:
class FlightInput extends RefCounted:
    var forward: float = 0.0
    var strafe: float = 0.0
    var rotate_yaw: float = 0.0
    var rotate_pitch: float = 0.0
    var rotate_roll: float = 0.0
    var boost: bool = false
    var fire: bool = false
    var sequence: int = 0

docs.title: FlightParams
docs.text: Contains ship-specific parameters loaded from ShipData: base speed, rotation rates, bank angle limits, etc.

docs.title: Simulation Method
docs.text:
static func simulate(ship: FlightController, input: FlightInput, params: FlightParams, state: FlightState, delta: float) -> void
Applies input to the ship's physics state deterministically — same inputs always produce same outputs regardless of frame timing (within reason).

docs.title: ShipReplicationService
docs.text: An autoload singleton (/root/ShipReplicationService) that manages the lifecycle of ship replication: - Creates RemoteShipController instances when peers join - Removes them when peers leave or disconnect - Handles world assignment changes (peer moves to different world instance) - Coordinates with NetworkShipManager for spawn synchronization

docs.text: Total codebase footprint: ~2871 lines across 7 files

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Server-authoritative NPC replication system with client-side prediction and reconciliation. Handles NPC promotion/demotion, state synchronization across clients, and world runtime lifecycle management.

This subsystem is a core part of the 3dSpace game architecture, integrating with multiple other systems to provide seamless gameplay experiences. It follows the FW_ framework conventions and uses deterministic data pipelines for reproducible results.

  • Server-authoritative: Multiplayer operations use server as source of truth
  • Sectorized architecture: Galaxy data is divided into sectors for streaming efficiency
  • Runtime caching: Generated data persists between sessions to avoid regeneration
