docs.title: NPCs & Patrol Wings
docs.text: NPC ships populate the galaxy with life and danger. They exist in two modes — lightweight simulation when you're far away, and full combat AI when you get close enough to matter. This page covers how they're organized, what roles they play, and how their behavior changes based on your actions.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The NPC system creates a living galaxy through world-generation-seeded traffic that fills every system with ships going about their business. Each NPC is assigned a role from the faction's job mix, which determines what kind of ship it flies and what route it follows. The density of NPCs in any given system depends on its type — trade hubs buzz with activity while frontier systems are sparsely populated.

NPCs exist in three tiers: - Abstract — registered but not yet simulated (placeholder state) - Lightweight — position tracked, visible on radar/navball, no AI processing or weapons - Full Runtime — active combat AI with weapons, shields, and full ships-parts-lore integration

The transition between tiers is seamless: a trader drifting through space becomes a hostile pirate the moment you draw your weapon.

docs.title: Patrol Wings
docs.text: Patrol wings are organized groups of NPC ships that patrol together in formation. They're the primary way faction forces maintain presence in controlled systems.

docs.title: Wing Structure
docs.text:
Each wing has a unique ID, tracks its member actors, and maintains formation offsets for coordinated movement. The default formation is Vee — leader at origin, wingmen offset ±120m laterally and 65m behind. This matches the convoy escort pattern used throughout the game.

Wings are spawned by PatrolManager when a system becomes active. Each member gets: - A resolved faction ship visual (deterministic based on system seed) - A patrol route speed derived from its ship data's base speed and engine multiplier - Registration as a LIGHTWEIGHT tier actor with the PATROL role

docs.title: Wing Composition
docs.text: Wing size varies from 2 to 3 ships by default. The composition is faction-dependent — a Federation border system might spawn a tight Vee of patrol fighters, while Pirate space could have looser formations of raiders. The PATROL_WING_DIFFICULTY constant (set to 4) influences which ship variants are selected from the faction's loadout profiles.

docs.title: Wing States
docs.text:
Wings transition between states: - Patrol — normal cruising, lightweight simulation - Combat — triggered when any member is threatened, all members promote simultaneously - Fleeing — (reserved for future use)

When one wing member triggers a promotion event, the entire wing promotes together. This ensures you don't get picked off patrol ships one at a time — they fight as a unit.

docs.title: NPC Simulation Service
docs.text: NpcSimulationService is the persistent autoload singleton that manages all NPC state across the galaxy. It's the brain behind the simulation, tracking every actor's position, task, and tier regardless of whether it's currently visible in your viewport.

docs.title: How Simulation Works
docs.text: When you enter a system, register_active_system() creates an NpcSystemOrchestrator for that system. The orchestrator ticks at a configurable rate (default 0.25s) to advance all lightweight actors along their routes. This means NPCs continue moving even when you're not directly observing them — they're always somewhere, doing something.

docs.title: Route Generation
docs.text:
Each NPC gets a deterministic route based on its role and the system's anchors (stations, gates, belts). The seeded RNG ensures the same system always produces the same traffic patterns:

  • Traders follow station-to-belt routes, carrying cargo between economic nodes
  • Miners do round-trips between stations and asteroid belts, with mining dwell time at the belt
  • Patrols orbit gates and stations, maintaining presence at strategic points
  • Pirates lurk near highway lanes or drift in slow orbits near trade routes

Route speeds are role-specific: pirates move fastest (82 units/s), escorts follow (66), patrols (58), traders (62), miners slowest (42). Individual NPCs get ±15% variance from their base speed based on a hash of their system coordinates and index.

docs.title: Population Bounds
docs.text:
Each system type has minimum and maximum NPC counts that scale with density:

System Type Min Max Trade Hub 20 40 Industrial 16 35 Border 10 24 Mining 12 28 Pirate 12 20 Frontier 8 14

The minimum is always 8 actors regardless of system type, ensuring even the emptiest systems feel alive.

docs.title: Promotion & Demotion Lifecycle
docs.text: NPCs don't start in combat mode — they begin as lightweight simulation and promote when you get close enough to matter. This keeps performance stable while making encounters feel dynamic.

docs.title: Promotion Triggers
docs.text:
Four events can trigger promotion:

  1. Proximity (1500 units) — You fly within range of a hostile NPC that's actively engaged (intercepting, fleeing, or complying). Idle patrols won't promote just because you're nearby; they need to be in an active task state.
  2. Target Lock (3000 units) — You lock onto the NPC with your targeting system.
  3. Hail (4000 units) — You hail the NPC from within range.
  4. Weapon Aggression (2500 units) — You fire weapons near the NPC, or it takes damage.

When a patrol wing member is triggered, ALL wing members promote simultaneously. This prevents you from sniping isolated patrol ships while their formation watches helplessly.

docs.title: Demotion Cooldown
docs.text: After combat ends, NPCs don't immediately return to lightweight mode. A 30-second cooldown ensures they stay active long enough for cleanup (loot drops, debris, etc.). During this period, demotion is blocked if the NPC: - Is currently targeted by another player - Has been hailed recently - Was recently damaged - Has non-zero hostility mode - Is in an active task state (hailed, complying, fleeing, engaging, intercepting)

docs.title: Combat State
docs.text:
When promoted, NPCs get full combat capabilities: - Hull and shield health from their ships-parts-lore data - Weapons enabled with AI-controlled fire cadence - Point defense systems activated - Full AiFlightController behavior tree running

The damage model is standard: shields absorb damage first, then hull. If an NPC takes damage while still lightweight (before promotion), the damage is queued and applied immediately upon promotion. This ensures you can't "pre-damage" a patrol ship before it becomes active.

docs.title: AI Behaviors
docs.text: Promoted NPCs use AiFlightController with behavior trees defined in ai-behavior-trees. The controller supports five distinct states, each with its own tactical logic:

docs.title: Patrol State
docs.text: The default state for lightweight and non-hostile promoted NPCs. Ships follow their assigned route waypoints at a steady speed. No weapons, no targeting — just drifting through space on their scheduled route.

docs.title: Pursue State
docs.text:
When a target is acquired, the AI enters pursuit mode. It has two sub-modes: - Chase — Direct pursuit, closing to weapon range and holding position for firing solutions - Orbit Strafe — Circular maneuvering around the target at ~600m radius (roughly 50% of typical weapon range), maintaining constant bearing while keeping weapons trained

Pursuit uses predictive targeting with lead time calculation. The AI estimates where the target will be when its projectiles arrive and aims accordingly. Target scoring considers distance, angle behind target, threat level, and a small random factor for unpredictability.

docs.title: Evade State
docs.text: When taking heavy fire or below critical hull thresholds, NPCs enter evasion mode. They break off combat and flee in the opposite direction of their attacker. Evasion has a 3-second cooldown to prevent rapid state oscillation — an NPC won't immediately re-engage after fleeing.

docs.title: Flank State
docs.text: For more aggressive AI (high aggression personality), the flank behavior attempts to maneuver to the target's rear arc for a firing advantage. The AI arcs left or right around the target, maintaining distance while trying to get behind. This is the most tactically sophisticated behavior and appears on elite enemy variants.

docs.title: Retreat State
docs.text: When shields are depleted and hull is critically low, NPCs retreat rather than fight to the death. They disengage and flee toward their last known safe position (usually their patrol route's starting point).

docs.title: Combat Personality
docs.text:
Each NPC has personality traits configured through EnemyData profiles: - Aggression — How quickly the AI escalates from passive to hostile behavior - Risk Tolerance — Willingness to take damage for tactical advantage - Accuracy Variance — Random deviation in weapon aim (0.15 default, making NPCs miss sometimes)

These traits are faction-specific: Federation patrols are disciplined and methodical, Pirates are aggressive and reckless, Traders avoid conflict entirely.

docs.title: Ship Roles
docs.text: Each NPC is assigned one of six roles, which determines its behavior, route, and how it responds to player interaction. The role distribution varies by faction through the FACTION_JOB_MIXES configuration.

docs.title: Trader (Role 0)
docs.text:
The most common civilian ship in the galaxy. Traders follow station-to-belt routes carrying cargo between economic nodes. They have moderate compliance chance (50%) when hailed — they'll usually comply with demands but may flee if standing is poor. Their ships are typically medium-sized freighters with minimal weapons.

Route pattern: Station → Asteroid Belt → Station (round-trip with dwell time) Speed: 62 units/s base, reduced to 80% for haul tasks Cargo value hint: 55-100% of maximum

docs.title: Miner (Role 1)
docs.text:
Miners operate between stations and asteroid belts, spending time at the belt before returning. They have lower compliance chance (40%) than traders but higher than pirates. Miners are typically smaller ships optimized for cargo capacity over speed.

Route pattern: Station → Belt (30-90s dwell) → Station (5-15s unload dwell) Speed: 42 units/s base (slowest role) Cargo value hint: 30-75% of maximum

docs.title: Patrol (Role 2)
docs.text:
Faction security forces maintaining presence in controlled systems. Patrols are the most likely to engage hostile players and have the highest compliance chance when hailed by players with good standing. They're typically armed fighter or interceptor-class ships.

Route pattern: Gate → Station → Gate (continuous patrol loop) Speed: 58 units/s base, reduced to 90% for patrol routes Behavior: Will engage hostile players, may respond to piracy events in nearby systems

docs.title: Escort (Role 3)
docs.text:
Military escorts assigned to protect high-value convoys or installations. They have similar behavior to patrols but with higher aggression and better combat performance. Escorts are typically the most heavily armed ships in a faction's patrol roster.

Route pattern: Similar to patrols, often following trade lanes Speed: 66 units/s base (faster than regular patrols) Behavior: Aggressive engagement, will intervene when piracy is detected nearby

docs.title: Pirate (Role 4)
docs.text:
Hostile NPCs that lurk near highway lanes and gate approaches. They have the lowest compliance chance (10%) and highest aggression. Pirates are typically fast, lightly armored ships designed for hit-and-run tactics.

Route pattern: Lurk — slow orbit around a waypoint near trade routes Speed: 82 units/s base (fastest role), with intercept speed at 135% bonus Behavior: Will attack on sight, flee when heavily damaged, may trigger patrol response if they attack traders/miners

docs.title: Smuggler (Role 5)
docs.text:
Specialized civilian NPCs that operate in the gray areas between factions. They have high compliance chance (70%) — more stubborn than regular traders but less confrontational than pirates. Their cargo value hints are elevated (45-95%), making them attractive targets for piracy.

Route pattern: Similar to traders but with more erratic timing Speed: 78 units/s base (faster than traders, slower than pirates) Behavior: Will comply under pressure but tend to flee rather than fight

docs.title: Faction Job Mixes
docs.text:
Each faction has a different distribution of NPC roles, reflecting their economic and military priorities:

Faction Trader Miner Patrol Pirate Escort Collective 30% 30% 30% 5% 5% Federation 25% 20% 45% 0% 10% Pirates 10% 10% 10% 60% 10% Traders 60% 10% 10% 10% 10% Ygret 40% 20% 20% 10% 10%

The Federation has the highest patrol presence (45%) and zero pirates, reflecting their role as galactic peacekeepers. Pirates dominate their own space with 60% hostile NPCs. Traders are overwhelmingly civilian-focused at 60%.

docs.title: System Type Modifiers
docs.text:
System type can shift the job mix from the faction baseline:

  • Industrial systems get +15% miners, +10% traders
  • Trade hubs get +20% traders, -10% patrols (more commerce, less security)
  • Border systems get +20% patrols, +10% pirates (tension on the frontier)
  • Mining systems get +25% miners, -10% traders
  • Pirate systems get +30% pirates, -15% traders, -10% patrols

These modifiers are applied before normalizing weights, ensuring the total always sums to 1.0.

docs.title: Interaction System
docs.text:
When you hail an NPC, request_interaction() determines their response based on role, faction standing, and current task:

  • Already hailed/complying — Returns immediately (no double-hail)
  • Compliance check — Based on role base chance + player reputation modifier
  • Good standing (>50): +20% compliance
  • Poor standing (<-30): -20% compliance
  • If compliant — NPC drops cargo and enters comply state
  • If not compliant:
  • Traders/Miners flee and may trigger patrol response
  • Patrols/Escorts engage immediately
  • Pirates respond with NO_RESPONSE (they don't care about your hails)

Patrol intervention occurs when a pirate attacks near a patrol — if the patrol is within 5000 units, it will intercept the pirate. This creates emergent gameplay where piracy can trigger faction responses.

docs.title: Network Replication
docs.text:
In multiplayer, lightweight NPCs are replicated to all peers within radar range (25,000 units by default). Each peer receives position summaries for visible contacts but only the server runs full AI logic. Promoted NPCs get full network replication with combat state synchronization — hull, shields, behavior state, and weapon firing status are all synced between client and server.

The NpcRuntimeBridge class handles promotion/demotion transitions in a network-aware way, ensuring that promoted ships appear consistently across all connected clients regardless of who triggered the event.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: The player surface interaction system enables on-foot exploration and interaction with world objects while the player is landed on a planetary body. SurfaceInteractService scans for nearby interactable POIs (surface landmarks, cities, generic POIs) within a configurable distance, manages HUD prompts to prevent UI conflicts, and opens information popups when the player interacts. The system bridges PlayerAvatarController (the on-foot avatar) with world objects through group-based scanning and metadata-driven display names. It handles popup lifecycle management, radial menu integration, and integrates with individual instrument scripts for science activities like deploying gravity balls or thermal penetrators.

docs.title: Architecture Overview
docs.text:
┌───────────────────────┐         ┌──────────────────────────┐
│   PLAYER AVATAR       │         │   WORLD OBJECTS          │
│                       │         │                          │
│ PlayerAvatarController│  target │ Groups:                  │
│ (movement, state)     │◀────────│ nav/surface_landmark     │
│                       │         │ nav/surface_city         │
│ Signals:              │         │ nav/poi                  │
│ - interact_requested  │         │                          │
│ - radial_menu_request │         │ SurfaceLandmarkRuntime   │
│ - scan requests       │         │ (landmarks, cities)      │
└───────────┬───────────┘         └────────────┬─────────────┘
            │                                   │
            │  SurfaceInteractService           │
            │  (scanning, popup management)     │
            │                                   │
            │  ┌─────────────────────────────┐  │
            │  │ fps_interaction_popup.gd    │  │
            │  │ (entity info display)       │  │
            │  └─────────────────────────────┘  │
            │                                   │
            ▼                                   ▼
   FpsHudOverlay              SurfaceLandmarkRuntime
   (prompts, toasts)          (near scenes, markers)

docs.title: SurfaceInteractService — Proximity Scanner and Popup Manager
docs.text:
class_name SurfaceInteractService, extends Node. This is the central coordinator for surface interactions. It runs a periodic scan loop that finds the nearest interactable object within range and manages the interaction popup lifecycle.

Configuration: - interact_distance: 25.0m — maximum distance to detect interactables - scan_interval: 0.3s — time between proximity scans (throttled accumulator) - target_groups: [&"nav/surface_landmark", &"nav/surface_city", &"nav/poi"] — groups scanned for candidates

State: - _local_avatar: Reference to the current PlayerAvatarController - _nearest_target: The closest valid interactable node (or null) - _nearest_distance: Distance to nearest target - _popup: The interaction popup CanvasLayer instance (lazily created) - _popup_open: Whether a popup is currently displayed

Scan loop (_process(delta)): 1. Clears target if avatar is invalid 2. Skips scanning while radial science menu is active (is_radial_menu_active()) 3. Skips scanning while any other popup is open (checked via Game.is_any_popup_open()) 4. Accumulates delta time; when >= scan_interval, calls _scan_nearby() 5. If popup is open, returns early (keeps target set without rescanning) 6. On "fps_interact" key press with a valid nearest target, opens the interaction popup

Proximity scanning (_scan_nearby()): - Iterates all nodes in target_groups - Computes distance from avatar to each node's global position - Tracks the closest node within interact_distance - When the nearest target changes: sets _local_avatar.set_interact_target(nearest) which updates the HUD prompt; logs the change

Popup management: - _open_interaction_popup(): Lazily creates the popup via _ensure_popup() (adds to root), calls show_entity(_nearest_target), connects to dismissed signal, sets _popup_open = true - _on_popup_dismissed(): Sets _popup_open = false, clears target so ship re-enter can fire again - _on_radial_menu_opened(): Closes any open popup when the radial science menu opens (prevents conflicts)

docs.title: PlayerAvatarController — On-Foot Avatar State Machine
docs.text:
class_name PlayerAvatarController, extends CharacterBody3D. The on-foot avatar has two states: GROUNDED and AIRBORNE. It handles movement, gravity, slope following via Godot's built-in move_and_slide() with floor snap.

Movement parameters: - Walk speed: 6 m/s, Sprint: 10 m/s, Crouch: 3 m/s - Acceleration: 14 m/s^2, Deceleration: 18 m/s^2 - Air control: 0.4 (reduced horizontal control while airborne) - Jump velocity: 5 m/s, max jumps: 2 - Walkable slope: up to 55 degrees - Coyote time: 0.15s, jump buffer: 0.15s

Interact target: _interact_target is set by SurfaceInteractService.set_interact_target(). When non-null, the "fps_interact" action is consumed by the service instead of triggering ship re-enter. This prevents accidental re-entry while examining a landmark.

Radial menu state: _radial_menu_active is set when the radial science menu opens and cleared on dismiss or after an action is performed. While active, surface interaction scanning is paused to prevent UI conflicts.

docs.title: Science Action Integration
docs.text:
The avatar's perform_science_action(index) method maps radial menu selections to specific instruments:

Index Action Condition 0 Spawn gravity ball GROUNDED + valid spawn location 1 Spawn thermal penetrator GROUNDED + valid spawn location 2 Activate spectrometer Always (opens overlay) 3 Measure radiation Always (opens overlay) 4 Spawn gas sniffer GROUNDED + valid spawn location 5 Open planet science dossier Always (opens overlay) 6 Spawn seismic thumper GROUNDED + valid spawn location 7 Start lidar scan Always (opens overlay) 8 Spawn magnetometer drifter GROUNDED + valid spawn location

Each action either spawns a physical instrument object in the world (gravity ball, thermal penetrator, etc.) or opens an HUD overlay widget. The spawned instruments have their own scripts (GasSniffer, ThermalPenetrator, etc.) that handle scanning and emit results back to the avatar via signals.

docs.title: Gravity and Movement on Planetary Surfaces
docs.text:
The avatar attaches to a planetary surface via attach_to_surface_frame(), which sets up: - _attached_frame: A CelestialFrame providing body-local coordinate transforms - _surface_up: The local upward direction (planet radial) - _local_position: Position in the planet's body-local space

Movement uses Godot's built-in physics with floor snap (apply_floor_snap()) to follow terrain. The avatar's collision shape is a capsule configured via standing_height (1.8m) and capsule_radius (0.35m). Two separate collision layers are used: - COLLISION_LAYER_SHIP_TERRAIN (layer 17): For ship-only terrain patches; avatars do NOT collide with this - COLLISION_LAYER_AVATAR_TERRAIN (layer 18): For avatar-only fine walking collision

docs.title: HUD Prompt Management
docs.text: The FpsInteractPromptWidget displays "Press F — {display_name}" prompts. It receives the interact target from SurfaceInteractService.set_interact_target() and updates its text accordingly. The prompt is hidden when: - No target is in range (target cleared) - A popup is open (interaction consumed by popup) - The radial science menu is active

docs.title: Popup Lifecycle
docs.text:
The interaction popup (fps_interaction_popup.gd) follows this lifecycle:

  1. Open: SurfaceInteractService._open_interaction_popup() creates the popup if needed, calls show_entity(_nearest_target) to populate it with entity data from metadata
  2. Active: Target remains set on avatar (prevents ship re-enter); radial menu is blocked; other popups are ignored
  3. Dismiss: Popup emits dismissed signal; service clears target and sets _popup_open = false; next "fps_interact" press can trigger ship re-enter

docs.title: Relationships
docs.text:
• Consumes player-possession-system for avatar lifecycle (PlayerPossession.local_avatar_changed)
  • Integrates with fps-hud-instruments for prompt display and instrument overlays via FpsHudOverlay
  • Uses surface-features-generation landmarks as interactable targets (group nav/surface_landmark)
  • Coordinates with moon-landing-surface-interaction for surface frame attachment and gravity handling

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: 3dSpace is a Godot 4.6 multiplayer space simulation game built with GDScript. The codebase comprises ~402 scripts and ~132 scenes organized into a framework addon (FW_) plus game subsystems.

docs.title: Directory Structure
docs.text:
3d-space/
├── addons/fw/          # FW_ Framework addon (32 scripts)
│   ├── Audio/          # Audio service
│   ├── Bootstrap/      # Game bootstrap
│   ├── Config/         # Configuration persistence
│   ├── Input/          # Input handling & rebinding
│   ├── Net/            # Network service
│   ├── Platform/       # Platform abstraction
│   ├── Resources/      # Preload queue, resource cache
│   ├── Save/           # Save system
│   ├── Scenes/         # Scene router
│   ├── Signals/        # Framework event bus
│   ├── State/          # State machine
│   ├── UI/             # Context menus, modal overlays
│   └── Utils/          # Core utils, debug, shaders, tweens
├── scripts/            # Game scripts (402 total)
│   ├── ai/             # NPC AI: behavior trees, flight controllers, steering
│   ├── autoload/       # Global singletons: FM, PatrolM, NpcSimulationService
│   ├── backdrop/       # World backdrop: planets, nebulae, skybox, phenomena
│   ├── countermeasures/# Countermeasure systems
│   ├── debug/          # Debug tools and helpers
│   ├── flight/         # Player flight: view modes, VFX sync, debug
│   ├── level/          # Level generation: galaxy hierarchy, megastructures, POIs
│   ├── network/        # Multiplayer: replication, prediction, snapshots
│   ├── resources/      # Data assets: ships, factions, stations, presets
│   ├── systems/        # Cross-cutting game systems
│   ├── title/          # Title screen and main menu
│   ├── tools/          # Development utilities
│   ├── ui/             # HUD, menus, galaxy map, settings
│   ├── vfx/            # Visual effects: ship VFX, explosions
│   ├── weapons/        # Weapon systems: lasers, plasma, rockets, limpets
│   └── world/          # World management
├── scenes/             # Scene files (132 total)
├── materials/          # PBR materials
├── shaders/            # Custom GLSL shaders
└── resources/          # Godot resource files (.tres, .res)

docs.title: Framework Addon (FW_)
docs.text: All framework services use the FW_ prefix and are registered as autoloads. They provide: - Configuration persistence ([fw-config-service](../entities/fw-config-service.md)) - Scene management ([fw-scene-router](../entities/fw-scene-router.md)) - State machines ([fw-state-machine](../entities/fw-state-machine.md)) - Input handling, networking, save system, audio

docs.title: Autoload Singletons
docs.text: Three major autoloads drive the game world simulation: 1. FM (faction-manager) — Faction reputation and standing 2. PatrolM (patrol-manager) — NPC patrol wing management (class_name commented out) 3. NpcSimulationService (npc-simulation-service) — Full NPC lifecycle simulation

docs.title: Data-Driven Design
docs.text: Game content is defined via custom Resource classes (FactionData, ShipPart, StationData, etc.) stored in scripts/resources/. These are loaded at runtime and used by procedural generation systems.

docs.title: Procedural Level Generation
docs.text: The level system (procedural-level-builder) generates galaxy sectors with: - Star systems, planets, nebulae (backdrop subsystem) - Megastructures (dyson swarms, orbital tethers, etc.) - Faction territories and patrol assignments - POIs (gates, stations, mineral deposits)

docs.title: Multiplayer Architecture
docs.text: Network layer uses a custom replication system with: - Ship snapshot codec and delivery policies (scripts/network/) - Client prediction buffers - Event replication service for non-physics events - Shared flight model for deterministic physics

docs.title: Key Conventions (from AGENTS.md)
docs.text:
• Tab indentation — Godot default, not spaces
  • Type annotations required on all function parameters and return types
  • @onready for guaranteed node references
  • res:// paths for scene/resource references
  • class_name for global classes (though some autoloads are missing this)
  • Signal naming: past tense or descriptive (start_requested, transition_finished)

docs.title: Ship Systems Coordinator
docs.text: title: Ship Systems Coordinator created: 2026-05-17 updated: 2026-05-17 type: concept tags: [flight, resources] sources: [scripts/systems/ship_systems_coordinator.gd, scripts/systems/shield_manager.gd, scripts/systems/fuel_manager.gd] confidence: high

docs.title: Ship Systems Coordinator
docs.text: Total codebase footprint: ~1842 lines across 5 files

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Unified ship state management that coordinates all subsystems (weapons, shields, engines, fuel, cargo) into a single runtime state. Replaces scattered individual managers with a coordinated approach.

This subsystem is a core part of the 3dSpace game architecture, integrating with multiple other systems to provide seamless gameplay experiences. It follows the FW_ framework conventions and uses deterministic data pipelines for reproducible results.

docs.title: Key Design Decisions
docs.text:
• Deterministic generation: All system state derives from seeded data structures
  • Server-authoritative: Multiplayer operations use server as source of truth
  • Sectorized architecture: Galaxy data is divided into sectors for streaming efficiency
  • Runtime caching: Generated data persists between sessions to avoid regeneration

docs.title: Key Files (1842 lines total)
docs.text:
• scripts/systems/ship_systems_coordinator.gd (266 lines)
  • scripts/systems/shield_manager.gd (229 lines)
  • scripts/systems/fuel_manager.gd (224 lines)
  • scripts/systems/cargo_manager.gd (306 lines)
  • scripts/systems/targeting_manager.gd (817 lines)

docs.title: File Details
docs.text:
• ShipSystemsCoordinator: ensure_systems(parent: Node = null)
  • ShieldManager: _ready(), configure_from_part(part: ShipPart)
  • FuelManager: _ready()

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
The subsystem follows a layered architecture pattern:

  1. Data layer: Resource definitions (SystemRecipe, GalaxySectorSummary, etc.) define the static configuration
  2. Generation layer: Procedural builders transform data into runtime objects
  3. Runtime layer: World managers handle live simulation and client synchronization
  4. Replication layer: Network services broadcast state changes to connected clients

docs.text:
• ship-systems
  • combat-system
  • flight-controller

docs.title: Open Questions
docs.text:

  • [ ] Are there performance implications for large sector counts?
  • [ ] Should event replication use a more granular delivery policy?

docs.title: Ship Systems — Shields, Fuel, Cargo, Mining & Targeting
Overview
docs.text: Ship systems define the configurable attributes and runtime state of every ship in the game. The system is built around a data-driven architecture where ShipData resources hold all static configuration (stats, parts, weapons), while ShipRuntimeState tracks mutable values (current hull, shields, fuel). Parts are modular components that modify base stats when installed into slot-based loadouts.

docs.title: ShipData Resource
docs.text: Located at /home/j/3d-space/scripts/ship_data.gd (814 lines), ShipData is the central configuration resource for all ships. It extends Godot's Resource class and is assigned to every ship node at runtime via apply_ship_data().

docs.title: Core Stats
docs.text: Property Type Default Description mass float 1000.0 Base ship mass (affects inertia) base_speed float 40.0 Base movement speed boost_multiplier float 2.0 Speed multiplier during boost rotation_speed Vector3 (1.6, 1.4, 2.0) Yaw/Pitch/Roll rotation rates throttle_change_rate float 1.5 Throttle ramp speed damping float 2.5 Velocity damping factor brake_strength float 3.0 Deceleration rate

docs.title: Mass Composition
docs.text:
Total mass is the sum of all component masses: 

total_mass = base_mass + hull_mass + engine_mass + shield_mass

docs.title: Parts System
docs.text: Ship parts are modular components that modify ship stats when installed into designated slots. Each part extends ShipPart and is applied via typed methods on ShipData.

docs.title: Part Types & Slots
docs.text: Slot ID Type Constant Part Class Purpose "hull" — HullPart Hull integrity, mass "engine" — EnginePart Speed, boost, jump drive, travel drive "shield" SLOT_TYPE_INTERNAL (1) ShieldPart Shield capacity, recharge, visual properties "cargo" SLOT_TYPE_INTERNAL (1) CargoBayPart Cargo capacity "limpet_bay" — — Limpet/drone storage count "hardpoint_N" SLOT_TYPE_HARDPOINT (0) WeaponData Weapon hardpoints (N = 0–7) Countermeasure slots SLOT_TYPE_COUNTERMEASURE (3) CountermeasureData Countermeasure groups (default 4)

docs.title: EnginePart
docs.text:
Located at /home/j/3d-space/scripts/resources/engine_part.gd (28 lines), defines engine performance and jump drive capabilities.

Performance multipliers — Applied to base stats: - speed_multiplier — Final speed = base_speed × speed_multiplier × travel_speed_multiplier - boost_multiplier — Boost speed multiplier - throttle_multiplier — Throttle change rate modifier - rotation_multiplier — Rotation speed modifier

Boost system: - boost_value — Total boost time in seconds (default 5.0) - boost_recharge — Recharge rate in units/second (default 1.0)

Jump Drive: - has_jump_drive — Whether the engine includes a jump drive - jump_range_ly — Maximum jump distance in light-years (default 20.0) - jump_fuel_efficiency — Fuel consumed per light-year (lower is better, default 1.0) - jump_warmup_time — Preparation time before jumping (default 5.0s) - jump_cooldown_time — Cooldown between jumps (default 10.0s) - fuel_tank_capacity — Fuel tank capacity in units (default 100.0)

Travel Drive: - travel_speed_multiplier — Multiplier for travel mode speed (default 40.0, so final = base × 40) - travel_spinup_time — Time to reach full travel speed (default 2.0s) - travel_fuel_consumption — Fuel consumed per second in travel mode (default 0.25/s)

docs.title: ShieldPart
docs.text:
Located at /home/j/3d-space/scripts/resources/shield_part.gd (16 lines), defines shield properties.

Property Default Description capacity 120.0 Maximum shield hit points recharge_rate 18.0 Shield recovery rate per second recharge_delay 1.0 Delay before recharge begins after damage radius 3.5 Shield bubble radius in world units base_alpha 0.08 Visual opacity of shield bubble shield_color (0.2, 0.6, 1.0) Shield bubble color impact_strength 0.8 Visual impact intensity impact_radius 0.6 Impact spark radius impact_fade 1.2 Impact visual fade rate

docs.title: CargoBayPart
docs.text: Located at /home/j/3d-space/scripts/resources/cargo_bay_part.gd, defines cargo capacity for a ship's cargo bay module.

docs.title: Runtime State: ShipRuntimeState
docs.text: Located at /home/j/3d-space/scripts/resources/ship_runtime_state.gd (31 lines), this resource tracks mutable values that change during gameplay. It is initialized from ShipData and updated by the ship controller during simulation.

docs.title: Fields
docs.text: Field Type Description hull_current / hull_max float Current/max hull integrity shield_current / shield_max float Current/max shield capacity boost_level / boost_max float Current/max boost energy fuel_current / fuel_max float Current/max jump fuel cargo_amount int Current cargo units carried limpet_count int Available limpets/drones destroyed bool Whether the ship is destroyed

docs.title: Initialization
docs.text:
func initialize_from_ship_data(ship_data: ShipData) -> void
Sets all max values from ShipData and initializes current values to maximum. Called when a ship is spawned or loaded.

docs.title: Loadout System
docs.text: The loadout system manages equipment slots and installed parts. It's accessed via ShipData.get_or_build_loadout() which returns a ShipLoadoutData resource.

docs.title: Slot Management
docs.text:
func _set_loadout_slot(slot_id: String, part: ShipPart) -> void
Assigns a part to a named slot in the loadout. Called automatically when parts are applied via apply_hull_part(), apply_engine_part(), etc.

docs.title: Module Retrieval
docs.text:
func get_hull_module() -> HullPart
func get_engine_module() -> EnginePart
func get_shield_module() -> ShieldPart
Retrieves installed parts from the loadout by slot ID. Returns null if no part is installed or loadout is not seeded.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
func validate_loadout() -> Array[String]
Returns an array of validation issue strings. Called during ship initialization to catch configuration errors.

docs.title: Weapon Groups
docs.text: ShipData supports up to 8 weapon groups (WEAPON_GROUP_COUNT = 8), each containing one or more weapons with a fire policy.

docs.title: WeaponGroupData
docs.text: Each group has: - weapons: Array[WeaponData] — Weapons in this group - fire_policy: FirePolicy — How weapons fire (SIMULTANEOUS or SEQUENTIAL)

docs.title: Fire Policies
docs.text: Policy Behavior SIMULTANEOUS All weapons fire at once SEQUENTIAL Weapons fire one at a time, cycling through the group

docs.title: Weapon Selection
docs.text:
func set_active_weapon_group(index: int) -> void  # Switch to group N
func get_next_non_empty_group_index(current: int, direction: int) -> int  # Cycle forward/backward
func get_active_weapon_data() -> WeaponData  # Get current weapon

docs.title: Countermeasures
docs.text: Countermeasures are defensive items deployed from countermeasure slots. Each slot holds a CountermeasureData resource.

docs.title: Management
docs.text:
func get_countermeasure_groups() -> Array[CountermeasureData]
func get_countermeasure_group(index: int) -> CountermeasureData
func set_countermeasure_group(index: int, countermeasure: CountermeasureData) -> void

docs.title: Jump Drive System
docs.text: Jump drives enable fast travel between systems. The system is integrated into EnginePart and managed through ShipData.

docs.title: Key Methods
docs.text: Method Description has_jump_drive() Checks if engine has jump drive get_jump_fuel_capacity() Returns fuel tank capacity (from engine or default) get_jump_range() Returns max jump distance in light-years get_jump_fuel_efficiency() Returns fuel cost per light-year get_jump_warmup_time() Returns preparation time before jumping

docs.title: Fuel Consumption
docs.text:
Jump fuel is consumed based on: 

fuel_cost = distance_ly × jump_fuel_efficiency
If remaining fuel < fuel_cost, the jump cannot be initiated.

docs.title: Ship Stats Snapshot
docs.text: ShipStatsSnapshot (referenced via SHIP_STATS_SNAPSHOT_SCRIPT) provides a computed summary of all ship stats for UI display and AI decision-making: - Total mass breakdown (base, hull, engine, shield) - Speed calculations (base, boosted, travel mode max) - Rotation speeds per axis - Shield capacity and recharge rate - Weapon group summaries

docs.title: Key Files Reference
docs.text: File Purpose Lines scripts/ship_data.gd Central ship configuration resource — stats, parts, weapons, countermeasures 814 scripts/resources/ship_runtime_state.gd Runtime mutable state — hull, shields, boost, fuel, cargo 31 scripts/resources/engine_part.gd Engine performance + jump drive + travel drive config 28 scripts/resources/shield_part.gd Shield capacity, recharge, visual properties 16 scripts/resources/cargo_bay_part.gd Cargo bay capacity configuration — scripts/resources/hull_part.gd Hull integrity and mass configuration —

docs.title: Relationships
docs.text:
• Consumed by flight-controller for physics parameters (mass, speed, rotation rates)
  • Used by combat-system for shield/hull damage application and weapon range
  • Integrated with networking-multiplayer via ShipRuntimeState snapshot fields
  • Drives ui-subsystems HUD displays (shield bar, fuel gauge, cargo indicator)

docs.title: Ships & Parts — Hulls, Engines, Shields, Hardpoints & Loadouts
Overview
docs.text:
Ship customization in the game is built around a modular parts system. Every ship is defined by a ShipData resource that specifies its base performance characteristics (mass, speed, rotation), then enhanced through interchangeable modules installed into predefined slots. This system creates meaningful trade-offs: heavier hulls provide more HP but reduce maneuverability, powerful engines draw more power from the reactor, and shield upgrades consume both mass and electrical capacity.

The parts system supports two modes of ship definition: - Pre-configured ships (player vessels, NPC enemies) with all stats baked into ShipData exports - Dynamic loadouts using ShipLoadoutData with slot-based module installation and validation

Enemy ships use a separate EnemyLoadoutProfile system that randomly assembles ships from weighted pools filtered by difficulty level and faction affiliation.

docs.title: Part Categories
docs.text: Every part inherits from the base ShipPart class, which defines three universal properties: | Property | Type | Description | |----------|------|-------------| | mass | float | Weight added to ship total (affects acceleration and turning) | | power_draw | float | Electrical load when active (consumes reactor output) | | power_supply | float | Electrical generation contribution (negative draw = supply) |

docs.title: Hull Parts (HullPart)
docs.text:
Hull modules define a ship's structural integrity and damage resistance. They are installed in the dedicated hull slot and form the foundation of any loadout.

Property Default Description hull_hp 100.0 Hit points — determines how much damage the ship can absorb before destruction damage_resistance 0.0 Percentage reduction of incoming damage (0–1 scale)

Hull parts also contribute to total ship mass (hull_mass) and may include integrated cargo bays via the CargoBayPart subclass, which adds cargo_capacity slots for collected goods.

docs.title: Engine Parts (EnginePart)
docs.text: Engine modules are the most complex part type, governing both combat maneuverability and interstellar travel capabilities. They are installed in the engine slot and affect nearly every aspect of ship performance.

docs.title: Combat Performance
docs.text: Property Default Description speed_multiplier 1.0 Multiplier applied to base ship speed during normal flight boost_multiplier 1.0 Multiplier for emergency boost/sprint speed throttle_multiplier 1.0 How aggressively throttle changes translate to acceleration rotation_multiplier 1.0 Pitch, yaw, and roll rate multiplier thrust 0.0 Raw thrust force in Newtons (adds to base ship thrust)

docs.title: Boost System
docs.text: Engines include a boost mechanic for short bursts of enhanced speed: | Property | Default | Description | |----------|---------|-------------| | boost_value | 5.0 | Total boost duration in seconds before depletion | | boost_recharge | 1.0 | Recharge rate in units per second after boost ends |

docs.title: Jump Drive (Interstellar Travel)
docs.text: Engines with jump drives enable fast travel between star systems: | Property | Default | Description | |----------|---------|-------------| | has_jump_drive | false | Whether this engine includes a jump drive | | jump_range_ly | 20.0 | Maximum jump distance in light-years | | jump_fuel_efficiency | 1.0 | Fuel consumed per light-year (lower = better) | | jump_warmup_time | 5.0 | Seconds to prepare for a jump (vulnerable period) | | jump_cooldown_time | 10.0 | Cooldown before next jump is available | | fuel_tank_capacity | 100.0 | Total fuel capacity in units |

docs.title: Travel Drive (In-System Cruise)
docs.text: The travel drive enables high-speed movement within a star system: | Property | Default | Description | |----------|---------|-------------| | travel_speed_multiplier | 40.0 | Speed multiplier when travel mode is active (base × 40 = cruise speed) | | travel_spinup_time | 2.0 | Time to reach full travel speed from idle | | travel_fuel_consumption | 0.25 | Fuel consumed per second while in travel mode |

docs.title: Shield Parts (ShieldPart)
docs.text:
Shield generators create an energy barrier around the ship that absorbs damage before it reaches the hull. Shields recharge automatically after taking damage, but only when not under fire.

Property Default Description capacity 120.0 Maximum shield hit points — total damage absorption before depletion recharge_rate 18.0 Shield HP restored per second after taking no damage for recharge_delay seconds recharge_delay 1.0 Seconds of no damage taken before shields begin recharging radius 3.5 Visual radius of the shield bubble around the ship base_alpha 0.08 Opacity of the shield visual (very subtle by default) shield_color #3399FF Default blue color of the shield energy field impact_strength 0.8 Visual intensity multiplier when shields absorb damage impact_radius 0.6 Area around impact point affected by visual feedback impact_fade 1.2 How quickly impact visuals fade after absorption

Shields also contribute to ship mass (shield_mass) and power consumption (shield_power_draw). Some shield parts may provide negative power draw (power supply), effectively acting as reactor modules.

docs.title: Power Management
docs.text:
Power is managed through the power_draw and power_supply properties on all parts. The total ship power balance determines whether systems can operate at full capacity: - Positive net draw: Ship operates normally; excess draw may cause system failures - Negative net draw (supply): Ship generates surplus power, potentially enabling overclocking

Engine and shield parts are the primary consumers of electrical power, while some advanced modules can generate power for other systems.

docs.title: Hardpoints (WeaponData)
docs.text:
Hardpoint slots mount weapons on the ship's exterior. Weapons are defined by WeaponData resources and installed into hardpoint slots via the loadout system. The game supports up to 8 weapon groups, each containing one or more weapons that can be fired simultaneously or in sequence.

Property Default Description display_name "Laser" Weapon name shown in UI weapon_type "laser" Classification (laser, projectile, missile, etc.) damage 5.0 Damage per tick while beam is active ramp_time 1.5 Seconds to reach full power output from cold start weapon_range 1200.0 Maximum effective range in game units beam_color Red #FF3333 Color of the laser beam visual beam_width 0.05 Thickness of the beam heat_capacity 100.0 Total heat units before overheating occurs heat_rate 25.0 Heat generated per second while firing heat_recharge 20.0 Heat dissipation rate when not firing

Weapons also support advanced features: - Gimbaled mounting: Weapons can track targets independently of ship orientation (gimbaled, gimbal_turn_rate) - Heat management: Overheat mechanics with configurable thresholds and cooldowns - Charged shots: Hold fire to charge for increased damage at the cost of time and heat - Homing projectiles: Missiles and torpedoes with turn rate and lock-on cone

docs.title: Countermeasures (CountermeasureData)
docs.text:
Countermeasure systems provide defensive capabilities against incoming threats. They are installed in dedicated countermeasure slots and can operate automatically or manually.

Property Default Description countermeasure_type "point_defense" Classification (point defense, ECM, chaff, etc.) auto_trigger true Whether the system activates automatically when threats are detected trigger_range 300.0 Distance at which countermeasures activate cooldown 1.0 Seconds between activation cycles ammo_capacity 500 Total ammunition available ammo_per_shot 1 Ammunition consumed per activation

Point defense systems automatically target and destroy incoming projectiles within their trigger range. Other countermeasure types (ECM, chaff) provide area denial or sensor disruption effects.

docs.title: Utility Slots (ShipPart)
docs.text: Utility slots are flexible internal spaces that can accommodate any ShipPart-derived module. These slots typically house cargo bays, sensor arrays, or specialized equipment that doesn't fit into dedicated hardpoint or countermeasure slots.

docs.title: Player Ships (ShipData)
docs.text:
Player vessels are defined by comprehensive ShipData resources that specify every aspect of the ship's performance:

Category Properties Description Base Performance mass, base_speed, boost_multiplier, rotation_speed Inherent ship characteristics before part modifications Flight Dynamics throttle_change_rate, damping, brake_strength, auto_level How the ship responds to player input Hull & Defense hull_hp, hull_damage_resistance, shield_capacity, shield_recharge_rate Defensive capabilities Cargo & Logistics cargo_capacity, limpet_capacity, probe_count Collection and exploration capacity Weapons weapon_type, weapon_range, weapon_damage, weapon_group_data[] Offensive armament (up to 8 weapon groups) Countermeasures countermeasure_slot_count, countermeasure_groups[] Defensive systems Jump Drive jump_fuel_capacity, jump_fuel_efficiency, jump_warmup_time, jump_range Interstellar travel capabilities Visuals visual_scene, wrapper_scene_path, model_orientation_offset, model_scale 3D model configuration

docs.title: Enemy Ships (EnemyData)
docs.text: Enemy vessels are defined by EnemyData resources that specify both the ship's physical characteristics and its AI behavior profile. Enemies use EnemyLoadoutProfile to randomly assemble their equipment from weighted pools.

docs.title: Behavior Types
docs.text: Type Code Description Patrol "patrol" Guards a designated area, engages threats within detection radius Aggressive "aggressive" Actively pursues targets, prioritizes combat over positioning Passive "passive" Avoids conflict, retreats when threatened, only fires in self-defense

docs.title: Combat Parameters
docs.text: Property Default Description detection_radius 200.0 Distance at which the enemy detects player ships aggression 0.5 0–1 scale affecting fire rate, chase persistence, and targeting cone width risk_tolerance 0.5 0–1 scale affecting retreat threshold and strafe aggressiveness accuracy_variance 0.15 Aim jitter amount (lower = more accurate AI)

docs.title: Pursuit Behaviors
docs.text:
Enemies use different pursuit strategies based on their configuration: - Chase: Direct pursuit of the target at pursue_speed_multiplier × base speed - Orbit Strafe: Maintains orbital distance (orbit_radius) while circling the target

Both behaviors include turn slowdown when approaching targets from unfavorable angles (pursue_turn_slowdown_angle, pursue_turn_slowdown_factor) and lead-time prediction for accurate targeting (pursue_lead_time).

docs.title: Strafing Patterns
docs.text: Enemies can strafe using three modes: | Mode | Description | |------|-------------| | Burst | Short bursts of lateral movement followed by pauses (strafe_burst_duration, strafe_change_interval) | | Continuous | Constant lateral oscillation between min/max distances | | Orbit | Circular orbit around the target at fixed radius and speed |

docs.title: Retreat Logic
docs.text:
Enemies retreat when their hull drops below a threshold, modified by risk tolerance: 

retreat_threshold = retreat_hull_threshold × (1.0 - risk_tolerance)
They re-engage only when shields recharge above retreat_shield_recharge_threshold.

docs.title: Loadout System
docs.text: The loadout system manages which parts are installed in which slots, with validation to ensure compatibility between slot types and module types.

docs.title: Slot Types
docs.text: Type Code Accepts Description Hardpoint 0 WeaponData External weapon mounts (laser turrets, missile pods) Internal 1 Any ShipPart Hull, engine, shield, cargo bay modules Utility 2 Any resource Flexible internal space for any module type Countermeasure 3 CountermeasureData Defensive systems (point defense, ECM) Drone 4 Any resource Drone control and deployment systems

docs.title: Slot Definitions (ShipSlotDefinition)
docs.text:
Each slot has an ID, display name, type classification, and optional tags for filtering compatible modules: 

@export var slot_id: String = "hardpoint_0"
@export var display_name: String = "Forward Laser Mount"
@export var slot_type: SlotType = SlotType.HARDPOINT
@export var tags: PackedStringArray = ["forward", "laser"]

docs.title: Loadout Validation (ShipLoadoutData.validate_assignment)
docs.text:
The system validates that each installed module is compatible with its slot type: - Countermeasure slots only accept CountermeasureData - Hardpoint slots only accept WeaponData - Internal slots only accept ShipPart subclasses - Utility and drone slots accept any resource

Invalid loadouts are reported as validation issues, preventing ships from operating with incompatible configurations.

docs.title: Total Mass Calculation
docs.text:
total_mass = base_ship_mass + hull_mass + engine_mass + shield_mass
Mass affects: - Acceleration: Heavier ships accelerate more slowly (throttle_change_rate is effectively divided by mass) - Rotation: Heavier ships turn more slowly (rotation speed inversely proportional to mass) - Damping: Heavier ships have higher momentum and take longer to stop

docs.title: Power Balance
docs.text:
net_power = total_power_supply - total_power_draw
Power balance affects: - Shield operation: Insufficient power may cause shields to flicker or fail - Weapon firing: Overloaded reactors reduce weapon fire rate or prevent charging - Engine performance: Power-starved engines may not reach full thrust output

docs.title: Enemy Loadout Profiles (EnemyLoadoutProfile)
docs.text:
Enemy ships are assembled from weighted pools filtered by difficulty and faction: 

# Each pool contains EnemyLoadoutOption entries with weights
base_ships: [option1 (weight 50), option2 (weight 30)]
hull_parts: [optionA (weight 40), optionB (weight 60)]
engine_parts: [optionX (weight 70), optionY (weight 30)]
shield_parts: [optionP (weight 25), optionQ (weight 75)]
weapons: [weapon1 (weight 80), weapon2 (weight 20)]

Options are filtered by: - Difficulty range: min_difficulty to max_difficulty (1–10 scale) - Faction restrictions: allowed_faction_ids list (empty = available to all factions) - Weighted selection: Higher weight = more likely to be selected

Point defense systems are additionally gated by difficulty range and faction whitelist, controlled by point_defense_chance, point_defense_min_difficulty, point_defense_max_difficulty, and point_defense_allowed_faction_ids.

docs.title: Key Files Reference
docs.text: File Purpose Lines scripts/resources/ship_part.gd Base part class: mass, power_draw, power_supply, part_type enum (7 types) 10 scripts/resources/hull_part.gd Hull module: HP and damage resistance 9 scripts/resources/engine_part.gd Engine module: speed/boost/throttle/rotation multipliers, jump drive, travel drive 28 scripts/resources/shield_part.gd Shield generator: capacity, recharge, visual properties, impact effects 16 scripts/resources/cargo_bay_part.gd Cargo module: storage capacity 4 scripts/resources/countermeasure_data.gd Countermeasure system: auto-trigger, range, cooldown, ammo 12 scripts/resources/weapon_data.gd Weapon definition: damage, heat management, beam properties, homing 56 scripts/resources/ship_slot_definition.gd Slot type enum (HARDPOINT/INTERNAL/UTILITY/COUNTERMEASURE/DRONE) with tags 21 scripts/resources/ship_loadout_data.gd Loadout manager: slots + installed modules dictionary, validation logic 70 scripts/ship_data.gd Comprehensive ship definition: all parts, weapons (8 groups), countermeasures, jump drive, probes, visuals 814 scripts/resources/enemy_data.gd Enemy AI profile: behavior type, combat params, pursuit/strafe/retreat config 59 scripts/resources/enemy_loadout_profile.gd Weighted part pools with difficulty/faction filtering and point defense gating 66 scripts/resources/enemy_loadout_option.gd Individual loadout option: resource reference, weight, difficulty range, faction restrictions 24

docs.title: Relationships
docs.text:
• Consumed by ship-systems for runtime ship state management and part application
  • Drives combat-system through weapon damage, heat management, and countermeasure interactions
  • Provides visual identity via visual_scene and vfx_preset properties integrated with vfx-visual-effects
  • Enemy loadouts feed into npcs-patrol-wings for NPC ship spawning and fleet composition

docs.title: Sky Parameter System
docs.text: title: Sky Parameter System created: 2026-05-17 updated: 2026-05-17 type: concept tags: [vfx] sources: [scripts/systems/sky_params.gd, scripts/systems/sky_hero_object_profile.gd, scripts/systems/sky_parameter_mapper.gd] confidence: high

docs.title: Sky Parameter System
docs.text: Total codebase footprint: ~1479 lines across 5 files

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Dynamic sky rendering system that maps celestial bodies and phenomena to shader parameters. Handles hero objects (stars, planets), nebulae, and atmospheric effects with LOD-aware parameter updates.

This subsystem is a core part of the 3dSpace game architecture, integrating with multiple other systems to provide seamless gameplay experiences. It follows the FW_ framework conventions and uses deterministic data pipelines for reproducible results.

docs.title: Key Design Decisions
docs.text:
• Deterministic generation: All system state derives from seeded data structures
  • Server-authoritative: Multiplayer operations use server as source of truth
  • Sectorized architecture: Galaxy data is divided into sectors for streaming efficiency
  • Runtime caching: Generated data persists between sessions to avoid regeneration

docs.title: Stations & Megastructures
docs.text: Procedural station system and large-scale megastructures that populate every world-generation system.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Every generated system contains stations (player-accessible hubs) and megastructures (visual/lore landmarks). Both are defined in SystemRecipe data created by the procedural-level-builder pipeline, then instantiated at runtime with deterministic parameters derived from the galaxy seed.

Key files: - /scripts/resources/station_data.gd — StationData resource (345 lines) - /scripts/level/station_docking_manager.gd — Docking flow + service menu (1040 lines) - /scripts/ui/station_market_popup.gd — Buy/sell market UI (650 lines) - /scripts/resources/system_recipe.gd — MegastructureData definition (lines 392–402)

docs.title: StationType Enum (StationData.StationType)
docs.text: Type Display Name Default Population Docking Bays Default Services OUTPOST Outpost 500 2 Refuel, Restock, Information STATION Station 5,000 4 Trade, Missions, Refuel, Repair, Restock MEGASTATION Mega Station 50,000 12 All services + Crew MILITARY Military Base 3,000 6 Missions, Refuel, Repair, Restock (security: 0.9) RESEARCH Research Station — — Information, Missions MINING Mining Hub — — Trade, Refuel, Repair AGRICULTURAL Agricultural Station — — Trade, Refuel, Restock INDUSTRIAL Industrial Complex — — Trade, Refuel, Repair HABITAT Habitat — — Trade, Missions, Information PRISON Penal Colony — — Information only SCIENTIFIC Science Outpost — — Information, Missions

docs.title: StationSize Enum
docs.text:
• SMALL: < 1,000 inhabitants
  • MEDIUM: 1,000–10,000
  • LARGE: 10,000–100,000
  • MASSIVE: > 100,000

docs.title: ServiceType Enum
docs.text:
TRADE, MISSIONS, SHIPYARD, REFUEL, REPAIR, RESTOCK, CREW, INFORMATION, BLACK_MARKET

Each station type gets a default service set via StationData.get_default_services(). Services can be added/removed at runtime.

docs.title: Station Properties
docs.text:
• faction_id — controlling faction (default: "independent")
  • orbit_distance_au / orbital_inclination — orbital parameters
  • tech_level — 0–10 scale
  • security_level — 0.0–1.0 (affects market restrictions, black market availability)
  • has_market — commodity trading enabled
  • market_volatility — price fluctuation factor
  • market_allowed_categories / market_restricted_categories / market_preferred_categories / market_discouraged_categories — dynamic category controls

docs.title: Detection Flow (StationDockingManager)
docs.text:
1. Scan loop (every 0.25s): iterates all nodes in "nav/station" group, computes distance from player ship
  2. Prompt display: when nearest station ≤ docking_range (default 1200 units), shows on-screen prompt
  3. Player input: pressing flight_interact opens the docking menu

docs.title: Service Menu (ActionMenuOverlay)
docs.text:
The menu is built dynamically in _refresh_menu_for_station() and includes:

  • Refuel — restores jump fuel, cost = 4 cr/unit (from REFUEL_COST_PER_UNIT)
  • Repair — restores hull integrity, cost = 6 cr/unit (REPAIR_COST_PER_UNIT)
  • Restock — refills ammo + probes, cost = 2 cr/ammo-unit + 5 cr/probe (AMMO_COST_PER_UNIT, PROBE_COST_PER_UNIT)
  • Open Market — launches station-market-popup
  • Accept Convoy Contract — from SystemEventRuntime.get_merchant_convoy_offer()
  • Accept Pirate Raid — from SystemEventRuntime
  • Sell Discovery Data — sells bundled unsold discoveries via TradeService
  • All Services — applies all available services at once

docs.title: Reputation Gate
docs.text: Docking is denied if the player's standing with the station's faction is too low. The menu shows "Docking denied by {station} ({standing})".

docs.title: Station Market (StationMarketPopup)
docs.text: A 650-line popup for commodity trading, opened from the docking menu or via open_at() / open().

docs.title: Layout
docs.text:
• Sell list — player's cargo items available to sell (filtered by category)
  • Buy list — station's stock (filtered by category)
  • Category filters — dropdowns populated from TradeService.get_all_categories()
  • Quantity spinners — per-item buy/sell amounts
  • Quote labels — live price quotes with color coding
  • Transaction history — last 14 entries

docs.title: Badge System (color-coded item categories)
docs.text: Badge Color Meaning Allowed default white Accepted at this station Preferred green Better buy/sell rates Discouraged orange Weaker rates Restricted red Blocked (high security stations) Black Market orange Contraband accepted Demand Spike / Oversupply — Active market event

docs.title: Trade Flow
docs.text:
1. Player selects item → quantity spinner updates max value
  2. Quote computed via TradeService.quote_sell() or quote_buy()
  3. Buy/sell button triggers async request (request_sell / request_buy)
  4. Result tracked in _pending_request_ids, resolved on transaction_completed signal
  5. UI refreshes automatically

docs.title: Security Integration
docs.text: Market help text shows current station security level and whether black market is available: "Contraband blocked at high security" vs "Black Market allows contraband".

docs.title: Megastructures
docs.text:
All megastructures share a common interface pattern defined by SystemRecipe.MegastructureData:

class MegastructureData:
    var structure_id: String = ""
    var kind: int          # MegastructureKind enum value
    var shape_kind: int    # MegastructureShapeKind enum value
    var faction_id: String = ""
    var display_name: String = ""
    var anchor_entity_ids: PackedStringArray
    var control_points: PackedVector3Array
    var metadata: Dictionary  # kind-specific parameters
    var seed_value: int = 0

docs.title: MegastructureKind Enum (20+ types)
docs.text:
Core 13: HIGHWAY, PLANET_RING, RING_HABITAT, SOLAR_ARRAY, SHIPYARD, SENSOR_ARRAY, AQUEDUCT, HABITAT_RING, DEFENSE_PLATFORM, ORBITAL_TETHER, DYSON_SWARM, DYSON_SHELL_SEGMENT, SATELLITE_CONSTELLATION

New additions (post-wiki): - ARCLOGY_CLUSTER — interconnected arcology clusters (ArcologyClusterMegastructure) - FACTORY_COMPLEX — industrial manufacturing complexes (FactoryComplexMegastructure) - NANO_FAB_LAB — nanofabrication laboratories (NanofabLabMegastructure) - RECYCLING_PLANT — material recycling and processing plants (RecyclingPlantMegastructure) - REFINERY_STATION — resource refining stations (RefineryStationMegastructure) - WAREHOUSE_HUB — logistics and storage hubs (WarehouseHubMegastructure) - ORBITAL_CITY — large-scale orbital cities with surface integration (OrbitalCityMegastructure) - SPACE_COLONY_HUB — deep-space colony hubs with multi-ring design (SpaceColonyHubMegastructure) - PROPULSION_TEST_FACILITY — engine testing facilities (PropulsionTestFacilityMegastructure)

docs.title: MegastructureShapeKind Enum (8 shapes)
docs.text: POINT, PATH, RING, ARC, CLUSTER, BAND, SHELL_SEGMENT, AREA

docs.title: DefensePlatform (DefensePlatformMegastructure)
docs.text:
• Profiles: gate_bastion (radial turret arms), station_redoubt (dual-side fortifications)
  • Components: central keep, radial arms with turret pairs, perimeter lights
  • Parameters: cluster_radius, turret_count, bastion_height, platform_width
  • Visuals: hull shader (defense_platform_hull.gdshader) + accent shader with pulse animation

docs.title: HabitatRing (HabitatRingMegastructure)
docs.text:
• Shape: full ring or arc segments (72-segment procedural mesh)
  • Components: ring band, habitation modules (MultiMesh), beacon markers
  • Parameters: inner_radius, outer_radius, arc_fraction, start_angle_degrees, module_count, beacon_count
  • Visuals: habitat_ring_band.gdshader with window glow and stripe patterns

docs.title: Shipyard (ShipyardMegastructure)
docs.text:
• Profiles: drydock_spine (linear keel), side_arm_frame (lateral bays), circular_dock_frame (ring dock)
  • Components: main frame, guide light strips, dock bay markers
  • Parameters: frame_length, frame_width, frame_height, dock_bay_count, guide_light_count
  • Visuals: hull shader with panel grid + accent shader for lighting

docs.title: DysonSwarm (DysonSwarmMegastructure)
docs.text:
• Shape: orbital energy collection bands around a star
  • Components: multiple concentric bands, solar panels (QuadMesh), support structures
  • Parameters: band_radius, panel_width, panel_height, arc_fraction, band_count, band_spacing
  • Visuals: dyson_swarm_cluster.gdshader with energy pulse animation across panel grid

docs.title: SolarArray (SolarArrayMegastructure)
docs.text:
• Shape: collector panels on a central spine structure
  • Components: spine beam, panel arrays, hub connectors
  • Parameters: band_radius, panel_width, panel_height, arc_fraction, band_count, band_spacing
  • Visuals: solar_collector_panel.gdshader with scan-line effect

docs.title: Aqueduct (AqueductMegastructure) & OrbitalTether (OrbitalTetherMegastructure)
docs.text:
• Shape: path-based ribbon structures (connect two points via Curve3D)
  • Components: extruded mesh along curve, endpoint anchors, brace markers
  • Parameters: width, height, style ("tube", "industrial_pipe", "lattice")
  • Visuals: shared megastructure_strip.gdshader (aqueduct) or orbital_tether_strip.gdshader with pulse animation

docs.title: SensorArray (SensorArrayMegastructure)
docs.text:
• Styles: relay_dishes (parabolic dishes on masts), sensor_spires (tall antenna spires)
  • Components: central hub, element array (masts + dishes/spires), beacon ring
  • Parameters: cluster_radius, element_count, mast_height, dish_radius
  • Visuals: hull shader with panel grid + accent shader with pulse animation

docs.title: Condition System (All Megastructures)
docs.text:
Every megastructure supports four visual conditions that affect detail density and emission:

Condition Detail Scale Emission Multiplier pristine 1.0x base strength weathered 0.86–0.94x ~0.78–0.92x degraded 0.52–0.72x ~0.52–0.68x damaged 0.35–0.72x ~0.02–0.30x

Condition is stored in metadata and applied during _build_visuals() to scale element counts, reduce panel density, and dim emission effects.

docs.title: Visual Construction Pattern
docs.text: All megastructures follow the same construction pattern: 1. configure() — sets metadata from recipe data, adds to nav groups ("nav/megastructure" + type-specific group) 2. _clear_runtime_children() — frees previous children for regeneration 3. _build_visuals() — creates procedural geometry using BoxMesh, QuadMesh, ArrayMesh, and MultiMeshInstance3D 4. Custom shaders per-type with parameters: tint, emission_strength, stripe_density/speed/strength, metallic/roughness

docs.title: Nav Groups
docs.text: Each megastructure adds itself to "nav/megastructure" plus a type-specific group for targeting: - "nav/defense_platform", "nav/habitat_ring", "nav/shipyard", "nav/dyson_swarm", "nav/solar_array", "nav/aqueduct", "nav/orbital_tether", "nav/sensor_array"

docs.title: Integration with World Generation
docs.text: Megastructures and stations are generated by the procedural-level-builder pipeline: 1. SystemGenerator creates a SystemRecipe from GalaxySystemData.system_seed 2. Recipe includes arrays of StationData and MegastructureData entries 3. At runtime, ProceduralLevelBuilder instantiates each megastructure by calling its configure() method with the recipe data 4. Stations are placed at orbital distances from the star using deterministic positioning

  • combat-system — defense platforms as potential combat targets
  • economy-trade — commodity trading, convoy contracts, pirate raids

docs.title: Surface Features Generation
docs.text: title: Surface Features Generation created: 2026-05-17 updated: 2026-05-17 type: concept tags: [world-gen] sources: [scripts/backdrop/landable/surface_city_layout_generator.gd, scripts/backdrop/landable/surface_naming_service.gd, scripts/backdrop/landable/surface_landmark_runtime.gd] confidence: high

docs.title: Surface Features Generation
docs.text: Total codebase footprint: ~1905 lines across 6 files

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Procedural surface feature generation for landable planets including cities, landmarks, and outposts. Uses quadtree-based terrain chunking with named city layouts and landmark placement.

This subsystem is a core part of the 3dSpace game architecture, integrating with multiple other systems to provide seamless gameplay experiences. It follows the FW_ framework conventions and uses deterministic data pipelines for reproducible results.

docs.title: Key Design Decisions
docs.text:
• Deterministic generation: All system state derives from seeded data structures
  • Server-authoritative: Multiplayer operations use server as source of truth
  • Sectorized architecture: Galaxy data is divided into sectors for streaming efficiency
  • Runtime caching: Generated data persists between sessions to avoid regeneration
